# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section details the security architecture, network security group (NSG) rules, and encryption standards for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric deployment. The design ensures that all data flows, access patterns, and integrations with Microsoft cloud services are secured according to industry best practices and Microsoft’s latest recommendations.

## 7.1 Azure NSG Overview

Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) are critical components for controlling network traffic to and from Azure resources. NSGs allow granular control over inbound and outbound network communications by defining rules based on source, destination, port, and protocol. For Universiti Malaysia Sabah, Enfrasys Solutions recommends implementing NSGs to enforce a zero-trust network posture, ensuring only explicitly allowed traffic can traverse the environment.

The NSG rules for the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine are designed to facilitate secure communication with Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and essential Microsoft cloud endpoints. These rules are strictly limited to necessary services, minimizing the attack surface and ensuring compliance with Azure networking best practices. Enfrasys Solutions shall be responsible for the configuration and ongoing management of these NSG rules, ensuring that all changes are documented and validated against UMS’s security policies.

The following tables detail the required inbound and outbound NSG rules. Only network security rules are included in this section, in accordance with the design scope.

### Inbound NSG Rules

For the OPDG VM, inbound access is typically restricted to management and support scenarios. The default stance is to deny all inbound traffic unless explicitly required for operational needs, such as remote administration or monitoring from trusted networks.

| Name                  | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination           | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|----------------------|------------------|----------|--------|
| Deny_All_Inbound      | 4096     | Any                   | *            | Any                  | *                | Any      | Deny   |

### Outbound NSG Rules

Outbound rules are crafted to allow the OPDG VM to communicate securely with Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, authentication services, and to perform connectivity checks. Only essential endpoints and ports are permitted, enforcing least-privilege access for all egress traffic.

| Name                        | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                          | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                      | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |
| Deny_All_Outbound           | 4096     | Any                     | *            | Any                                                                                                                 | *                 | Any      | Deny   |

These rules ensure that only secure, encrypted communications are permitted between the OPDG VM and Microsoft cloud services, while all other traffic is denied by default. This approach aligns with the principle of least privilege and supports UMS’s compliance and audit requirements.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Encryption is a foundational element of the security architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s Microsoft Fabric deployment. Enfrasys Solutions recommends and implements encryption for data at rest and in transit across all relevant services, including Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG).

### Encryption at Rest

All data stored within Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and associated Azure services is encrypted at rest using Microsoft-managed keys, leveraging Azure Storage Service Encryption (SSE) and Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) where applicable. This ensures that any data persisted in storage accounts, databases, or OneLake repositories is protected from unauthorized access, even in the event of physical compromise of the underlying storage infrastructure.

For the OPDG VM, Azure Disk Encryption is enabled, utilizing BitLocker (for Windows) or DM-Crypt (for Linux) to secure the operating system and data disks. This measure guarantees that sensitive data processed or cached by the gateway remains encrypted on the VM.

### Encryption in Transit

Data in transit between UMS’s on-premises environment, the OPDG VM, and Microsoft cloud services is protected using industry-standard Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocols. All communications to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints occur over HTTPS (TCP port 443), ensuring confidentiality and integrity of the data as it traverses public and private networks.

Conditional Access policies, when available, further enhance in-transit security by enforcing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) and restricting access based on user and device context. Although UMS currently does not meet the licensing requirements for advanced Conditional Access policies, MFA is enforced at the tenant level, providing an additional layer of protection against unauthorized access.

The following diagram illustrates the secure access flow and enforcement of conditional access (where applicable) for users accessing Microsoft Fabric resources.

This diagram depicts the end-to-end authentication and access control process for users connecting to Microsoft Fabric. User authentication attempts are evaluated by Entra ID’s conditional access engine, which collects contextual signals, makes access decisions, and enforces policies such as MFA. Only users who meet the security requirements are granted access to Microsoft Fabric components, ensuring robust protection of UMS’s data assets.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 7.1: Secure authentication and conditional access flow for Microsoft Fabric.

Enfrasys Solutions will continue to monitor Microsoft’s security recommendations and update encryption practices as new features and compliance requirements emerge, ensuring Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s environment remains secure and resilient against evolving threats.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration strategy for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), detailing the phased approach, roles, and responsibilities to ensure a seamless transition to the new Microsoft Fabric-based analytics environment.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration / Setup

A successful migration begins with meticulous preparation, ensuring all foundational elements are in place prior to any data movement or system cutover. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a structured pre-migration phase that includes finalizing the solution design, confirming hardware and data sources, and establishing the core Fabric environment. This approach mitigates risks, aligns stakeholder expectations, and ensures that all technical prerequisites are satisfied before proceeding to pilot or production activities.

Key preparatory tasks include the formal review and sign-off of the design document, confirmation of the new server for the staging area database, setup of the Microsoft Fabric environment, and installation of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). These steps are critical to guarantee that both UMS and Enfrasys Solutions have a shared understanding of the migration scope, technical requirements, and operational dependencies.

## 8.2 Pilot

The pilot phase is designed to validate the migration approach, test technical integrations, and ensure that operational workflows function as intended in a controlled environment. Enfrasys Solutions recommends that the pilot deployment include a representative subset of data sources, user groups, and reporting scenarios to simulate real-world usage without impacting the broader UMS community.

During the pilot, Enfrasys Solutions will lead the configuration of the Microsoft Fabric workspace, deployment of the On-Premises Data Gateway, and initial data ingestion from the staging area database. UMS will provide access to required data sources, facilitate user acceptance testing, and coordinate feedback from pilot users. This collaborative approach allows for early detection of issues, refinement of migration scripts, and validation of security and compliance controls.

Upon successful completion of the pilot, a joint review session will be conducted to document lessons learned and finalize the production migration plan. This ensures that any technical or process gaps identified during the pilot are addressed before scaling to the full production environment.

## 8.3 Production Rollout

The production rollout is structured into distinct timeline phases to minimize disruption and ensure a smooth transition for all stakeholders. Enfrasys Solutions recommends the following phased approach, with explicit ownership assigned to each activity and a clear D-7 (pre-migration), D0 (cutover), and D+1 (post-migration) structure.

### D-7: Pre-Migration

In the week leading up to the production cutover, Enfrasys Solutions and UMS will jointly validate the readiness of all technical components, perform final data synchronization, and communicate the migration schedule to all affected users. This phase is critical for identifying any last-minute issues and ensuring that rollback procedures are in place.

| Item | Activities | Action By | Status |
|------|------------|-----------|--------|
| 1    | Final validation of Fabric environment and OPDG connectivity | Enfrasys | Pending |
| 2    | User communication and migration schedule announcement | UMS | Pending |
| 3    | Backup of existing data warehouse and staging area | UMS | Pending |
| 4    | Migration dry-run and cutover rehearsal | Enfrasys / UMS | Pending |

### D0: Cutover

On the day of migration, Enfrasys Solutions will execute the data migration scripts, monitor the ingestion process, and validate the integrity of migrated datasets. UMS will oversee user communications, coordinate with business units, and verify that critical reports and dashboards are functioning as expected in the new environment.

| Item | Activities | Action By | Status |
|------|------------|-----------|--------|
| 1    | Execute production data migration | Enfrasys | Pending |
| 2    | Real-time monitoring of migration process | Enfrasys | Pending |
| 3    | Functional validation of reports and dashboards | UMS | Pending |
| 4    | Issue escalation and resolution | Enfrasys / UMS | Pending |

### D+1: Post-Migration Support

Immediately following the cutover, both Enfrasys Solutions and UMS will engage in post-migration validation, user support, and performance monitoring. This ensures that any post-migration issues are promptly identified and resolved, and that the new environment is stable and fully operational.

| Item | Activities | Action By | Status |
|------|------------|-----------|--------|
| 1    | Post-migration data integrity checks | Enfrasys | Pending |
| 2    | User support and issue triage | UMS / Enfrasys | Pending |
| 3    | Performance and usage monitoring | Enfrasys | Pending |
| 4    | Final migration sign-off | UMS | Pending |

## 8.4 Post-Migration / Support

A robust post-migration support framework is essential to ensure long-term operational stability and user satisfaction. Enfrasys Solutions will provide hypercare support during the initial stabilization period, including proactive monitoring of the Microsoft Fabric environment, rapid incident response, and ongoing performance optimization.

UMS will assume responsibility for day-to-day operational support, user access management, and routine reporting tasks following the hypercare period. Enfrasys Solutions will facilitate a formal handover, including knowledge transfer sessions, documentation of operational procedures, and escalation protocols for advanced troubleshooting.

Continuous monitoring will be enabled to track data pipeline health, user activity, and system performance, ensuring that any anomalies are quickly detected and addressed. Regular review meetings between Enfrasys Solutions and UMS will be scheduled to assess system health, review support tickets, and plan for future enhancements or scaling requirements.

The diagram represents a technical architecture focused on user access management within an Azure environment. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This is likely a Resource Group within Azure, serving as a container for resources like virtual machines, databases, and more. It organizes and manages resources effectively.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component appears to be a service or application within the Fabric-RG, possibly related to user management or a specific application fabric that handles user interactions and data processing.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It likely involves policies that determine how and when users can access the UMS Fabric.

4. **External User**: Represents users outside the organization who need access to the UMS Fabric. Their access is managed through Conditional Access policies to ensure security and compliance.

**Data Flow and Connections**:
- The arrow indicates a flow of access or data from the External User to the UMS Fabric.
- Conditional Access acts as a gatekeeper, enforcing security policies before allowing the External User to interact with the UMS Fabric.
- The setup ensures that only authorized users can access the resources within the Fabric-RG, maintaining security and operational integrity.

The diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated against Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   - **Signal**: Various signals are collected, such as user and group information, and network location.
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access permissions.
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, determining whether the user can proceed.

3. **Inbound Access to Microsoft Fabric**: If access is granted, the user is allowed inbound access to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: The diagram shows integration with various Microsoft Fabric components, including:
   - Data services and analytics tools.
   - OneLake, which is likely a data storage or management service within Microsoft Fabric.

This architecture ensures secure and conditional access to Microsoft Fabric resources, leveraging Entra's capabilities to evaluate and enforce access policies based on user context and network conditions.

The diagram represents a data processing and analytics architecture within the UMS Fabric environment. It illustrates the flow of data from ingestion to visualization using various Azure components.

1. **Data Ingestion**: 
   - Data originates from the UMS Staging Area, specifically a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse.
   - Data Factory is used to ingest data into the system.

2. **Data Processing Layers**:
   - **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: The initial layer where raw data is stored after ingestion.
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Data is transformed and cleansed, moving from the Bronze to Silver layer.
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: Final layer where data is further refined and optimized for analysis.

3. **Data Transformation and Science**:
   - Data Factory is utilized for orchestrating data movement and transformation between layers.
   - Data Science processes are applied to the Silver layer, likely involving machine learning or advanced analytics.

4. **Identity Management**:
   - Identity_SM component is integrated within the Gold layer, possibly for managing user access or data security.

5. **Data Storage and Connectivity**:
   - OneLake is used for centralized data storage, ensuring seamless access and integration across the architecture.
   - Data from the Gold layer is connected via OneLake to Power BI for visualization and reporting.

6. **Visualization**:
   - Power BI is the tool used for creating interactive reports and dashboards, leveraging the processed data from the Gold layer.

This architecture supports efficient data processing, transformation, and visualization, enabling comprehensive analytics and insights.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components:

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse**:
   - This component represents the on-premises data warehouse where data is stored and managed using Microsoft SQL Server 2017. It serves as the staging area for data processing and analysis.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**:
   - The OPDG acts as a bridge between the on-premises data warehouse and external services. It facilitates secure data transfer without requiring inbound ports, ensuring data security and compliance. The gateway enables outbound secure connections to external systems.

3. **UMS Fabric**:
   - UMS Fabric is the external service or platform that interacts with the on-premises data warehouse through the OPDG. It receives data via the secure outbound connection established by the gateway.

**Data Flow**:
- Data originates from the UMS Staging Area within the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse.
- The On-Premise Data Gateway securely transmits data from the warehouse to the UMS Fabric.
- The connection is outbound and secure, eliminating the need for inbound ports, which enhances security by reducing exposure to external threats.

This architecture ensures secure and efficient data transfer between on-premises systems and external platforms, leveraging the capabilities of the On-Premise Data Gateway for seamless integration.

The diagram illustrates a data processing and machine learning workflow within an Azure environment, leveraging UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and integration.

1. **Data Source**: 
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, stored within OneLake, which is part of the UMS Fabric ecosystem.

2. **Machine Learning Processes**:
   - **AutoML**: 
     - **AutoML Experiment**: Data from XX_Silver_LK Tables is fed into an AutoML experiment. This process involves automated machine learning model training and selection.
     - **Result Scoring (Using Notebook)**: The results from the AutoML experiment are scored using a notebook, which likely involves evaluating model performance and metrics.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**:
     - **Modelling (Using Notebook)**: Alternatively, data can be processed through manual machine learning modeling using notebooks, allowing for custom model development and experimentation.

3. **Data Transformation and Storage**:
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The output from both AutoML and manual machine learning processes is stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables, indicating a refined and processed data state ready for further analysis or reporting.
   - **Identity_SM**: This component is associated with the XX_Gold_LK Tables, possibly serving as a data identity or management service to ensure data integrity and security.

4. **Data Visualization and Reporting**:
   - **Power BI**: The XX_Gold_LK Tables are connected to Power BI via OneLake, enabling visualization and reporting of the processed data for business intelligence purposes.

The workflow demonstrates a comprehensive approach to data processing, machine learning, and visualization within an Azure-based architecture, utilizing both automated and manual methods for model development and data refinement.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

# UMS Power BI & Fabric Design Review

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a concise overview of the project’s business objectives, scope, and the intended use and audience for this Solution Design Document.

## 2.1 Project Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) has embarked on a strategic initiative to modernize its data analytics capabilities by implementing Microsoft Power BI and Microsoft Fabric. The primary business objective is to enable data-driven decision-making across the university through a robust, scalable, and secure analytics platform. This project encompasses the design and implementation of a comprehensive data workflow that spans data ingestion, transformation, machine learning, and visualization. The scope includes the establishment of a dedicated Microsoft Fabric workspace, the development of end-to-end ETL pipelines using Fabric Data Factory, execution of advanced analytics with Fabric Synapse, and the integration of Power BI for business-aligned reporting and insights.

The high-level goals of this initiative are to:
- Streamline data integration and orchestration processes using Fabric Data Factory.
- Leverage Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, empowering UMS to handle complex data workloads efficiently.
- Facilitate machine learning applications to support predictive analytics and academic research.
- Deliver actionable business intelligence through Power BI, ensuring that stakeholders at all levels have timely access to relevant data.
- Align UMS’s cloud adoption strategy with Microsoft’s best practices for security, scalability, and operational excellence.

By adopting this architecture, UMS aims to enhance its analytical maturity, improve operational efficiency, and foster a culture of data-driven innovation across the institution.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

The purpose of this Solution Design Document is to provide a comprehensive technical blueprint for the implementation of Microsoft Power BI and Fabric at Universiti Malaysia Sabah. It serves as the authoritative reference for all design decisions, architectural patterns, and security controls adopted throughout the project. This document is intended to facilitate thorough review and formal sign-off by both UMS and Enfrasys Solutions, ensuring that all requirements are met and risks are addressed prior to implementation. Additionally, it will serve as an ongoing technical reference for UMS IT personnel, project sponsors, and support teams throughout the lifecycle of the solution.

## 2.3 Document Audience

This Solution Design Document is intended for the following audiences:
- UMS IT Department: Technical leads, system administrators, and support engineers responsible for managing and maintaining the Power BI and Fabric environment.
- UMS Business Stakeholders: Department heads, data analysts, and decision-makers who will leverage the analytics platform for operational and strategic insights.
- Enfrasys Solutions Architects and Consultants: The design, implementation, and support team responsible for delivering the solution in accordance with best practices.
- Project Sponsors and Executive Leadership: Senior management overseeing the project’s alignment with organizational goals and compliance requirements.

Responsibility for document review and formal sign-off is jointly assigned to Universiti Malaysia Sabah and Enfrasys Solutions, ensuring all technical, operational, and business requirements are validated and approved prior to project execution.

# 3.0 Microsoft Fabric and Power BI Overview

This section provides Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) with a comprehensive overview of the core Microsoft technologies forming the foundation of the proposed data platform. Enfrasys Solutions recommends this section as essential reading to ensure all stakeholders understand the technology stack, its capabilities, and its alignment with UMS’s business objectives.

## 3.1 Microsoft Fabric

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated, end-to-end analytics platform designed to unify data movement, data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence within a single SaaS environment. Fabric enables organizations to break down data silos by providing a cohesive set of experiences for data professionals, including data engineers, data scientists, and business analysts.

At the heart of Microsoft Fabric are several core services that collectively address the entire data lifecycle:

- **Data Factory**: This service provides robust data integration and orchestration capabilities. It allows for the ingestion of data from diverse sources, both on-premises and in the cloud, and supports the creation of complex ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines. Data Factory ensures that data is efficiently collected, cleansed, and made available for downstream analytics.
- **Synapse**: Fabric Synapse offers advanced analytics and big data processing capabilities. It enables large-scale data transformation, interactive data exploration, and supports both batch and real-time analytics workloads. Synapse empowers UMS to derive actionable insights from vast datasets through a familiar, unified workspace.
- **Data Science**: The Data Science experience in Fabric provides a collaborative environment for building, training, and deploying machine learning models. It supports the entire machine learning lifecycle, from data preparation to model operationalization, enabling UMS to leverage predictive analytics and AutoML workflows for advanced business scenarios.
- **OneLake**: OneLake is the unified data lake storage layer for Microsoft Fabric. It centralizes all organizational data in a single, secure, and scalable repository, facilitating seamless data access and collaboration across Fabric services.

By adopting Microsoft Fabric, UMS benefits from a modern, cloud-native data platform that accelerates time-to-insight, reduces operational complexity, and enhances data governance through integrated security and compliance features.

## 3.2 Power BI

Power BI is Microsoft’s industry-leading business intelligence and data visualization platform. It empowers users to connect to a wide range of data sources, transform raw data into interactive dashboards and reports, and share insights across the organization in a secure and governed manner.

A key advantage of Power BI is its deep integration with Microsoft Fabric. Through this integration, Power BI can natively connect to data stored and processed within Fabric’s OneLake, Data Factory, and Synapse environments. This seamless connectivity allows UMS to visualize data in near real-time, ensuring that decision-makers have access to the latest insights derived from the ETL and machine learning processes orchestrated within Fabric.

The combination of Power BI and Microsoft Fabric provides UMS with an end-to-end analytics solution—from data ingestion and transformation, through advanced analytics and machine learning, to intuitive, business-aligned visualizations. This integrated approach eliminates data silos, improves data quality, and streamlines the delivery of actionable intelligence to stakeholders across the university.

## 3.3 Microsoft Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is Microsoft’s cloud-based identity and access management service. It provides secure authentication, single sign-on (SSO), and role-based access control (RBAC) for users, applications, and resources within the Microsoft ecosystem.

Within the context of Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, Entra ID plays a critical role in safeguarding UMS’s data assets. It ensures that only authorized personnel can access sensitive data, analytics workspaces, and machine learning resources. Entra ID supports granular permission assignment, conditional access policies, and seamless integration with on-premises Active Directory, enabling UMS to enforce organizational security policies and regulatory compliance requirements.

Enfrasys Solutions assigns responsibility for the definition and configuration of Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Entra ID to its solution architects, ensuring that UMS’s technology stack is designed according to industry best practices and tailored to the university’s unique operational needs. UMS is responsible for providing directory information, user role definitions, and participating in access control reviews to ensure alignment with institutional policies.

# 4.0 Network Design and Decision

This section details the network architecture, connectivity, and gateway deployment for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s Microsoft Fabric implementation. The design emphasizes secure, efficient integration between on-premises resources and cloud services.

## 4.1 Network Connectivity Overview

Enfrasys Solutions recommends a hybrid network architecture that ensures secure, reliable connectivity between Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s on-premises data infrastructure and Microsoft Azure cloud services. This architecture is designed to protect sensitive university data while enabling seamless data movement and analytics within Microsoft Fabric.

The core principle of the proposed network design is the use of outbound-only connections from the on-premises environment to the cloud. By restricting all traffic to outbound connections, Universiti Malaysia Sabah eliminates the need to open inbound ports on its firewall, significantly reducing the attack surface and mitigating the risk of unauthorized access. This approach aligns with industry best practices for hybrid cloud security, ensuring that data transfers are initiated only from trusted, internal sources.

In this architecture, data originating from the on-premises Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse is securely transmitted to the Azure environment via the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). The OPDG acts as a secure bridge, facilitating communication between the university’s data warehouse and cloud-based Microsoft Fabric components. All data transfers leverage encrypted outbound connections, ensuring data confidentiality and integrity during transit. This design supports compliance with Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s internal security policies and regulatory requirements.

The network also incorporates Azure Conditional Access policies through Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory). These policies enforce granular access control, evaluating user and device context before granting access to Microsoft Fabric resources. By integrating conditional access, the university ensures that only authorized users—whether internal or external—can access sensitive data and analytics tools, further strengthening the overall security posture.

The following diagram illustrates the secure network flow, conditional access enforcement, and the interaction between on-premises infrastructure and Microsoft Fabric services.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 4.1: Conditional access flow for Microsoft Fabric authentication and data access.

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 4.2: Data flow from on-premises SQL Server through the On-Premises Data Gateway to Microsoft Fabric.

## 4.2 Microsoft Fabric Data Gateway

Microsoft Fabric On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a critical component for hybrid data integration. The OPDG enables secure data transfer between on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric services in the cloud. It acts as a bridge, allowing cloud services such as Power BI, Azure Data Factory, and Microsoft Fabric to access data residing within the university’s internal network without exposing those resources to the public internet.

Enfrasys Solutions will design the OPDG deployment to ensure optimal performance, high availability, and robust security. The gateway will be installed on a dedicated virtual machine within the university’s on-premises infrastructure. This VM will be configured to initiate only outbound connections to Azure, eliminating the need for inbound firewall rules and thereby reducing the risk of external threats.

Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for providing the necessary on-premises infrastructure, including the virtual machine and network configuration details. Enfrasys Solutions will supply deployment guidance, configuration best practices, and ongoing support to ensure a smooth integration process.

The following table outlines the recommended specifications for the virtual machine hosting the On-Premises Data Gateway. These specifications are based on Microsoft’s best practices to ensure reliable operation and support for anticipated data workloads.

| Component         | Specification                                   |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Operating System  | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework    | Version 4.8                                    |
| vCPU              | 8 cores                                        |
| RAM               | Minimum 8 GB                                   |

Enfrasys Solutions will be responsible for the design, deployment guidance, and configuration of the Data Gateway. Universiti Malaysia Sabah shall provision the virtual machine according to the specifications above and provide detailed network and infrastructure information to facilitate a secure and efficient deployment.

This approach ensures a robust, scalable, and secure foundation for hybrid data integration, supporting Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s analytics and reporting objectives within Microsoft Fabric.

# 5.0 Roles in Microsoft Fabric and Power BI

This section defines the roles, responsibilities, and access assignments for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) personnel within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environments. Proper role assignment is critical to ensure secure, efficient, and auditable data operations.

## 5.1 Microsoft Fabric Administrator Role

Microsoft Fabric is a unified analytics platform that integrates data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence across the Microsoft ecosystem. The Fabric Administrator, often referred to as the Data Engineer in organizational contexts, is responsible for the secure and effective management of Fabric resources, workspace governance, and data pipeline orchestration.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends that UMS assign Fabric Administrator privileges only to experienced personnel with a comprehensive understanding of data governance, security, and platform operations. The Fabric Administrator role carries significant responsibilities, including the creation and management of workspaces, assignment of workspace roles, configuration of data pipelines, and enforcement of security and compliance policies. Administrators are also responsible for monitoring platform usage, managing user access, and ensuring that data assets are protected according to UMS’s internal policies and regulatory requirements.

By centralizing these responsibilities, UMS ensures that data assets remain secure, operational risks are minimized, and platform changes are properly authorized and documented. Enfrasys Solutions will provide guidance and best practices for the ongoing management and review of administrator assignments.

## 5.2 Microsoft Fabric-native Roles

Microsoft Fabric and Power BI provide a set of native workspace roles to facilitate granular access control and operational separation. These roles are designed to align with common organizational responsibilities and data access needs. The primary native roles include Workspace Admin, Contributor, and Viewer.

Workspace Admins have full control over workspace settings, user assignments, and data assets within the workspace. Contributors are empowered to create, modify, and manage datasets, reports, and pipelines but do not have authority over workspace-level security or administrative settings. Viewers are granted read-only access, enabling them to consume published reports and dashboards without the ability to alter underlying data or resources.

This role-based access model enhances security by enforcing the principle of least privilege, ensuring that users are only granted permissions necessary for their job functions. It also streamlines operational workflows and supports clear audit trails for data access and modification. Enfrasys Solutions recommends that UMS periodically review role assignments to maintain alignment with evolving organizational structures and project requirements.

The table below summarizes the mapping between workspace roles, suggested responsibilities, and the designated UMS personnel-in-charge (PIC):

| No. | Workspace Role        | Suggested Role                          | UMS PIC                                                                                                                                         |
|-----|----------------------|-----------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Workspace Admin      | Fabric Admin / Data Engineer            | Dr Suraya Binti Alias, Sektor Analitik Data DARTIC, PPDM Official, Mohd. Zamrie Bin Musa, Noor Afiza Binti Momen, Ts AG Azihar @ AG KU Azihar Bin AG Tangah |
| 2   | Workspace Contributor| Data Engineer / Business User Admin     | Mohd Khairul Nizam Bin Senu (DB), Assie Zaee Petrus (DB), Muhaimin Bin Mad Noor (Infra), Nuraliah Binti Alias (Infra), Chong Tze Chien (Server Security), Abdul Latif Matali (Server Security) |
| 3   | Workspace Viewer     | Data Analyst / Stakeholder / Project Director | Director, Dr Farashazillah Binti Yahya, Stakeholders: Roslee Bin Talip, Dayangku Rozlina BTE PG. Aliuddin, Norhanieyati Binti Norhaizat, Zaidie @ Mohd Zaidie Bin Adilai, Professor Madya TS Dr Mohd Hanafi Bin Ahmad Hijazi |

## 5.3 UMS Workspace Role and Access

A robust access management strategy is critical to the security and operational effectiveness of UMS’s analytics environment. Enfrasys Solutions has mapped UMS personnel to recommended workspace roles based on their job functions and areas of responsibility within the organization. This mapping ensures that each user receives the minimum necessary permissions to perform their duties, reducing the risk of unauthorized access or accidental data modification.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends the following role assignments, which UMS shall review and confirm prior to implementation. Enfrasys Solutions will facilitate the initial configuration and provide documentation to support ongoing role management. UMS is responsible for maintaining the accuracy of personnel assignments and promptly updating access as organizational changes occur.

| No. | Workspace Role        | Suggested Role                          | UMS PIC                                                                                                                                         |
|-----|----------------------|-----------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|

# 6.0 Microsoft Fabric and Power BI Design and Decision

This section details the architecture, workflow, security, and governance design for Microsoft Fabric and Power BI as implemented for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The design leverages modern data engineering, analytics, and visualization paradigms, ensuring a robust, scalable, and secure data platform.

## 6.1 Data Workflow Design Considerations

A well-architected data workflow is essential for UMS to ensure data reliability, integrity, and accessibility from ingestion through to advanced analytics and visualization. The workflow must support various workload types, including data engineering, data science, and business intelligence, while maintaining clear separation of raw, cleansed, and transformed data. Enfrasys Solutions has carefully considered the end-to-end data journey, from the initial extraction of data from on-premises SQL Server, through multi-layered Lakehouse storage, to advanced analytics and Power BI visualization.

Key considerations include the use of Azure Data Factory for orchestrating data movement, the implementation of Bronze, Silver, and Gold Lakehouse layers for progressive data refinement, and the integration of both automated and manual machine learning workflows. The design also ensures that all modeled and scored data is centrally stored and made available for semantic modeling and business reporting. These considerations collectively support scalability, maintainability, and future extensibility for UMS’s analytics initiatives.

| ID   | Description                                                                                                          | Workload Type    |
|------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| DC01 | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                                 | Data Factory     |
| DC02 | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                                | Data Engineering |
| DC03 | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse.                                     | Data Engineering |
| DC04 | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse.                                    | Data Engineering |
| DC05 | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook, and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering |
| DC06 | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold).          | Data Factory     |
| DC07 | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse | Data Science     |
| DC08 | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.                         | Power BI         |
| DC09 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                              | Power BI         |

## 6.2 Data Workflow Design Decisions

Microsoft Fabric is a comprehensive analytics platform that unifies data engineering, data integration, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence under a single SaaS experience. Fabric’s Lakehouse architecture, coupled with Power BI, provides UMS with a scalable, high-performance environment for modern analytics workloads.

The selected architecture utilizes a multi-layered Lakehouse pattern (Bronze, Silver, Gold) to ensure data quality and traceability. Data is ingested from the on-premises SQL Server via Azure Data Factory and the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG), then processed through the Bronze (raw), Silver (cleansed), and Gold (transformed) layers. Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query tools are employed for transformation and enrichment. For machine learning, both AutoML and manual notebook-driven workflows are supported, with results stored in the Gold layer. Semantic models are built on top of the Gold Lakehouse and exposed via OneLake to Power BI for visualization.

This approach enables UMS to maintain a clear lineage of data, enforce governance standards, and facilitate both self-service and advanced analytics. The architecture supports future expansion, such as deeper integration with Azure ML or advanced governance tools, without disrupting existing workflows.

The following diagram illustrates the end-to-end data workflow, highlighting the movement and transformation of data across the platform.

The diagram below presents the technical architecture for UMS’s data processing and analytics workflow. Data originates from the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, is securely transferred via the On-Premises Data Gateway, and moves through the Bronze, Silver, and Gold Lakehouse layers in Microsoft Fabric. Machine learning processes (both AutoML and manual) are integrated, and final outputs are exposed to Power BI for business reporting.

|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Adopt a multi-layered Lakehouse architecture (Bronze, Silver, Gold) for data storage and transformation.         | Ensures data quality, traceability, and supports advanced analytics.                          |
| DD02 | Use Azure Data Factory and OPDG for secure, automated data ingestion from on-premises SQL Server.                | Enables seamless, scheduled data movement with minimal manual intervention.                   |
| DD03 | Employ Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query for data transformation and enrichment.                        | Provides flexibility for both low-code and code-first data engineering tasks.                 |
| DD04 | Integrate AutoML and manual ML workflows using Fabric Notebooks.                                                 | Supports both rapid prototyping (AutoML) and custom model development (manual).               |
| DD05 | Centralize all modeled and scored data in the Gold Lakehouse for consistency.                                    | Simplifies downstream data modeling and reporting.                                            |
| DD06 | Build semantic models on top of Gold Lakehouse and expose via OneLake to Power BI.                               | Delivers high-performance, governed analytics to business users.                              |
| DD07 | Utilize Power BI Desktop for advanced data visualization and reporting.                                           | Empowers self-service BI and interactive dashboards for stakeholders.                         |

## 6.3 Access Design Considerations and Decisions

Securing access to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI is critical to protecting UMS’s sensitive data assets. The platform leverages Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) for identity and access management, ensuring that only authenticated and authorized users can interact with data resources. While Conditional Access policies provide granular control over user access based on risk signals and context, current licensing constraints at UMS preclude their immediate implementation. Instead, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is enforced at the tenant level to enhance security posture.

### 6.3.1 Conditional Access

Conditional Access is a feature of Microsoft Entra ID that evaluates user sign-in attempts based on signals such as user identity, device compliance, location, and risk. It can enforce policies such as requiring MFA or blocking access under certain conditions. For UMS, Conditional Access is not currently available due to the absence of the necessary Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses. Enfrasys Solutions recommends revisiting this approach in the future as licensing and security needs evolve.

The diagram below illustrates how Conditional Access would function within the Microsoft Fabric environment, evaluating user authentication attempts and enforcing access decisions.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 6.3: Conditional Access authentication and enforcement workflow.

| No.  | Conditional Access                                                                                                              |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage.  |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy.                                                                              |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts.                                          |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment.                                                                            |

### 6.3.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

Given the ineligibility for Conditional Access, MFA is enforced at the tenant level for all UMS users. MFA significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access by requiring users to provide a second form of verification in addition to their password. This approach ensures that only legitimate users can access sensitive analytics and data assets, even if credentials are compromised. Enfrasys Solutions strongly recommends maintaining tenant-wide MFA enforcement as a baseline security measure.

### 6.3.3 Microsoft Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID is the cloud-based identity and access management service that underpins authentication for Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and other Azure resources. It centralizes user management, supports MFA, and—when appropriately licensed—enables Conditional Access for advanced security controls. All user provisioning, access assignments, and authentication flows for UMS’s analytics platform are managed through Entra ID, ensuring a unified security perimeter.

# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section defines the security architecture and supporting controls for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric and Power BI deployment, with a particular focus on network security, encryption, and compliance responsibilities.

## 7.1 Azure NSG Overview

A Network Security Group (NSG) in Microsoft Azure is a fundamental security component that enables organizations to control inbound and outbound traffic to Azure resources at the subnet or network interface level. NSGs use rules to permit or deny traffic based on factors such as source/destination IP address, port, and protocol, thereby reducing the attack surface and enforcing least-privilege access.

For the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine, Enfrasys Solutions has designed a set of outbound NSG rules to ensure secure communication with Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and essential Microsoft cloud services. These rules are crafted to enable only the minimum necessary connectivity, using secure protocols and restricting destination endpoints to trusted Microsoft domains. This approach not only supports the operational needs of the OPDG but also aligns with Azure networking best practices and the principle of defense-in-depth.

By implementing these NSG rules, UMS benefits from enhanced security posture, reduced risk of unauthorized access, and improved visibility over network flows. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for defining and implementing these security controls, while UMS is tasked with reviewing them to ensure alignment with institutional compliance and governance requirements.

The following table details the specific NSG outbound rules for the OPDG VM:

| Name                         | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                                      | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|------------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services    | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication    | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test | 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                                  | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules collectively ensure that only secure, necessary traffic is permitted outbound from the OPDG VM, thereby supporting both operational requirements and security best practices.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Encryption is a critical pillar of the overall security architecture for any cloud-based analytics and data platform. Microsoft Fabric and Power BI leverage robust encryption mechanisms to protect data both in transit and at rest, ensuring confidentiality and integrity throughout the data lifecycle.

Encryption in transit is achieved through the use of industry-standard protocols such as HTTPS and SSL/TLS. All communications between the OPDG VM, Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and supporting Microsoft cloud services are secured using HTTPS over TCP port 443. This ensures that sensitive data, authentication credentials, and configuration information are protected from interception or tampering during transmission over public and private networks. The NSG rules defined above further enforce this by allowing only encrypted traffic to the necessary Microsoft endpoints.

Encryption at rest is provided by Microsoft Fabric’s OneLake storage platform, which utilizes Azure Storage Service Encryption (SSE) with Microsoft-managed keys by default. All data stored within OneLake—including datasets, reports, and dataflows—is automatically encrypted using strong cryptographic algorithms (e.g., AES-256). This ensures that data remains protected even if storage media are compromised. Additionally, Microsoft Fabric supports integration with Azure Key Vault for customer-managed keys, offering UMS the flexibility to enhance control over encryption key management if required by compliance policies.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for designing and implementing the necessary encryption controls, including the configuration of HTTPS/SSL for all data flows and validation of at-rest encryption settings within Microsoft Fabric and OneLake. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing these controls to ensure they meet the university’s data protection, privacy, and regulatory compliance requirements. This collaborative approach ensures that both operational security and institutional governance objectives are met.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration strategy for the Microsoft Fabric solution, tailored for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The approach is structured to ensure a seamless transition from planning through post-migration support, with clear task ownership between Enfrasys Solutions and UMS.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration/Setup

A successful deployment begins with meticulous preparation. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a structured pre-migration phase to minimize risk and ensure that all dependencies are addressed before any system changes occur. This phase includes the delivery and sign-off of the final design document, confirmation of source systems, environment setup, and the installation of critical data gateway components.

The delivery of the design document is essential to align all stakeholders and secure formal approval from UMS. Confirmation of the new server and data warehouse copy (staging area database) ensures that the data to be ingested into Microsoft Fabric is accurate and accessible. The Fabric environment setup, led by Enfrasys Solutions, establishes the foundational cloud infrastructure. Finally, the installation of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a collaborative task, requiring both Enfrasys Solutions for technical implementation and UMS for access provisioning and validation.

The following table details the preparatory actions, responsible parties, and current status:

| No. | Activities                                                         | Planned Start    | Action By         | Status       | Remarks |
|-----|--------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|-------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1   | Deliver Design Document to be reviewed by UMS & Sign Off           | Fri, 7-Nov-25    | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |
| 2   | Confirmation on the new server (data warehouse copy – Staging Area DB) to be ingested | Mon, 10-Nov-25   | UMS               | Not-Started  |         |
| 3   | Fabric Environment Setup                                            | Wed, 12-Nov-25   | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |         |
| 4   | Install On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in VM to establish Data Gateway | Mon, 17-Nov-25   | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |

## 8.2 Pilot

The pilot phase is a critical step to validate the technical design, data flows, and operational readiness of the Microsoft Fabric environment before full-scale production rollout. Enfrasys Solutions will lead the deployment of a controlled pilot, ingesting a subset of data from the staging area database into the Fabric environment. This pilot will test the connectivity established through the On-Premises Data Gateway, validate data transformation processes, and confirm that security controls are functioning as intended.

During the pilot, UMS is responsible for providing access to the necessary data sources and promptly addressing any data quality or connectivity issues identified. Enfrasys Solutions will monitor system performance, document any issues, and coordinate remediation steps. Upon successful completion of the pilot, both parties will jointly review outcomes and sign off on readiness to proceed to production.

## 8.3 Production

The production rollout is executed in a phased manner to minimize disruption and ensure a smooth transition. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a structured timeline, with key milestones as follows:

- **D-7 (One week before cutover):** Final validation of pilot outcomes, user communication, and scheduling of migration activities. UMS is responsible for notifying all relevant stakeholders and ensuring that no changes are made to source systems during the migration window.
- **D0 (Cutover Day):** Enfrasys Solutions will execute the migration of all agreed data sets into the Microsoft Fabric production environment. System integration, data validation, and initial user acceptance testing are performed in close coordination with UMS.
- **D+1 (Post-migration):** Immediate post-migration support is provided by Enfrasys Solutions to address any issues, finalize user acceptance, and ensure business continuity. UMS validates data integrity and system accessibility.

This phased approach reduces risk, provides clear communication checkpoints, and ensures that both technical and business requirements are met before the solution is fully operational.

## 8.4 Post-Migration/Support

After the production cutover, Enfrasys Solutions provides dedicated post-migration support to address any residual issues and facilitate knowledge transfer to UMS personnel. This includes system monitoring, troubleshooting, and performance tuning to ensure optimal operation of the Microsoft Fabric environment.

A formal handover process is conducted, during which Enfrasys Solutions delivers all relevant documentation, operational runbooks, and conducts training sessions for UMS administrators and users. UMS is responsible for validating that all operational requirements are met and for assuming ownership of day-to-day management tasks upon completion of the support period.

This structured approach ensures a smooth transition, minimizes operational risk, and empowers UMS to maximize the value of their Microsoft Fabric investment.

### Others

Power BI Security and Compliance: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/enterprise/service-security](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/enterprise/service-security)

The diagram illustrates a technical architecture involving Azure components and user access management. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This represents a Resource Group in Azure, which is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It is depicted with an icon suggesting a fabric or mesh, indicating a structured environment for resource management.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely refers to a User Management System within the Azure environment, possibly utilizing Azure Fabric for scalable and reliable service deployment. It is part of the Fabric-RG, suggesting it is a resource managed within this group.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature in Azure Active Directory that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It is represented by a shield icon, indicating its role in protecting resources by enforcing policies.

4. **External User**: This component represents users outside the organization who need access to resources within the Azure environment. The diagram shows a connection from the UMS Fabric to the External User, indicating that user access is managed and facilitated through the UMS Fabric, with Conditional Access policies applied to ensure secure and compliant access.

The flow suggests that external users interact with the UMS Fabric, which is governed by Conditional Access policies to ensure secure access to resources within the Fabric-RG.

The diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated against Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   
   - **Signal**: Various signals are gathered, such as user and group information, and network location. These signals help determine the context of the authentication attempt.
   
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding the access request. This decision considers the risk level and compliance requirements.
   
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, which could involve granting access, requiring additional authentication, or denying access.

3. **Inbound to Microsoft Fabric**: Once the conditional access policies are applied, the request is processed inbound to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: The diagram shows integration with various Microsoft Fabric components, including OneLake, which is likely a data lake service within the ecosystem. Other components depicted include tools for analytics, data processing, and visualization.

This architecture ensures secure and compliant access to Microsoft Fabric resources by leveraging conditional access policies to evaluate and enforce authentication requests.

The diagram represents a technical architecture for data processing and analytics using Azure services. 

1. **UMS Staging Area**: Data originates from a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, labeled as the UMS Staging Area. This is the initial data source.

2. **Data Factory**: The data is ingested into the system using Azure Data Factory, which orchestrates the data movement and transformation processes.

3. **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: The ingested data is first stored in the Bronze layer, which typically contains raw, unprocessed data.

4. **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Data is then transformed and moved to the Silver layer, where it is cleaned and enriched.

5. **Data Science**: The Silver layer data is processed using Data Science tools, likely involving machine learning or advanced analytics to derive insights.

6. **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The processed data is stored in the Gold layer, which contains refined, business-ready data.

7. **Identity_SM**: This component likely handles identity management or security measures, ensuring data integrity and access control.

8. **OneLake**: The architecture utilizes OneLake for unified data storage, facilitating seamless data access and management.

9. **Power BI**: The final output is connected to Power BI for visualization and reporting, enabling stakeholders to interact with the data insights.

The entire process is encapsulated within the UMS Fabric, indicating a cohesive data management framework.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components: a SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, an On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG), and UMS Fabric.

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse**: This component represents the data warehouse where data is stored and managed. It serves as the staging area for data processing and analytics.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**: This gateway facilitates secure data transfer between the on-premise data warehouse and cloud services. It enables outbound secure connections without requiring inbound ports, ensuring data security and compliance.

3. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely represents a cloud-based service or platform that interacts with the on-premise data warehouse through the OPDG. It receives data from the warehouse for further processing, analytics, or integration into other services.

The data flow begins at the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, where data is sent to the OPDG. The OPDG then securely transmits the data to the UMS Fabric using outbound connections, ensuring no inbound ports are needed, thus enhancing security.

The diagram illustrates a data processing and machine learning workflow within an Azure environment, leveraging UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and integration.

1. **Data Source**: 
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, containing pre-processed data ready for machine learning experiments.

2. **Machine Learning Processes**:
   - **AutoML**: 
     - **AutoML Experiment**: Data from XX_Silver_LK Tables is fed into an automated machine learning experiment, which automatically selects the best model and parameters.
     - **Result Scoring (Using Notebook)**: The results from the AutoML experiment are scored using a notebook, providing insights and metrics on model performance.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**:
     - **Modelling (Using Notebook)**: Alternatively, data can be processed through manual machine learning modeling using notebooks, allowing for custom model development and experimentation.

3. **Data Storage and Integration**:
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The output from both AutoML and Manual Machine Learning processes is stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables, representing refined and validated data.
   - **Identity_SM**: This component likely handles identity management or metadata services, ensuring data integrity and security.

4. **Data Visualization and Reporting**:
   - **Power BI**: The XX_Gold_LK Tables are connected to Power BI via OneLake, enabling advanced data visualization and reporting capabilities for end-users.

5. **Infrastructure**:
   - **UMS Fabric**: Provides the overarching framework for data management and orchestration.
   - **OneLake**: Facilitates seamless data integration and connectivity between components, ensuring efficient data flow and accessibility.

The diagram effectively outlines the flow of data from initial processing to advanced analytics and visualization, highlighting the integration of automated and manual machine learning techniques within a robust Azure-based architecture.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.
