# UMS Power BI & Fabric Design Review

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a high-level overview of the project, the intent of this Solution Design Document, and its intended audience, with a focus on Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s strategic adoption of Microsoft Fabric and Power BI.

## 2.1 Project Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) is undertaking a transformative initiative to modernize its data analytics capabilities by adopting Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. The primary business objective is to establish a robust, scalable, and secure data platform that enables end-to-end data workflows—from ingestion and transformation to advanced analytics and visualization. This project encompasses the design and implementation of a Microsoft Fabric workspace tailored to UMS’s operational and business requirements.

The project scope includes the development of comprehensive Extract, Transform, Load (ETL) pipelines, the integration of machine learning processes, and the deployment of business-aligned analytics dashboards using Power BI. By leveraging Microsoft Fabric’s core services—such as Fabric Data Factory for data integration and orchestration, Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, and integrated Data Science capabilities for machine learning—UMS aims to enhance decision-making, operational efficiency, and academic research outcomes.

The high-level goals of this initiative are to align UMS’s cloud adoption strategy with the latest advancements in Microsoft Fabric, ensure seamless data integration across diverse sources, enable advanced analytics, and provide intuitive, role-based data visualizations for stakeholders across the university.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

The Solution Design Document serves as the authoritative reference for the architecture, technical decisions, and implementation approach for UMS’s Microsoft Fabric and Power BI deployment. Its primary intent is to provide a comprehensive blueprint that guides all stakeholders through the review, approval, and execution phases of the project.

This document details the proposed infrastructure, workflow designs, security considerations, and operational processes. It is structured to facilitate informed decision-making, ensure alignment with UMS’s business objectives, and support compliance with best practices and regulatory requirements. The Solution Design Document is to be used for formal review and sign-off by both Universiti Malaysia Sabah and Enfrasys Solutions, establishing a mutual understanding and agreement on the technical direction prior to implementation.

Enfrasys Solutions and Universiti Malaysia Sabah share joint responsibility for the review and sign-off of this document. Enfrasys Solutions is accountable for the accuracy and completeness of the technical design, while UMS is responsible for validating alignment with institutional requirements and strategic objectives.

## 2.3 Document Audience

This Solution Design Document is intended for a diverse audience comprising both technical and business stakeholders. The primary readers include:

- UMS IT leadership and technical teams responsible for data platform management, integration, and security.
- UMS business stakeholders and academic leaders who will leverage analytics insights for strategic and operational decision-making.
- The Enfrasys Solutions project team, including architects, engineers, and consultants tasked with delivering the solution.

By addressing the needs and perspectives of these groups, the document ensures that all parties are equipped with the necessary information to support successful project delivery, risk mitigation, and ongoing platform governance. Both UMS and Enfrasys Solutions are responsible for reviewing and endorsing the content, thereby ensuring collective ownership of the solution’s design and implementation.

# 3.0 Microsoft Fabric & Power BI Overview

This section provides a comprehensive overview of the core Microsoft technologies forming the foundation of Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) data platform solution: Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Microsoft Entra ID. Enfrasys Solutions delivers these technology overviews to ensure a clear understanding of each component’s purpose, capabilities, and integration within the overall architecture. UMS is responsible for reviewing and validating these descriptions for alignment with institutional standards and requirements.

## 3.1 Microsoft Fabric

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated, end-to-end analytics platform designed to unify data movement, engineering, science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence within a single SaaS environment. Microsoft Fabric streamlines the process of ingesting, transforming, storing, and analyzing data by providing a suite of tightly integrated services, each tailored for a specific aspect of the data lifecycle. The core services within Microsoft Fabric include:

- **Data Factory**: A robust data integration and orchestration service, Data Factory enables the ingestion of data from diverse sources, including on-premises databases and cloud repositories. It supports the creation of data pipelines for seamless extraction, transformation, and loading (ETL) processes, ensuring data is reliably moved and prepared for downstream analytics.
- **Synapse**: Synapse delivers advanced data analytics and big data processing capabilities. It provides a unified analytics workspace where data engineers and analysts can perform large-scale data exploration, transformation, and aggregation using both serverless and provisioned resources.
- **Data Science**: The Data Science component empowers users to develop, train, and operationalize machine learning models directly within the Fabric environment. It supports both automated machine learning (AutoML) and custom notebook-driven experimentation, facilitating advanced analytics and predictive modeling.
- **OneLake**: OneLake is a unified, enterprise-grade data lake that serves as the central storage layer for all data assets within Fabric. It provides a single point of access for structured and unstructured data, supporting efficient data sharing, governance, and management across the organization.

By leveraging Microsoft Fabric, UMS benefits from a cohesive analytics ecosystem that eliminates data silos, simplifies operational complexity, and accelerates time-to-insight. The platform’s native integration between services ensures that data flows seamlessly from ingestion to transformation, machine learning, and visualization, all governed by a consistent security and compliance framework.

## 3.2 Power BI

Power BI is Microsoft’s enterprise business intelligence and data visualization platform, designed to enable organizations to transform raw data into actionable insights through interactive dashboards and reports. Power BI integrates natively with Microsoft Fabric, allowing users to connect directly to curated datasets, semantic models, and dataflows stored in OneLake or processed through Fabric’s ETL and data science pipelines.

Within the UMS solution, Power BI serves as the primary interface for business users, analysts, and decision-makers to explore, analyze, and present data. The integration with Fabric ensures that Power BI has real-time access to the latest, business-ready data residing in the Gold Lakehouse layer, as well as advanced analytics outputs generated by machine learning models. This direct connectivity streamlines the process of building and sharing insights, ensuring that all stakeholders operate from a single source of truth.

The synergy between Power BI and Fabric delivers several key benefits to UMS:
- **Seamless Data Access**: Power BI can connect to Fabric’s semantic models via Direct Lake (OneLake), eliminating the need for complex data exports or duplications.
- **Advanced Analytics Integration**: Outputs from machine learning experiments and data science workflows are readily available for visualization and operationalization.
- **Enterprise Security**: Power BI leverages the same access controls and governance policies enforced by Fabric and Entra ID, ensuring secure and compliant analytics.

## 3.3 Microsoft Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is Microsoft’s cloud-based identity and access management service, providing secure authentication, authorization, and identity governance for users, devices, and applications. Entra ID is foundational to the security architecture of the UMS data platform, underpinning access management for all Fabric and Power BI resources.

In the context of this solution, Entra ID serves several critical roles:
- **Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO)**: Users authenticate to Fabric and Power BI using their institutional credentials, ensuring a seamless and secure experience.
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**: Entra ID enables granular assignment of permissions, ensuring that only authorized users can access sensitive data, manage workspaces, or perform administrative tasks.
- **Conditional Access and Compliance**: Entra ID supports advanced security policies, such as multi-factor authentication (MFA) and conditional access, to enforce compliance and protect against unauthorized access.

By standardizing on Entra ID, UMS ensures that all users and administrators interact with the data platform within a secure, governed, and auditable environment. This approach reduces the risk of data breaches, simplifies user lifecycle management, and aligns with best practices for cloud security.

## 3.4 Technology Summary Table

The following table summarizes the core technologies, their primary purposes, and their roles within the UMS solution. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for providing these technology overviews, while Universiti Malaysia Sabah is tasked with reviewing and confirming their applicability.

| Technology         | Purpose                                                        | Role in UMS Solution                                         |
|--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| Microsoft Fabric   | Unified analytics platform for data integration, engineering, science, and BI | Foundation for end-to-end data workflows, ETL, ML, and analytics |
| Data Factory       | Data ingestion and orchestration                               | Ingests and prepares data from SQL Server to Fabric Lakehouses |
| Synapse            | Advanced analytics and big data processing                     | Enables large-scale data transformation and aggregation        |
| Data Science       | Machine learning development and operationalization            | Facilitates AutoML and custom ML workflows                    |
| OneLake            | Centralized enterprise data lake                              | Stores all raw, refined, and business-ready data              |
| Power BI           | Business intelligence and data visualization                   | Delivers dashboards, reports, and insights to business users  |
| Microsoft Entra ID | Identity and access management                                | Secures access to Fabric and Power BI resources               |

Enfrasys Solutions will continue to provide guidance and best practices for the adoption and integration of these technologies. Universiti Malaysia Sabah shall review the technology stack to ensure alignment with institutional requirements and strategic objectives.

## 3.5 Data Workflow Architecture Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the end-to-end data workflow architecture as designed for Universiti Malaysia Sabah. It depicts the flow of data from initial ingestion in the UMS Staging Area, through transformation and machine learning processes in Fabric, to final visualization in Power BI. This architecture leverages the integrated capabilities of Data Factory, Synapse, Data Science, OneLake, and Power BI, all governed by Entra ID for secure access.

![](images\slide_009_img_003.png)

Figure 1: High-level data workflow architecture from ingestion to analytics and visualization.

## 3.6 Machine Learning Workflow Diagram

The following diagram demonstrates the machine learning workflow within Microsoft Fabric, highlighting the use of AutoML and manual notebook-driven processes. Data flows from the Silver Lakehouse into machine learning experiments, with results scored and stored in the Gold Lakehouse. Power BI then connects to these curated outputs for business reporting and analytics.

![](images\slide_021_img_005.png)

Figure 2: Machine learning workflow leveraging AutoML, notebooks, and integration with Power BI.

# 4.0 Network Design and Decision

This section details the network architecture and connectivity decisions for the Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) Microsoft Fabric deployment, focusing on secure, reliable integration between on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric in the cloud.

## 4.1 Network Connectivity Overview

A robust network topology is essential to ensure seamless data flow and secure communication between UMS’s on-premises environment and Microsoft Fabric in Azure. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a hybrid architecture that leverages outbound-only connectivity for all cloud integrations, thereby reducing the attack surface and aligning with best practices for cloud security.

The overall topology consists of UMS’s on-premises data center, where critical data assets such as the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse reside, and the Azure cloud environment, where Microsoft Fabric services are provisioned. Connectivity between these environments is facilitated by the Microsoft On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG), which acts as a secure bridge for data transfer. The OPDG is deployed within a dedicated virtual machine in the UMS data center, ensuring that all communication with Microsoft Fabric and related Microsoft cloud services is initiated outbound from the trusted internal network. This design eliminates the need for inbound firewall exceptions, thereby minimizing exposure to external threats.

Outbound-only connections are a critical security measure, as they ensure that all traffic from the OPDG to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services is encrypted via HTTPS. This approach also simplifies firewall configuration and supports compliance with UMS’s internal IT security policies. Enfrasys Solutions will document the network design and required firewall rules, while UMS is responsible for providing detailed on-premises network topology information to ensure compatibility and optimal performance.

## 4.2 Microsoft Fabric Data Gateway

The Microsoft On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a core component of the hybrid data integration strategy. The OPDG enables secure, high-throughput communication between UMS’s on-premises SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse and Microsoft Fabric services in Azure. By deploying the gateway within a dedicated virtual machine, UMS ensures that sensitive data is transmitted securely without the need for inbound network access.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends the OPDG be installed on a Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) or Windows 10 (64-bit) virtual machine, provisioned with sufficient CPU and memory resources to handle anticipated data loads. The gateway leverages the .NET Framework 4.8 runtime and is optimized for environments where data privacy and regulatory compliance are paramount. The VM must be configured to allow only outbound HTTPS (TCP port 443) and HTTP (TCP port 80, for connectivity checks) traffic to specific Microsoft endpoints, as detailed in the network security group (NSG) rules.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for designing, documenting, and validating the OPDG deployment and associated network rules. UMS must provide detailed on-premises network information, including IP addressing, firewall configuration, and routing details, to facilitate seamless integration and support troubleshooting.

The following table summarizes the recommended VM specifications for hosting the OPDG:

| Component         | Specification                                  |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Operating System  | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework    | Version 4.8                                    |
| vCPU              | 8 cores                                        |
| RAM               | Minimum 8 GB                                   |

The architecture diagram below illustrates the data flow from the UMS SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, through the On-Premises Data Gateway, to Microsoft Fabric in Azure. The diagram highlights the outbound-only connection model, ensuring secure, controlled data transfer.

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 1: Data flow from UMS SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse to Microsoft Fabric via On-Premises Data Gateway.

## 4.3 Outbound Security Rules for Data Gateway

Securing the outbound connectivity of the OPDG virtual machine is critical to maintaining the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data as it traverses from UMS’s on-premises environment to Microsoft Fabric. Enfrasys Solutions has defined a set of Azure Network Security Group (NSG) rules that must be applied to the OPDG VM’s network interface or subnet. These rules are designed to allow only the minimum necessary outbound traffic required for the OPDG to function, in accordance with Microsoft’s security best practices.

The following table details the required NSG rules:

| Name                        | Priority | Source                 | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                       | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                    | 80               | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules collectively ensure that the OPDG VM can authenticate with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD), communicate securely with Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints, and perform network connectivity status checks. All other outbound traffic should be denied by default to maintain a strong security posture.

Enfrasys Solutions will oversee the design and implementation of these network security controls, while UMS is responsible for ensuring that the on-premises firewall and network infrastructure permit the required outbound connections from the OPDG VM.

## 4.4 Conditional Access and User Authentication

To further secure access to Microsoft Fabric resources, Enfrasys Solutions recommends the implementation of Entra Conditional Access policies. These policies evaluate user and device signals, such as user identity, group membership, and network location, before granting access to Microsoft Fabric services. This approach ensures that only authorized users, whether internal or external to UMS, can access sensitive data and analytics resources.

The authentication flow begins with a user attempting to connect to Microsoft Fabric. The request is evaluated against Entra Conditional Access policies, which aggregate contextual signals and enforce access decisions in real time. If the policy requirements are met, access is granted; otherwise, the user is denied entry. This layered security model protects UMS’s data assets against unauthorized access and aligns with modern zero-trust principles.

The following diagram illustrates the conditional access flow for Microsoft Fabric:

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 2: Conditional access flow for Microsoft Fabric authentication.

Enfrasys Solutions will design and document the required conditional access policies, while UMS is responsible for reviewing and approving policy configurations to align with institutional security requirements.

# 5.0 Roles in Microsoft Fabric & Power BI

This section defines the roles, responsibilities, and access assignments within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). It details the administrative and workspace roles, their permissions, and the recommended personnel mapping to ensure secure, efficient, and auditable platform operations.

## 5.1 Microsoft Fabric Administrator Role

Microsoft Fabric is a unified analytics platform that integrates data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence into a single SaaS environment. The Fabric Administrator role is a pivotal position within this ecosystem, responsible for overseeing platform governance, security, and operational health.

The Fabric Administrator is entrusted with configuring tenant-level settings, enforcing security and compliance policies, managing user provisioning, and monitoring resource usage. This role is also responsible for delegating workspace-level permissions, integrating with Microsoft Entra ID for identity management, and ensuring that all activities within the Fabric environment adhere to Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s data governance requirements. Enfrasys Solutions recommends that only highly trusted personnel with a strong understanding of both technical and organizational policies be assigned to this role, as administrators have extensive permissions that can impact the entire analytics platform.

The responsibilities of the Fabric Administrator include, but are not limited to:

- Managing and assigning workspace roles across the organization.
- Configuring and enforcing data access policies.
- Monitoring platform usage and auditing activities for compliance.
- Overseeing integration with external data sources and security controls.
- Coordinating with Enfrasys Solutions for escalated support and platform updates.

The following table summarizes the key capabilities of the Fabric Administrator role:

| Capability | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| Tenant Settings Management | Configure global settings, enable/disable features, and set compliance policies. |
| User and Role Provisioning | Assign users to workspaces and roles, manage group memberships. |
| Security Enforcement | Apply data protection, access controls, and monitor audit logs. |
| Resource Monitoring | Track usage, quotas, and optimize resource allocation. |
| Integration Oversight | Manage connections to external data sources and third-party applications. |

## 5.2 Fabric-native Roles

Microsoft Fabric and Power BI provide a comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC) model to enable granular permissions management. Native roles are designed to align with common analytics and data governance personas, ensuring that each user has the appropriate level of access for their responsibilities.

The primary native roles include:

- **Admin:** Full control over workspace and its content, including role assignments and deletion.
- **Contributor:** Can create, edit, and publish content within the workspace, but cannot manage workspace settings.
- **Member:** Can view and interact with content, and contribute to collaborative analytics tasks.
- **Viewer:** Read-only access to published reports and dashboards, suitable for stakeholders and decision-makers.

These roles are mapped to typical analytics functions such as Data Engineer, Data Scientist, Business Analyst, and Stakeholder. Assigning the correct role ensures that sensitive data is protected, operational workflows are streamlined, and audit requirements are met. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a least-privilege approach, granting users only the permissions necessary for their job functions.

The following table details the permissions associated with each workspace-level role:

| Permission | Admin | Contributor | Member | Viewer |
|------------|-------|-------------|--------|--------|
| Add/Remove Users | Yes | No | No | No |
| Publish Content | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Edit Content | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| View Content | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Delete Workspace | Yes | No | No | No |
| Manage Data Sources | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Share Reports | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Audit Logs Access | Yes | No | No | No |

This role structure ensures that Universiti Malaysia Sabah can maintain robust data security while enabling productive collaboration across analytics teams and stakeholders.

## 5.3 UMS Workspace Role and Access

Effective assignment of workspace roles is essential for operational clarity and platform security. Enfrasys Solutions has mapped the recommended workspace roles to Universiti Malaysia Sabah personnel based on their functional responsibilities and departmental alignment. This mapping is designed to facilitate streamlined analytics workflows, clear accountability, and compliance with organizational policies.

Enfrasys Solutions proposes the following role assignments. Universiti Malaysia Sabah shall review and confirm the personnel designated for each role to ensure alignment with internal governance and operational requirements.

| No. | Workspace Role | Suggested Role | UMS PIC |
|-----|---------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Workspace Admin | Fabric Admin / Data Engineer | Dr Suraya Binti Alias (Sektor Analitik Data DARTIC, PPDM Official), Mohd. Zamrie Bin Musa, Noor Afiza Binti Momen, Ts AG Azihar @ AG KU Azihar Bin AG Tangah |
| 2 | Workspace Contributor | Data Engineer / Business User Admin | Mohd Khairul Nizam Bin Senu (DB), Assie Zaee Petrus (DB), Muhaimin Bin Mad Noor (Infra), Nuraliah Binti Alias (Infra), Chong Tze Chien (Server Security), Abdul Latif Matali (Server Security) |
| 3 | Workspace Viewer | Data Analyst / Stakeholder / Project Director | Director, Dr Farashazillah Binti Yahya, Stakeholders: Roslee Bin Talip, Dayangku Rozlina BTE PG. Aliuddin, Norhanieyati Binti Norhaizat, Zaidie @ Mohd Zaidie Bin Adilai, Professor Madya TS Dr Mohd Hanafi Bin Ahmad Hijazi |

This mapping ensures that administrative control remains with trusted technical leads, contributors are empowered to manage and develop analytics content, and stakeholders receive timely access to insights without risk to data integrity. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for validating the personnel assignments and communicating any changes to Enfrasys Solutions to maintain accurate access control.

By implementing this structured role and access model, Universiti Malaysia Sabah will benefit from enhanced security, operational efficiency, and clear auditability within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environments.

# 6.0 Microsoft Fabric & Power BI Design and Decision

This section details the solution design for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) data workflow, analytics, and governance using Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. The design aligns with UMS’s business requirements for secure, scalable, and governed data analytics.

## 6.1 Data Workflow Design Considerations

A robust and scalable data workflow is essential for UMS to achieve seamless data ingestion, transformation, machine learning, and analytics. Microsoft Fabric provides a unified platform to orchestrate these processes, leveraging services such as Data Factory for integration, Synapse for analytics, and Data Science for machine learning. The workflow ensures that data flows securely from on-premises sources to cloud-based analytics, supporting both operational efficiency and data-driven decision-making.

The design leverages a multi-layered Lakehouse architecture (Bronze, Silver, Gold) to ensure data quality, traceability, and reusability. Each layer serves a distinct purpose: raw data landing (Bronze), cleansing and transformation (Silver), and business-ready analytics (Gold). Dataflows and notebooks facilitate movement and transformation between layers, while AutoML and manual ML processes enable advanced analytics. The integration with Power BI via OneLake ensures that insights are delivered efficiently to stakeholders.

The following table summarizes the key design considerations for each stage of the workflow:

| ID   | Description                                                                                                    | Workload Type    |
|------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| DC01 | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                           | Data Factory     |
| DC02 | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                          | Data Engineering |
| DC03 | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the Bronze Lakehouse.                               | Data Engineering |
| DC04 | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the Silver Lakehouse.                              | Data Engineering |
| DC05 | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering |
| DC06 | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold).    | Data Factory     |
| DC07 | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse. | Data Science     |
| DC08 | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.                   | Power BI         |
| DC09 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                        | Power BI         |

## 6.2 Data Workflow Design Decisions

Enfrasys Solutions recommends a modern data platform architecture utilizing Microsoft Fabric’s Lakehouse paradigm, Data Factory, and Power BI for UMS. This approach ensures data is efficiently ingested from on-premises sources, processed through scalable transformation layers, and made available for advanced analytics and visualization.

The data ingestion begins with the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG), securely bridging the Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse and Fabric’s Data Factory. Data flows into the Bronze Lakehouse as raw records, ensuring data lineage and auditability. Dataflow Gen2 and Notebooks are then used to cleanse and transform data into the Silver Lakehouse, preparing it for machine learning and analytics workloads. The Gold Lakehouse stores highly refined, business-ready data, supporting both manual and AutoML machine learning processes. Outputs from these processes are registered in the Fabric Model Registry for lifecycle management, and scored results are stored in Gold for downstream analytics.

Power BI connects directly to the Semantic Model, which is built atop the Gold Lakehouse, providing UMS with real-time, governed analytics. This architecture enables UMS to scale analytics, ensure data quality, and support advanced use cases such as predictive modeling.

The following diagram illustrates the end-to-end data workflow, from on-premises ingestion to advanced analytics and visualization:

The diagram below shows the complete data workflow architecture, highlighting the movement of data from the UMS Staging Area (Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse) through the On-Premise Data Gateway into Microsoft Fabric. Data is processed through Bronze, Silver, and Gold Lakehouse layers, with machine learning and analytics performed before visualization in Power BI.

![](images\slide_009_img_003.png)

Figure 6.1: End-to-end data workflow design from ingestion to analytics.

For machine learning, the following diagram demonstrates the integration of both AutoML and manual ML processes within the Fabric ecosystem. Cleaned data from the Silver Lakehouse is used for experimentation, with results scored and stored in the Gold Lakehouse, ensuring consistency and governance.

![](images\slide_021_img_005.png)

Figure 6.2: AutoML and manual ML workflow within Microsoft Fabric.

The following table summarizes the key design decisions supporting this architecture:

| No.  | Design Decision                                                                                                         | Decision         |
|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| DD01 | Implement a multi-layer Lakehouse architecture (Bronze, Silver, Gold) for data lifecycle management.                   | Adopted          |
| DD02 | Use Data Factory pipelines for secure and automated data ingestion from on-premises SQL Server.                        | Adopted          |
| DD03 | Employ Dataflow Gen2 and Notebooks for data cleansing, transformation, and movement between Lakehouse layers.          | Adopted          |
| DD04 | Integrate AutoML and manual ML using Fabric Notebooks for advanced analytics and model management.                     | Adopted          |
| DD05 | Register trained models in Fabric Model Registry and store scored outputs in Gold Lakehouse.                           | Adopted          |
| DD06 | Build Power BI Semantic Model on Gold Lakehouse and connect via OneLake for governed analytics and visualization.      | Adopted          |
| DD07 | Use On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) for secure data movement without inbound firewall rules.                           | Adopted          |

## 6.3 Access Design Considerations and Decisions

Securing access to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI is paramount for UMS’s data governance and compliance. Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is the foundation for identity and access management, supporting Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Conditional Access policies are designed to enforce contextual controls, such as user location, device status, and risk signals, before granting access to sensitive analytics resources.

Due to current licensing constraints, Conditional Access policies are not fully enabled for UMS. Instead, MFA is enforced at the tenant level to provide an additional security layer. This approach ensures that only authenticated and verified users can access the Fabric environment, while laying the groundwork for future enhancements should UMS acquire the necessary Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses.

### 6.3.1 Conditional Access

Conditional Access is a policy-based approach to enforcing access controls based on user context, device health, and risk assessment. While UMS does not currently have the required licensing for full Conditional Access, the design anticipates its future implementation.

The following diagram illustrates the high-level access flow, where user authentication attempts are evaluated by Entra Conditional Access policies. Signals are collected, a decision is made, and enforcement occurs before granting access to Microsoft Fabric resources.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 6.3: Conditional Access authentication and enforcement flow.

The following table documents the access decisions:

| No.  | Conditional Access                                                                                                     |
|------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                      |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts                                  |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                    |

### 6.3.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

Multi-Factor Authentication is enforced at the tenant level for all UMS users, providing an essential security layer in the absence of Conditional Access licensing. MFA requires users to provide a secondary verification factor, significantly reducing the risk of unauthorized access.

### 6.3.3 Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID is the cloud-based identity and access management service that underpins authentication and authorization for Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. All user accounts, group memberships, and access policies are centrally managed, ensuring compliance and auditability.

## 6.4 Virtual Machine (VM) Size

The On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a critical component for secure data integration between UMS’s on-premises SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric. The gateway is deployed on a dedicated virtual machine to ensure high availability, performance, and security. The following table specifies the recommended VM configuration for the Data Gateway:

| Component         | Specification                                   |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Operating System  | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework    | Version 4.8                                    |
| vCPU              | 8 cores                                        |
| RAM               | Minimum 8 GB                                   |

The following diagram illustrates the secure data flow from the on-premises SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, through the On-Premises Data Gateway, into the UMS Fabric environment. This architecture ensures that all data transfers are outbound, eliminating the need for inbound firewall rules and enhancing security.

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 6.4: Secure data integration using On-Premises Data Gateway.

## 6.5 Resource Organization Design

A well-structured resource organization is essential for effective governance, cost management, and operational efficiency. Enfrasys Solutions has designed a hierarchical management group structure, standardized naming conventions, and tagging strategies to support UMS’s long-term cloud adoption.

### 6.5.1 Management Groups

Management groups in Azure provide a scalable way to organize resources, apply governance policies, and delegate administrative responsibilities. For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions recommends a top-level management group for the university, with sub-groups for production, development, and analytics workloads. This structure facilitates policy enforcement, cost tracking, and access control across environments.

### 6.5.2 Naming and Tagging

Consistent naming conventions and tagging are critical for resource discoverability, automation, and cost allocation. The following tables define the naming standards for Azure resources and Microsoft Fabric components.

#### Azure Naming Convention

| Resource        | Abbreviation | Example                |
|-----------------|-------------|------------------------|
| Resource Group  | RG          | UMS-Fabric-RG          |
| Virtual Machine | VM          | UMS-Gateway-VM         |
| Storage Account | ST          | umsdatastorage01       |

#### Fabric Naming Convention

| No. | Item in Fabric    | Naming Convention                      |
|-----|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1   | Fabric Capacity   | stagingareadevcapacity01               |
| 2   | Workspace         | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                  |
| 3   | Lakehouse         | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_LK |
| 4   | Dataflow          | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_to_Gold_DF |
| 5   | Semantic Model    | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM   |
| 6   | Pipeline          | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL          |
| 7   | Report            | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report |

#### Acronyms

| Resource/Item   | Abbreviation |
|-----------------|--------------|
| Lakehouse       | LK           |
| Dataflow        | DF           |
| Semantic Model  | SM           |
| Pipeline        | PL           |
| Workspace       | WS           |

## 6.6 Governance Consideration

Effective governance ensures that UMS’s data platform remains secure, compliant, and cost-effective. Enfrasys Solutions documents the design and governance disciplines, while UMS is responsible for reviewing and providing feedback.

### 6.6.1 Governance Disciplines

Cost management is a critical discipline, ensuring that resource usage aligns with budgetary constraints and business priorities. Regular reviews and ownership assignment are essential for proactive governance.

| Cost Item             | Owner           | Review Frequency |
|-----------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Fabric Capacity Usage | UMS IT          | Monthly          |
| VM Consumption        | UMS IT          | Monthly          |
| Power BI Licensing    | UMS IT          | Quarterly        |
| Data Egress           | UMS IT          | Monthly          |

### 6.6.2 Violation Triggers and Actions

The following table defines triggers for governance violations and the corresponding actions and ownership assignments:

| Trigger                                 | Action                                       | Owner         |
|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|---------------|
| Exceeding Fabric Capacity Quota          | Notify IT, review workload, request increase | UMS IT        |
| Unauthorized Access Attempt              | Security incident response, audit            | UMS IT        |
| Unapproved Resource Deployment           | Resource review, decommission if necessary   | UMS IT        |
| Cost Overrun Detected                    | Cost analysis, optimize resources            | UMS IT        |

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for documenting the design and governance framework. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing, approving, and operationalizing these governance controls within their environment.

---

# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section defines the security architecture and technical controls for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric and Power BI deployment. The design addresses network security, access management, and encryption standards to ensure compliance with Microsoft best practices and to safeguard institutional data.

## 7.1 Azure Network Security Group (NSG) Overview

Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) are a fundamental security feature within Microsoft Azure, providing granular control over inbound and outbound network traffic to Azure resources. NSGs enable organizations to define security rules that allow or deny traffic based on source, destination, port, and protocol. This capability is essential for enforcing a least-privilege network model and protecting critical assets from unauthorized access or exposure.

For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions has designed NSG rules specifically for the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine, which facilitates secure connectivity between on-premises resources and Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that only legitimate traffic flows between the gateway VM and required Microsoft endpoints, minimizing the attack surface and supporting regulatory compliance.

The following tables summarize the proposed inbound and outbound NSG rules. Inbound rules are intentionally restrictive to prevent unsolicited access, while outbound rules are crafted to enable only the necessary communications for authentication, data transfer, and connectivity testing.

### Inbound NSG Rules

Inbound traffic to the OPDG VM is tightly controlled. By default, all inbound traffic is denied except for management and monitoring purposes, which are handled through separate, secured channels. No additional inbound rules are required for the OPDG VM to communicate with Microsoft Fabric or Power BI, as all operational traffic is outbound-initiated.

| Name                     | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination            | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|--------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Deny_All_Inbound         | 4096     | Any                   | *            | Any                   | *                 | Any      | Deny   |

### Outbound NSG Rules

The following outbound rules are required to enable the OPDG VM to securely interact with Microsoft cloud services. Each rule is assigned a unique priority to ensure proper evaluation order. These rules allow only HTTPS (TCP 443) and HTTP (TCP 80) traffic to specific Microsoft service endpoints, supporting secure authentication, data transfer, and connectivity checks.

| Name                        | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                    | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |
| Deny_All_Other_Outbound     | 4096     | Any                   | *            | Any                                                                                                            | *                 | Any      | Deny   |

These rules ensure that the OPDG VM can fulfill its operational requirements without exposing unnecessary network paths, thus reducing the risk of lateral movement and data exfiltration.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Encryption is a cornerstone of a secure cloud architecture, ensuring that data is protected both at rest and in transit. Microsoft Azure and Microsoft Fabric provide robust, enterprise-grade encryption mechanisms compliant with international standards such as FIPS 140-2 and ISO 27001.

### Encryption at Rest

All data stored within Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and associated Azure resources is encrypted at rest using Microsoft-managed keys by default. This includes data in OneLake, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage. Azure leverages industry-standard AES-256 encryption to protect data on disk. For highly sensitive workloads, UMS may elect to implement customer-managed keys (CMK) for additional control over key lifecycle and access policies. Enfrasys Solutions recommends enabling advanced auditing and monitoring to track access to encrypted data stores.

### Encryption in Transit

Data in transit between the OPDG VM, Microsoft Fabric, and Power BI services is secured using Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.2 or higher. All outbound connections from the gateway VM to Microsoft cloud endpoints are established over HTTPS (TCP 443), ensuring confidentiality and integrity of data as it traverses public and private networks. This approach mitigates the risk of man-in-the-middle attacks and eavesdropping.

Enfrasys Solutions has designed the network architecture to enforce encryption for all data flows, and recommends regular validation of TLS configurations using Microsoft’s security assessment tools. UMS is responsible for reviewing and approving the encryption design, as well as ensuring end-user devices and on-premises systems comply with these standards.

### Encryption Design Responsibilities

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for designing and documenting the encryption architecture, including the selection of encryption algorithms, key management strategies, and integration with Azure security controls. Universiti Malaysia Sabah shall review and approve the proposed encryption design, and ensure that all operational processes adhere to the documented standards.

## 7.3 Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

Conditional Access is a policy-based approach within Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) to enforce access controls based on user, location, device, and risk signals. While UMS’s current licensing does not support advanced Conditional Access policies (Entra ID P1/P2), Enfrasys Solutions has enforced Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level to strengthen authentication and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. This approach ensures that all users, including external collaborators, are subject to additional verification before accessing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI resources.

The following diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies and MFA enforcement. The process begins with a user authentication attempt, which is evaluated against Entra policies. If access is granted, the user is allowed inbound access to Microsoft Fabric, with all data flows protected by TLS.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 7.1: Authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access and MFA.

### Conditional Access Design Decisions

The following table summarizes the key design decisions for access control and MFA enforcement in the UMS environment.

| No.  | Design Decision                                                                                                         | Decision |
|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage | Deferred (pending advanced licensing) |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                       | Deferred (pending advanced licensing) |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts                                   | Enforced at tenant level              |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                    | Enabled with MFA                      |

Enfrasys Solutions will revisit Conditional Access policy configuration if UMS upgrades to Entra ID P1/P2 licensing in the future.

## 7.4 Summary of Security Responsibilities

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for designing, documenting, and implementing the NSG rules, encryption standards, and MFA enforcement. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing and approving the security design, monitoring compliance, and managing user onboarding and offboarding processes in accordance with institutional policy.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration strategy for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The approach is structured to ensure a smooth transition, minimize risks, and guarantee operational continuity through clear task ownership and phased execution.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration/Setup

A robust pre-migration phase is critical to the success of the overall deployment. This phase encompasses all preparatory activities required to establish the foundational environment, validate infrastructure readiness, and ensure alignment between Enfrasys Solutions and UMS. The preparatory steps include formal review and sign-off of the design documentation, confirmation of staging infrastructure, initial environment setup, and installation of connectivity components such as the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG).

Enfrasys Solutions will lead the technical execution of these tasks, leveraging best practices to ensure that all prerequisites are met before migration activities commence. UMS is responsible for providing timely validation and confirmation, particularly for infrastructure components that require institutional approval or provisioning.

The following table details the pre-migration activities, responsible parties, and current status:

| No | Activities                                                        | Planned Start   | Action By      | Status       | Remarks |
|----|-------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|---------------|--------------|---------|
| 1  | Deliver Design Document to be reviewed by UMS & Sign Off          | Fri, 7-Nov-25   | Enfrasys, UMS | Not-Started  |         |
| 2  | Confirmation on the new server (data warehouse copy – Staging Area DB) to be ingested | Mon, 10-Nov-25  | UMS           | Not-Started  |         |
| 3  | Fabric Environment Setup                                           | Wed, 12-Nov-25  | Enfrasys      | Not-Started  |         |
| 4  | Install On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in VM to establish Data Gateway | Mon, 17-Nov-25  | Enfrasys, UMS | Not-Started  |         |

This structured approach ensures that all foundational elements are in place, reducing the likelihood of downstream issues during migration and deployment.

## 8.2 Pilot

The pilot phase is designed to validate the deployment approach, test migration workflows, and surface any technical or operational issues in a controlled environment before full-scale rollout. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a timeline-driven pilot, structured as follows:

- **D-7 (One Week Before Cutover):** Finalize pilot scope, confirm pilot users, and ensure all pre-migration tasks are completed. Conduct readiness review with UMS stakeholders.
- **D0 (Pilot Cutover Day):** Execute migration of selected datasets and workloads to the Microsoft Fabric environment. Monitor system performance, data integrity, and user access in real-time.
- **D+1 (Post-Pilot Day):** Collect feedback from pilot users, resolve any identified issues, and document lessons learned. Conduct a formal review with UMS to confirm pilot success and readiness for production rollout.

This phased approach allows UMS to experience the new environment with minimal risk, while Enfrasys Solutions can fine-tune migration processes based on real-world feedback.

The following diagram illustrates the pilot migration timeline and key milestones:

![](images/pilot_migration_timeline.png)

Figure 1: Pilot migration timeline and milestones.

## 8.3 Production

Upon successful completion of the pilot, the production rollout will be executed in accordance with the validated migration plan. The production phase involves migrating all agreed-upon datasets, configuring operational monitoring, and ensuring that all users have appropriate access to the new environment.

Enfrasys Solutions will lead the execution of all migration activities, including data ingestion, system validation, and environment optimization. UMS is responsible for supporting user acceptance testing, validating migrated data, and communicating with end-users regarding the transition.

The production rollout will follow these structured steps:

1. Confirm production migration window and communicate to all stakeholders.
2. Execute data migration and environment configuration.
3. Perform comprehensive post-migration validation, including data integrity checks and performance benchmarking.
4. Facilitate user acceptance testing with UMS stakeholders.
5. Transition environment to steady-state operations.

The following table outlines the key production rollout activities and responsibilities:

| Item | Activities                                   | Action By      | Status      |
|------|----------------------------------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1    | Confirm production migration window          | Enfrasys, UMS | Planned     |
| 2    | Execute production data migration            | Enfrasys      | Planned     |
| 3    | Post-migration validation and benchmarking   | Enfrasys, UMS | Planned     |
| 4    | User acceptance testing                      | UMS           | Planned     |
| 5    | Transition to steady-state operations        | Enfrasys      | Planned     |

This disciplined approach ensures a seamless migration with clear accountability and minimal disruption to UMS operations.

## 8.4 Post-Migration/Support

Following the production migration, a dedicated post-migration support phase is essential to ensure operational stability, address any residual issues, and facilitate knowledge transfer to UMS personnel. Enfrasys Solutions will provide immediate post-migration support, monitor system health, and resolve any incidents that arise during the stabilization period.

Key activities in this phase include:

- Monitoring environment performance and resolving post-migration issues.
- Conducting knowledge transfer sessions and documentation handover.
- Formalizing the transition of operational responsibilities to UMS IT teams.
- Closing out the migration project with a final review meeting.

The following table details the post-migration support and handover activities:

| Item | Activities                                   | Action By      | Status      |
|------|----------------------------------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1    | Monitor environment and resolve incidents    | Enfrasys      | Planned     |
| 2    | Knowledge transfer to UMS IT teams           | Enfrasys      | Planned     |
| 3    | Documentation handover                       | Enfrasys      | Planned     |
| 4    | Final project review and closure             | Enfrasys, UMS | Planned     |

The post-migration support phase ensures UMS is fully equipped to manage and operate the new environment, with Enfrasys Solutions providing expert guidance throughout the transition period.

asset_library.description:
The diagram illustrates a technical architecture involving Azure components and user access management. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This represents a Resource Group in Azure, which is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It is depicted with an icon suggesting a fabric or mesh network, indicating a structured environment for managing resources.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely refers to a User Management System or a similar service within the Azure environment. It is part of the Fabric-RG, suggesting it is a resource managed within the specified resource group.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature in Azure Active Directory that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It is represented by a shield icon, indicating its role in protecting resources and managing user access.

4. **External User**: This represents a user outside the organization who needs access to resources within the Azure environment. The diagram shows a connection from the UMS Fabric to the External User, indicating that the user management system facilitates access for external users.

**Data Flow and Connections**:
- The UMS Fabric within the Fabric-RG manages user identities and access permissions.
- Conditional Access policies are applied to ensure that only authorized external users can access resources.
- The flow from UMS Fabric to External User suggests a process where user credentials and access rights are verified and granted based on predefined conditions.

This architecture ensures secure and managed access for external users to resources within the Azure environment, leveraging Azure's resource management and security capabilities.

The diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated against Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   - **Signal**: Various signals are gathered, such as user and group information, and network location.
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access permissions.
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, allowing or denying access based on the policy evaluation.

3. **Inbound Access to Microsoft Fabric**: If access is granted, the user is allowed inbound access to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: The diagram shows integration with various Microsoft Fabric components, including data and analytics services, represented by icons. OneLake is highlighted as a component within Microsoft Fabric, indicating its role in data storage or management.

This architecture ensures secure and conditional access to Microsoft Fabric resources, leveraging Entra policies to evaluate and enforce access control based on user context and network signals.

The diagram represents a data processing and analytics architecture using Azure components. 

1. **UMS Staging Area**: Data originates from a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, labeled as the UMS Staging Area. This is the initial data source.

2. **Data Factory**: Azure Data Factory is used to ingest data from the UMS Staging Area. It orchestrates the data movement and transformation processes.

3. **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: The ingested data is first stored in the Bronze layer, which typically contains raw, unprocessed data.

4. **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Data is then moved to the Silver layer, where it is cleaned and transformed. This layer contains more refined data.

5. **Data Science**: The refined data in the Silver layer is utilized for data science processes, likely involving analysis, machine learning, or other advanced computations.

6. **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The final data storage layer is the Gold layer, which contains highly processed and business-ready data.

7. **Identity_SM**: This component likely represents a service or module for identity management or security, ensuring data integrity and access control.

8. **OneLake**: Data is stored in OneLake, a unified data lake service, facilitating seamless data access and management.

9. **Power BI**: The processed data in the Gold layer is connected to Power BI via OneLake for visualization and reporting, enabling business intelligence and insights.

The architecture showcases a structured data flow from raw ingestion to refined analytics, leveraging Azure services for efficient data processing and visualization.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components: a SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, an On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG), and UMS Fabric.

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse**: This component serves as the initial data repository where data is stored and managed. It is likely used for staging data before further processing or analysis.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**: This gateway facilitates secure communication between the on-premise data warehouse and cloud services. It enables data to be securely transferred without requiring inbound ports, ensuring that the connection is outbound and secure.

3. **UMS Fabric**: This component represents a cloud-based service or platform that interacts with the on-premise data through the OPDG. It likely provides additional processing, analytics, or integration capabilities.

**Data Flow**:
- Data originates from the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse.
- It is securely transmitted to the UMS Fabric via the On-Premise Data Gateway.
- The OPDG ensures that the connection is outbound, enhancing security by eliminating the need for inbound ports.
- UMS Fabric processes or utilizes the data for further operations, analytics, or integration tasks.

The diagram represents a data processing and machine learning workflow within an Azure environment, utilizing UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and integration.

1. **Data Source**: 
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, likely containing pre-processed or intermediate data.

2. **Machine Learning Processes**:
   - **AutoML**: 
     - **AutoML Experiment**: Data from XX_Silver_LK Tables is fed into an AutoML experiment, which automates the process of model selection and hyperparameter tuning.
     - **Result Scoring (Using Notebook)**: The results from the AutoML experiment are scored using a notebook, indicating a custom evaluation or analysis process.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**:
     - **Modelling (Using Notebook)**: Alternatively, data can be processed through manual machine learning modeling using notebooks, allowing for custom model development and experimentation.

3. **Data Integration and Storage**:
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The output from both AutoML and manual machine learning processes is stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables, representing refined and validated data ready for further analysis or reporting.
   - **Identity_SM**: This component likely handles identity management or security, ensuring data integrity and access control.

4. **Data Visualization and Reporting**:
   - **Power BI**: The XX_Gold_LK Tables are connected to Power BI via OneLake, enabling visualization and reporting of the processed data for business intelligence purposes.

5. **Infrastructure**:
   - **UMS Fabric**: Provides the overarching framework for data management and orchestration.
   - **OneLake**: Facilitates seamless data integration and connectivity between components, ensuring efficient data flow and accessibility.

The diagram illustrates a comprehensive workflow for data processing, machine learning, and business intelligence within an Azure-based architecture.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.
