# UMS Power BI & Fabric Solution Design

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a concise overview of the project, the purpose of this Solution Design Document, and identifies the intended audience and their responsibilities for the Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) Power BI & Microsoft Fabric initiative.

## 2.1 Project Overview

The Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) Power BI & Fabric project is strategically scoped to modernize and streamline the university’s data management and analytics capabilities. The primary business driver for this initiative is to enable UMS to leverage advanced data workflows, transforming raw data into actionable insights that support informed decision-making and institutional excellence.

The project encompasses the design and implementation of an end-to-end data workflow within Microsoft Fabric, covering the full spectrum from data ingestion to transformation and visualization. This includes the establishment of a dedicated Fabric workspace, development of robust ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines, execution of machine learning processes, and seamless integration with Power BI for business-aligned analytics. The solution is designed to align with UMS’s broader cloud adoption strategy, ensuring scalability, security, and future-readiness.

Key Microsoft Fabric services to be utilized include:
- **Fabric Data Factory:** Facilitates data integration and orchestration, ensuring reliable and efficient movement of data from diverse sources into the analytics environment.
- **Fabric Synapse:** Provides advanced analytics and big data processing capabilities, enabling UMS to perform complex data transformations and aggregations at scale.
- **Data Science Capabilities:** Supports the development and operationalization of machine learning models, empowering UMS with predictive analytics and data-driven insights.
- **Power BI Integration:** Delivers interactive dashboards and reports, making analytics accessible to business users and stakeholders across the university.

By adopting this architecture, UMS will benefit from improved data governance, enhanced analytical capabilities, and a scalable platform that supports both current and future institutional needs.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

The intent of this Solution Design Document is to serve as the authoritative reference for the design, implementation, and governance of the Power BI & Microsoft Fabric solution for Universiti Malaysia Sabah. This document details the architectural decisions, technical specifications, workflows, and security considerations that underpin the proposed solution.

Throughout the project lifecycle, this document will function as the primary blueprint for all technical teams, project managers, and stakeholders. It ensures alignment between UMS’s business objectives and the technical implementation, providing a clear roadmap for deployment, ongoing management, and future enhancements. The Solution Design Document also facilitates effective communication between Universiti Malaysia Sabah and Enfrasys Solutions, supporting a structured and auditable project delivery process.

## 2.3 Document Audience

This Solution Design Document is intended for a broad range of stakeholders involved in the planning, implementation, and oversight of the Power BI & Microsoft Fabric project at Universiti Malaysia Sabah. The audience includes, but is not limited to:

- UMS IT leadership and technical staff responsible for data management, analytics, and cloud services.
- Enfrasys Solutions architects, consultants, and project delivery teams.
- Project sponsors and executive stakeholders at UMS who require visibility into the solution design and alignment with institutional goals.
- Governance and compliance officers overseeing data security and regulatory adherence.

The following table maps each stakeholder role to its primary responsibilities within the context of this document and the overall project:

| Stakeholder Role           | Responsibility                                      |
|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| UMS IT Team                | Review solution design, provide business requirements, validate technical alignment, and sign-off on deliverables. |
| UMS Project Sponsor        | Oversee project objectives, ensure alignment with institutional goals, and approve major milestones. |
| Enfrasys Solutions Architect | Author the Solution Design Document, lead architectural design, and ensure best practices are applied. |
| Enfrasys Solutions Consultant | Review and validate the technical content, provide subject matter expertise, and support implementation. |
| UMS Data Governance Officer | Ensure compliance with data security, privacy, and regulatory requirements. |
| UMS End Users              | Provide feedback on analytics needs and participate in user acceptance testing. |

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for authoring and reviewing this Solution Design Document, while Universiti Malaysia Sabah holds responsibility for final review and formal sign-off. This clear delineation of responsibilities ensures accountability and effective collaboration throughout the project lifecycle.

# 3.0 Microsoft Fabric, Power BI & Entra ID Overview

This section provides a comprehensive overview of the core Microsoft technologies that form the foundation of the proposed solution for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The following sub-sections detail Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Microsoft Entra ID, including their core capabilities, relevance to UMS, and how they underpin the overall data and analytics architecture.

## 3.1 Microsoft Fabric

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated analytics platform designed to unify data movement, data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence within a single SaaS environment. Fabric consolidates multiple Microsoft data services—such as Data Factory, Synapse, and Power BI—into a cohesive platform, enabling organizations to streamline their data workflows from ingestion to visualization.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends Microsoft Fabric as the backbone of UMS’s analytics architecture due to its ability to deliver end-to-end data workflows. Key capabilities include Fabric Data Factory for data integration and orchestration, Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, and built-in Data Science modules for machine learning applications. By leveraging Fabric, UMS can establish a unified workspace that supports the development of robust ETL pipelines, facilitates scalable machine learning processes, and integrates seamlessly with visualization tools.

The adoption of Microsoft Fabric ensures that UMS benefits from a modern, cloud-native platform that supports secure, scalable, and collaborative data operations. This approach not only accelerates time-to-insight but also aligns with best practices for cloud adoption and digital transformation within higher education.

## 3.2 Power BI

Power BI is Microsoft’s enterprise-grade business analytics service that empowers organizations to visualize data, share insights, and drive informed decision-making through interactive reports and dashboards. Power BI integrates natively with Microsoft Fabric, enabling seamless access to curated datasets and analytics outputs generated within the Fabric workspace.

Enfrasys Solutions has selected Power BI as the primary reporting and analytics interface for UMS. Power BI’s robust feature set includes advanced data modeling, real-time dashboarding, and support for custom visualizations. Its integration with Fabric ensures that data ingested, transformed, and analyzed within the Fabric environment is readily available for business-aligned analytics. This tight integration streamlines the workflow from data acquisition to actionable insights, reducing manual intervention and improving data governance.

For UMS, the deployment of Power BI will facilitate self-service analytics for business users, empower academic departments with timely insights, and provide executive stakeholders with consolidated, role-based dashboards. The solution architecture ensures that Power BI is both a consumer and an extension of the Fabric data platform, supporting advanced analytics scenarios and operational reporting.

## 3.3 Microsoft Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID, formerly known as Azure Active Directory (Azure AD), is Microsoft’s cloud-based identity and access management (IAM) service. Entra ID provides secure authentication, single sign-on (SSO), and conditional access controls for users, applications, and resources across the Microsoft cloud ecosystem.

Enfrasys Solutions has designated Microsoft Entra ID as the central identity provider for the solution, underpinning secure access to Fabric workspaces, Power BI reports, and all associated services. Entra ID’s capabilities include multi-factor authentication, role-based access control, and comprehensive auditing, ensuring that only authorized users can access sensitive data and analytics assets. Conditional access policies can be tailored to enforce security requirements based on user roles, device compliance, and network location.

For UMS, leveraging Microsoft Entra ID strengthens the security posture of the analytics environment, simplifies user management, and supports compliance with institutional and regulatory requirements. Entra ID’s integration with Fabric and Power BI ensures a seamless and secure user experience, enabling efficient collaboration while safeguarding institutional data. 

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the selection, configuration, and explanation of these core technologies to ensure alignment with UMS’s business objectives and IT governance standards.

# 4.0 Network Design and Decision

This section details the network architecture and design decisions supporting Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric deployment. It addresses secure connectivity between on-premises systems, the Data Gateway virtual machine, and Microsoft Fabric cloud resources, ensuring seamless, secure, and performant data movement.

## 4.1 Network Connectivity Overview

A robust and secure network architecture is fundamental to ensuring reliable data integration between UMS’s on-premises SQL Server environment and Microsoft Fabric in the cloud. Enfrasys Solutions has designed the network topology to facilitate secure and efficient data transfer, leveraging Microsoft’s best practices for hybrid connectivity.

The data integration workflow begins with the UMS on-premises SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, which serves as the primary data source. To enable secure and managed connectivity to Microsoft Fabric, an On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is deployed on a dedicated virtual machine within UMS’s data center. This gateway acts as a secure bridge, facilitating outbound-only connections to the Fabric cloud environment. By restricting the gateway to outbound traffic, the design minimizes the attack surface and eliminates the need for inbound firewall exceptions, greatly reducing security risks.

Once data is staged in the on-premises SQL Server, the OPDG establishes encrypted outbound connections to Microsoft Fabric, enabling seamless data transfer for analytics, reporting, and integration scenarios. This architecture ensures that sensitive data remains protected during transit and that UMS retains full control over its on-premises network perimeter.

The following diagram illustrates the end-to-end data flow and network connectivity between the on-premises SQL Server, the Data Gateway VM, and Microsoft Fabric resources:

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 1: Network connectivity and data flow from on-premises SQL Server through the Data Gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric.

This architecture ensures that all data transfers leverage secure, outbound-only connections, adhering to UMS’s security requirements and Microsoft’s recommended hybrid integration patterns.

## 4.2 Microsoft Fabric Data Gateway

The Microsoft On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a critical component that enables secure connectivity between UMS’s on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric cloud services. The OPDG acts as a conduit, allowing authorized cloud services to access on-premises data without exposing the internal network to inbound internet traffic.

### Role and Functionality

The OPDG is installed on a dedicated virtual machine within the UMS environment. Its primary function is to broker secure communications between the on-premises SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. The gateway uses outbound HTTPS connections to transmit data, ensuring that all communication is encrypted and that no inbound ports are required to be opened on the UMS firewall. This outbound-only architecture is a key security advantage, as it prevents unsolicited external access to the internal network.

### Deployment Topology and Security

Enfrasys Solutions recommends deploying the OPDG on a hardened Windows Server or Windows 10 virtual machine, isolated within a secure network segment. The VM should be sized appropriately to handle expected data volumes and concurrent requests, while adhering to Microsoft’s minimum requirements for the gateway service. All outbound communications from the gateway VM are restricted to specific Microsoft endpoints over secure protocols, as outlined in the network security group (NSG) rules below. This design ensures that the gateway can authenticate with Microsoft Entra ID, transfer data to Fabric and Power BI, and perform connectivity health checks, all while maintaining a strict security posture.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the network architecture and VM sizing, ensuring alignment with Microsoft’s best practices. UMS is responsible for preparing the on-premises environment, including network prerequisites and firewall configurations.

### VM Specification for Data Gateway

The following table details the recommended virtual machine specifications for the OPDG deployment:

| Component         | Operating System                       | .NET Framework | vCPU     | RAM         |
|-------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------|----------|-------------|
| Data Gateway VM   | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Server 2019    | Version 4.8    | 8 cores  | Minimum 8GB |

Enfrasys Solutions will provision and configure the Data Gateway VM according to these specifications. UMS shall ensure that the necessary on-premises infrastructure and network prerequisites are in place prior to deployment.

### Outbound Network Security Rules

To ensure secure and reliable connectivity, the following outbound NSG rules must be applied to the Data Gateway VM. These rules restrict outbound traffic to only the required Microsoft endpoints and ports, in line with Azure networking best practices.

| Name                        | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                    | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules ensure that the Data Gateway VM can securely authenticate, communicate, and transfer data to Microsoft Fabric and related services, while supporting connectivity verification and troubleshooting.

Enfrasys Solutions will implement and validate these network security rules during deployment. UMS is responsible for ensuring that external firewalls and proxies permit the required outbound connections from the Data Gateway VM.

---

This network design ensures that Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s hybrid data integration with Microsoft Fabric is secure, scalable, and aligned with both organizational policies and Microsoft’s reference architectures.

# 5.0 Roles in Microsoft Fabric

This section defines the roles, responsibilities, and assignment processes for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) within the Microsoft Fabric environment. It establishes a clear framework for role-based access control, ensuring effective governance, security, and operational efficiency across UMS’s analytics and data platform initiatives.

## 5.1 Microsoft Fabric Administrator Role

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated analytics platform that brings together data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence within a unified SaaS experience. At the core of its governance model is the Fabric Administrator role, which is critical for managing platform-wide settings, security, and compliance.

The Fabric Administrator is responsible for configuring tenant-level settings, managing workspace provisioning, overseeing data security policies, and ensuring compliance with Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s IT governance standards. This role has the authority to assign and revoke permissions, monitor usage, and enforce best practices for data lifecycle management. Administrators are also tasked with integrating Fabric with other Microsoft services, such as Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), and ensuring that all platform updates and new features are evaluated for organizational impact.

Assignment of the Fabric Administrator role must follow a formal process to maintain accountability and minimize risk. Enfrasys Solutions recommends that UMS nominate individuals from the Sektor Analitik Data DARTIC and PPDM Official teams, as these personnel are best positioned to understand both technical and business requirements. Enfrasys Solutions will facilitate the initial role mapping and provide guidance on best practices, while UMS is responsible for the actual assignment of users within their Entra ID environment.

## 5.2 Fabric-native Roles

Microsoft Fabric provides a granular role-based access control (RBAC) model, supporting a range of native roles tailored to different personas and responsibilities. These roles are designed to segregate duties, minimize the risk of unauthorized access, and streamline collaboration within analytics projects.

The primary Fabric-native roles include:

- **Fabric Administrator**: Holds tenant-level privileges to configure and govern the entire Fabric environment. Responsibilities include policy enforcement, workspace management, and integration oversight.
- **Data Engineer**: Focuses on building, maintaining, and optimizing data pipelines, datasets, and transformations. Data Engineers have permissions to create, edit, and manage dataflows, lakehouses, and datasets within assigned workspaces.
- **Data Analyst**: Specializes in analyzing data, building reports, and creating dashboards. Data Analysts typically have permissions to view and interact with datasets, as well as publish and share insights.
- **Workspace Contributor**: Granted permissions to add, edit, and manage content within a workspace, excluding administrative settings. Contributors often support Data Engineers and Analysts in collaborative development.
- **Workspace Viewer**: Restricted to read-only access, enabling users to view reports, dashboards, and datasets without the ability to modify content. This role is ideal for stakeholders, project directors, and executive sponsors.

By leveraging these roles, Universiti Malaysia Sabah can enforce the principle of least privilege, ensuring that users only have access to the data and tools necessary for their responsibilities. This approach enhances security, reduces the risk of data breaches, and promotes operational efficiency by aligning access with organizational roles.

## 5.3 UMS Workspace Role and Access

To operationalize the role-based access control model, Enfrasys Solutions has developed a mapping of Microsoft Fabric workspace roles to UMS personnel. This mapping ensures that each role is clearly defined, assigned to appropriate individuals, and aligned with UMS’s organizational structure.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the initial role mapping and recommendations. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for assigning users to these roles within the Microsoft Fabric environment, ensuring that only authorized personnel are granted access according to their designated responsibilities.

The following table details the recommended workspace role assignments for UMS:

| No. | Workspace Role      | Suggested Role                        | UMS PIC                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
|-----|---------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Workspace Admin     | Fabric Admin / Data Engineer           | Dr Suraya Binti Alias<br>Sektor Analitik Data DARTIC<br>PPDM Official<br>Mohd. Zamrie Bin Musa<br>Noor Afiza Binti Momen<br>Ts AG Azihar @ AG KU Azihar Bin AG Tangah                                                  |

| 3   | Workspace Viewer    | Data Analyst / Stakeholder / Project Director | Director<br>Dr Farashazillah Binti Yahya<br>Stakeholders<br>Roslee Bin Talip<br>Dayangku Rozlina BTE PG. Aliuddin<br>Norhanieyati Binti Norhaizat<br>Zaidie @ Mohd Zaidie Bin Adilai<br>Professor Madya TS Dr Mohd Hanafi Bin Ahmad Hijazi |

This structured approach to role assignment provides Universiti Malaysia Sabah with a robust governance model, ensuring that data access, modification, and administration are consistently managed in alignment with organizational policies and compliance requirements. Enfrasys Solutions will continue to support UMS in refining these assignments as the platform evolves and as new personnel or business requirements emerge.

# 6.0 Microsoft Fabric Design and Decision

This section details the comprehensive design and critical decisions for the Microsoft Fabric implementation at Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), encompassing data workflow, access, resource organization, and governance. The design leverages Microsoft Fabric's advanced analytics, data engineering, and security capabilities to ensure secure, scalable, and efficient data operations for UMS.

## 6.1 Data Workflow Design Considerations

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated analytics platform that unifies data engineering, data science, and business intelligence (BI) workloads. It provides a seamless environment for ingesting, transforming, modeling, and visualizing data, leveraging components such as Lakehouse, Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Power BI.

For UMS, the data workflow is designed to optimize both ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and machine learning (ML) processes. Data is ingested from the Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse into the Fabric environment using Azure Data Factory. The workflow employs a multi-layered Lakehouse architecture—Bronze for raw data, Silver for cleansed data, and Gold for transformed and modeled data. Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and SQL Analytics Endpoints are utilized for data transformation and enrichment.

Machine learning experimentation is conducted using both AutoML and manual approaches within Fabric Notebooks. The results are systematically stored in the Gold Lakehouse, ensuring consistency and traceability. Power BI connects to the Semantic Model via OneLake, enabling robust data visualization and reporting for UMS stakeholders.

The following table summarizes the key data workflow design considerations for both ETL and ML workloads:

| ID    | Description                                                                                                                      | Workload Type     |
|-------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|
| DC01  | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                                             | Data Factory      |
| DC02  | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                                            | Data Engineering  |
| DC03  | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse.                                                 | Data Engineering  |
| DC04  | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse.                                                | Data Engineering  |
| DC05  | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook, and Visual Query.    | Data Engineering  |
| DC06  | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold).                      | Data Factory      |
| DC07  | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse | Data Science      |
| DC08  | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.                                     | Power BI          |
| DC09  | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                                          | Power BI          |
| DE01  | The cleaned data stored in Silver Lakehouse will serve as the input for all machine learning experimentation.                    | Data Engineering  |
| DE02  | Manual Machine Learning approach using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python) for custom feature engineering and tuning.            | Data Science      |
| DE03  | Users will perform model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning in the Notebook environment for accuracy and explainability.       | Data Science      |
| DE04  | Trained models will be registered in the Fabric Model Registry for lifecycle management and reuse.                                | Data Science      |
| DE05  | Scoring Notebooks will apply trained models to new data, storing outputs in the Gold Lakehouse.                                  | Data Engineering  |
| DE06  | The Gold Lakehouse will serve as the standardized repository for all modeled or scored results.                                  | Data Engineering  |
| DE07  | The Semantic Model will connect to the Gold Lakehouse via Direct Lake (OneLake) for data modeling.                               | Power BI          |
| DE08  | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                                          | Power BI          |

## 6.2 Data Workflow Design Decisions

Enfrasys Solutions has architected the data workflow for UMS to ensure seamless integration between data ingestion, transformation, machine learning, and reporting. The design leverages Azure Data Factory for orchestrating data movement from the SQL Server data warehouse into the Fabric Lakehouse layers. Data transformation is achieved using Dataflow Gen2 and Notebooks, providing flexibility for both automated and manual processes.

For machine learning, both AutoML and manual modeling are supported, allowing UMS data scientists to choose the most appropriate approach for each use case. The Gold Lakehouse serves as the authoritative repository for all modeled and scored results, ensuring data consistency and simplifying downstream reporting.

Power BI is directly integrated via OneLake, enabling end-users to access up-to-date analytics and dashboards. This architecture not only optimizes data processing efficiency but also enhances data governance and traceability.

The following diagram illustrates the end-to-end data flow, from ingestion to reporting, within the UMS Fabric environment:

![](images\slide_009_img_003.png)

Figure 1: End-to-end data workflow from SQL Server ingestion to Power BI reporting.

The following diagram details the machine learning workflow, highlighting both AutoML and manual modeling paths:

![](images\slide_021_img_005.png)

Figure 2: Machine learning workflow in UMS Fabric, supporting both AutoML and manual modeling.

Key design decisions are summarized in the table below:

| No.   | Design Decision                                                                                                  | Decision                                                                                  |
|-------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01  | Data ingestion from SQL Server via Azure Data Factory into Bronze Lakehouse                                       | Implemented                                                                              |
| DD02  | Data transformation using Dataflow Gen2 and Notebooks, progression through Bronze, Silver, and Gold Lakehouses    | Implemented                                                                              |
| DD03  | Machine learning experimentation using both AutoML and manual approaches in Fabric Notebooks                      | Supported                                                                                |
| DD04  | Model registration and scoring outputs stored in Gold Lakehouse                                                   | Implemented                                                                              |
| DD05  | Power BI connects to Semantic Model via OneLake for real-time reporting                                           | Implemented                                                                              |
| DD06  | Data governance and traceability enforced at each stage of the workflow                                           | Enforced                                                                                 |

## 6.3 Access Design Considerations and Decisions

Securing access to Microsoft Fabric resources is paramount for UMS. Microsoft Fabric leverages Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) to provide robust identity and access management. Conditional Access policies allow organizations to enforce access controls based on user, device, location, and risk signals. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an additional layer of security, reducing the risk of unauthorized access. For UMS, due to licensing constraints, Conditional Access is not currently available; instead, MFA is enforced at the tenant level.

External user access is controlled to ensure only authorized collaborators can interact with UMS Fabric resources. All access policies are designed to balance security with operational usability, ensuring compliance with university and regulatory requirements.

The following diagram illustrates the access control flow, including user authentication, policy evaluation, and enforcement:

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 3: Authentication and conditional access flow for Microsoft Fabric.

### Conditional Access

Conditional Access is a powerful feature in Microsoft Entra ID that enables organizations to define policies controlling access to resources based on contextual signals. While UMS currently does not have the required licensing for Conditional Access, Enfrasys recommends revisiting this capability in the future to enhance security posture, especially as the environment scales or external collaboration increases.

### MFA

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is enforced at the tenant level for all UMS users. MFA requires users to provide additional verification during sign-in, significantly reducing the risk of credential compromise. Enfrasys recommends maintaining tenant-wide MFA enforcement as a baseline security measure.

### External User Access

External user access is managed through Entra ID guest accounts. Access is granted on a least-privilege basis, and all external users are subject to the same MFA requirements as internal users. This approach ensures that collaboration with partners or vendors does not compromise the security of UMS's data assets.

The following table summarizes the key access design considerations and decisions:

| ID   | Description                                                                                                          | Decision                                                                                  |
|------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| AC01 | Conditional Access for Power BI, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage                          | Not implemented (pending future licensing)                                                |
| AC02 | Inclusion of UMS users in Conditional Access policy                                                                   | Not applicable at present                                                                 |
| AC03 | Enforce MFA for all UMS users                                                                                        | Implemented at tenant level                                                               |
| AC04 | External users required to use MFA                                                                                   | Implemented                                                                               |
| AC05 | Access to Microsoft Fabric environment for authorized users only                                                      | Enforced                                                                                  |
| AC06 | Private Link for Fabric resources                                                                                    | Deferred for future consideration                                                         |

## 6.4 Virtual Machine (VM) Size

Although Microsoft Fabric is a managed service that abstracts much of the underlying infrastructure, certain integration points or supporting services may require dedicated virtual machines. Enfrasys recommends the following VM specifications for any supporting workloads (e.g., data integration, custom connectors, or legacy system interfaces) that interact with the Fabric environment.

| Component                | Operating System | .NET Framework | vCPU | RAM  |
|--------------------------|------------------|----------------|------|------|
| Data Integration Gateway | Windows Server 2022 | .NET 6.0      | 4    | 16GB |
| Custom Connector Host    | Windows Server 2022 | .NET 6.0      | 2    | 8GB  |
| Legacy System Interface  | Windows Server 2019 | .NET 4.8      | 2    | 8GB  |

Enfrasys is responsible for the design and sizing of these VMs, while UMS is responsible for operational acceptance and ongoing management.

## 6.5 Resource Organization Design

A well-structured resource organization is essential for effective management, security, and cost control in the Microsoft Fabric ecosystem. Enfrasys has designed a logical hierarchy using Azure Management Groups, consistent naming conventions, and clear tagging strategies to support UMS's operational needs and governance requirements.

### Management Groups

Management Groups in Azure provide a scalable way to organize resources and apply governance controls across subscriptions. For UMS, Enfrasys recommends a tiered structure:

- **Root Management Group**: Represents the overall UMS Azure environment.
- **Production Management Group**: Contains all production workloads, including the primary Fabric resources.
- **Development/Test Management Group**: Isolates development and test environments to prevent accidental impact on production.
- **Shared Services Management Group**: Hosts cross-cutting services such as networking, monitoring, and identity.

This structure enables UMS to apply policies, RBAC, and cost controls at the appropriate scope, ensuring compliance and operational efficiency.

### Naming and Tagging

Consistent naming conventions and tagging are critical for resource discoverability, automation, and cost management. The following table outlines the recommended naming conventions for key Fabric items:

| No. | Item in Fabric   | Naming Convention Example                        |
|-----|------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Fabric Capacity  | stagingareadevcapacity01                         |
| 2   | Workspace        | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                            |
| 3   | Lakehouse        | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK / StudentAffairs_Silver_LK |
| 4   | Dataflow         | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF               |
| 5   | Semantic Model   | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM             |
| 6   | Pipeline         | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL                    |
| 7   | Report           | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report         |

### Acronyms

The following table defines key acronyms used throughout the Fabric environment:

| Acronym | Definition                |
|---------|---------------------------|
| LK      | Lakehouse                 |
| DF      | Dataflow                  |
| SM      | Semantic Model            |
| PL      | Pipeline                  |
| WS      | Workspace                 |

## 6.6 Governance Consideration

Effective governance ensures that UMS's Microsoft Fabric environment remains secure, compliant, and cost-effective. Enfrasys has defined governance disciplines, cost management practices, and violation response mechanisms to support UMS's operational and regulatory requirements.

### Governance Disciplines

The following table outlines the primary governance disciplines and their respective owners:

| Discipline          | Description                                                           | Owner              |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Policy Management   | Definition and enforcement of access, security, and compliance policies | Enfrasys Solutions |
| Resource Tagging    | Application of consistent tags for management and reporting           | UMS                |
| Cost Management     | Monitoring and optimizing resource consumption and spend               | UMS                |
| Identity Management | Administration of user and group access                               | UMS                |
| Audit & Monitoring  | Continuous monitoring and auditing of activities                      | Enfrasys Solutions |

### Cost Management

Cost management is critical to ensure that UMS maximizes the value of its investment in Microsoft Fabric while avoiding unnecessary expenditure. The following table summarizes the cost management responsibilities:

| Resource              | Cost Center        | Owner              |
|-----------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Fabric Capacity       | Data Analytics     | UMS                |
| Workspaces            | Departmental Units | UMS                |
| Dataflows/Pipelines   | IT Operations      | UMS                |
| Reports/Dashboards    | Business Units     | UMS                |

### Violation Triggers and Actions

To maintain compliance and operational integrity, Enfrasys has defined triggers and actions for governance violations:

| Trigger                                 | Action                                    | Owner              |
|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Unauthorized access attempt              | Immediate access review and remediation    | Enfrasys Solutions |
| Non-compliant resource naming/tagging    | Automated notification and correction      | UMS                |
| Cost overrun beyond defined thresholds   | Alert and cost optimization review         | UMS                |
| Policy violation (e.g., missing MFA)     | Enforcement of corrective policy           | Enfrasys Solutions |

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the design and initial implementation of governance controls, while UMS is responsible for operational acceptance, ongoing monitoring, and compliance management.

# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section details the security architecture, controls, and operational responsibilities for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric deployment. The design addresses network security, encryption, and access management, ensuring robust protection of data and resources while assigning clear responsibilities to Enfrasys Solutions and UMS.

## 7.1 Azure NSG Overview

Network Security Groups (NSGs) are a foundational security feature within Microsoft Azure, providing granular control over inbound and outbound network traffic to resources deployed within virtual networks. NSGs enable organizations to define rules that permit or deny traffic based on source, destination, port, and protocol, thereby reducing the attack surface and enforcing least-privilege access.

For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions has designed NSG rules to secure the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine, which acts as a bridge between on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric services. The outbound rules are crafted to allow only essential communication with Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, Azure core services, and authentication endpoints. This approach ensures that the gateway VM can securely authenticate, transfer data, and verify connectivity, while blocking all unnecessary traffic. Inbound rules are tightly controlled, with access typically restricted to management or monitoring traffic as required.

UMS is responsible for validating that these security controls comply with institutional and regulatory requirements. Enfrasys Solutions will maintain, review, and update the NSG configurations in accordance with Azure best practices and evolving threat landscapes.

### Inbound NSG Rules

Inbound rules are typically minimal for data gateway VMs, as these are designed to initiate outbound connections rather than receive unsolicited inbound traffic. Only explicitly required management or monitoring ports should be allowed, and all other inbound access is denied by default.

| Name                  | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination             | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|------------------------|------------------|----------|--------|
| Deny_All_Inbound      | 4000     | *                     | *            | VM Private IP Addresses| *                | *        | Deny   |

### Outbound NSG Rules

Outbound rules are tailored to permit secure communication from the OPDG VM to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, Azure authentication services, and connectivity test endpoints. These rules ensure encrypted data transfer and support operational requirements.

| Name                        | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                  | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                              | 80               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Deny_All_Other_Outbound     | 4096     | *                       | *            | *                                                                                                           | *                | *        | Deny   |

The above configuration ensures that the OPDG VM can only communicate with required Microsoft endpoints over secure protocols, minimizing exposure to external threats.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Encryption is a critical component of the overall security posture for UMS’s Microsoft Fabric deployment. Enfrasys Solutions has designed a comprehensive encryption strategy that addresses both data at rest and data in transit, ensuring that sensitive institutional data remains protected throughout its lifecycle.

### Encryption at Rest

All data stored within Microsoft Fabric, including OneLake, Power BI datasets, and intermediate storage used by the Data Gateway, is encrypted at rest using Microsoft-managed keys by default. Azure Storage and related services leverage industry-standard AES-256 encryption, providing robust protection against unauthorized access to physical storage media. For enhanced compliance, UMS may opt to implement customer-managed keys (CMK) in future phases, allowing for direct control over key lifecycle and rotation.

The On-Premises Data Gateway VM disks are also encrypted using Azure Disk Encryption, which utilizes BitLocker (for Windows) or DM-Crypt (for Linux) to secure the underlying virtual hard disks. This ensures that any data cached or temporarily stored on the gateway VM is protected against compromise, even if the VM is decommissioned or the disks are accessed outside of Azure.

### Encryption in Transit

All data flows between the On-Premises Data Gateway, Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Azure authentication services are protected using TLS 1.2 or higher. Outbound connections from the gateway VM are established exclusively over HTTPS (TCP port 443), ensuring that all data transmitted to Microsoft endpoints is encrypted end-to-end. This includes authentication tokens, dataset transfers, and service-to-service communication within Microsoft Fabric.

Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) further strengthen in-transit security by ensuring that only authenticated and authorized users can initiate connections to Microsoft Fabric resources. Although UMS’s current licensing does not permit advanced Conditional Access policies, MFA is enforced at the tenant level to mitigate risks from compromised credentials.

The following diagram illustrates the secure data flow and access control architecture for UMS’s Microsoft Fabric environment. It highlights the enforcement of Conditional Access (where available), MFA, and encrypted communication channels between users, the OPDG VM, and Microsoft cloud services.

The diagram below represents the technical architecture for secure access and data flow within the UMS Microsoft Fabric environment. It demonstrates how external users interact with Microsoft Fabric resources, with access governed by Conditional Access policies and enforced MFA, and how all communications are encrypted in transit.

![](images\slide_006_img_001.png)

Figure 7.1: Secure access and data flow architecture for Microsoft Fabric with Conditional Access and MFA.

The next diagram further details the authentication and access control flow, emphasizing the evaluation and enforcement of Entra Conditional Access policies, the gathering of user and network signals, and the secure granting of access to Microsoft Fabric components.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 7.2: Authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra Conditional Access.

### Roles and Responsibilities

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the design, implementation, and ongoing review of all security controls, including NSG configurations and encryption mechanisms. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for validating that these security measures align with institutional policies, regulatory requirements, and audit standards. Both parties shall collaborate to ensure continuous improvement and rapid response to emerging security threats.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration approach for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The strategy is structured into four key phases—Pre-Migration/Setup, Pilot, Production, and Post-Migration/Support—to ensure a seamless, secure, and well-governed transition to the new platform. Each phase details the responsibilities of Enfrasys Solutions and UMS, with clear timelines and deliverables to facilitate accountability and minimize risk.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration/Setup

The Pre-Migration/Setup phase is critical for establishing the foundation of a successful migration. During this stage, all preparatory activities are meticulously planned and executed to ensure that both the technical environment and organizational stakeholders are ready for the subsequent phases. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a collaborative approach, where both parties validate the design, confirm infrastructure readiness, and establish secure connectivity.

Key preparatory activities include the delivery and review of the solution design document, confirmation of the new server environment for the staging area database, setup of the Microsoft Fabric environment, and installation of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) within the designated virtual machine. These steps are essential to guarantee data integrity, compliance, and operational readiness. By engaging UMS stakeholders early in the process, potential risks and gaps can be proactively identified and addressed, thereby reducing the likelihood of downstream issues during migration.

The following table details the planned activities, responsible parties, and current status for the Pre-Migration/Setup phase:

| No | Activity                                                      | Planned Start   | Action By           | Status       | Remarks |
|----|---------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|---------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1  | Deliver Design Document to be reviewed by UMS & Sign Off      | Fri, 7-Nov-25   | Enfrasys, UMS       | Not-Started  |         |
| 2  | Confirmation on the new server (data warehouse copy – Staging Area DB) to be ingested | Mon, 10-Nov-25   | UMS                 | Not-Started  |         |
| 3  | Fabric Environment Setup                                      | Wed, 12-Nov-25  | Enfrasys            | Not-Started  |         |
| 4  | Install On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in VM to establish Data Gateway | Mon, 17-Nov-25  | Enfrasys, UMS       | Not-Started  |         |

These activities are scheduled to be completed prior to the pilot phase, ensuring that the technical and operational prerequisites are met.

## 8.2 Pilot

The Pilot phase serves as a controlled validation of the migration strategy, processes, and technologies before full-scale production deployment. During this phase, a subset of data and workloads are migrated and tested to ensure that the new environment meets UMS’s operational, security, and performance requirements. The pilot is essential for identifying unforeseen issues, validating migration tools, and refining cutover strategies.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends that the pilot scope include representative datasets, critical business reports, and integration points with existing systems. Success criteria for the pilot are defined as follows: (1) successful data ingestion and synchronization, (2) seamless access to migrated data via Microsoft Fabric, (3) no critical errors or data loss, and (4) positive user acceptance testing feedback from UMS stakeholders. The pilot timeline is structured to allow for iterative testing, issue resolution, and sign-off before progressing to production.

The pilot phase is planned as follows:

| Item       | Activities                                         | Action By      | Status       |
|------------|----------------------------------------------------|---------------|--------------|
| D-7        | Finalize pilot migration plan and test scripts      | Enfrasys      | Not-Started  |
| D-3        | Conduct pilot migration dry-run                     | Enfrasys      | Not-Started  |
| D0         | Execute pilot migration and validate outcomes       | Enfrasys, UMS | Not-Started  |
| D+1        | Collect feedback, resolve issues, and sign-off      | UMS           | Not-Started  |

By executing the pilot in this structured manner, UMS can confidently proceed to full production migration with minimized risk.

## 8.3 Production

The Production phase encompasses the full-scale migration of data, workloads, and user access to the new Microsoft Fabric environment. This phase is executed based on the learnings and refinements from the pilot, ensuring that all technical, operational, and security requirements are met. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a phased cutover approach, where critical systems are migrated during designated maintenance windows to minimize business disruption.

Risk mitigation strategies include comprehensive backup and rollback procedures, real-time monitoring, and the presence of both Enfrasys and UMS technical teams during the cutover. The production migration timeline is carefully orchestrated to allow for pre-migration validation, live cutover, and immediate post-migration verification.

The production migration plan is detailed below:

| Item       | Activities                                            | Action By      | Status       |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------|--------------|
| D-7        | Communicate cutover schedule to stakeholders         | UMS           | Not-Started  |
| D-3        | Final pre-migration data backup                      | UMS           | Not-Started  |
| D0         | Execute production migration and cutover             | Enfrasys      | Not-Started  |
| D+1        | Post-migration verification and business validation  | Enfrasys, UMS | Not-Started  |

This structured approach ensures that UMS’s transition to the new platform is both secure and efficient, with minimal operational impact.

## 8.4 Post-Migration/Support

Following the production migration, the Post-Migration/Support phase provides UMS with ongoing support, knowledge transfer, and escalation pathways to ensure operational stability and user confidence. Enfrasys Solutions will deliver a structured support model, including hypercare support, documentation handover, and training sessions for UMS administrators and end-users.

The support model includes defined escalation paths for technical issues, with Enfrasys providing Level 2/3 support and UMS IT handling Level 1 user queries. A formal handover process will be conducted, ensuring that all operational documentation, runbooks, and support contacts are clearly communicated. This approach empowers UMS to maintain and operate the new environment independently while retaining access to expert assistance as needed.

The post-migration support plan is outlined below:

| Item       | Activities                                             | Action By      | Status       |
|------------|-------------------------------------------------------|---------------|--------------|
| D+1        | Initiate hypercare support period                     | Enfrasys      | Not-Started  |
| D+3        | Conduct knowledge transfer and documentation handover | Enfrasys      | Not-Started  |
| D+5        | Transition to standard support and closeout           | UMS           | Not-Started  |

Through this comprehensive support framework, UMS is assured of a smooth transition and sustained operational excellence post-migration.

# 9.0 Appendix

## 9.1 Appendix 1 Computing

### Introduction/Prerequisites

Azure Compute services provide scalable, on-demand computing resources for a variety of workloads. Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) must ensure that all prerequisites, such as appropriate Azure subscriptions, resource group configurations, and role-based access control (RBAC) assignments, are in place before deploying compute resources.

### Limits and Boundaries

Azure imposes certain limits and boundaries on compute resources, including virtual machine (VM) quotas, regional availability, and maximum resource allocations per subscription. UMS should review current Azure documentation to ensure planned deployments remain within these boundaries.

### Others

Azure Compute Overview: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/)

Azure Virtual Machine Quotas: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/azure-subscription-service-limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/azure-subscription-service-limits)

Azure VM Sizes: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/sizes](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/sizes)

## 9.2 Appendix 2 Network

### Introduction/Prerequisites

Azure Networking services enable secure, reliable connectivity for cloud resources. UMS must configure virtual networks, subnets, and network security groups in accordance with best practices and organizational security policies.

### Limits and Boundaries

Networking resources in Azure are subject to limits such as the number of virtual networks per region, subnet address space, and NSG rule quotas. UMS should validate network architecture against these limits to avoid deployment issues.

### Others

Azure Virtual Network Documentation: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-overview)

Azure Networking Limits: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/azure-subscription-service-limits#networking-limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/azure-subscription-service-limits#networking-limits)

Network Security Groups Overview: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/network-security-groups-overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/network-security-groups-overview)

## 9.3 Appendix 3 Identity & Security

### Introduction/Prerequisites

Azure identity and security services, including Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), provide authentication, authorization, and policy enforcement for cloud resources. UMS must ensure correct configuration of identity providers, user roles, and security settings.

### Limits and Boundaries

Identity and security services have specific boundaries, such as the number of directory objects, application registrations, and conditional access policies. UMS should monitor these limits to ensure compliance and operational integrity.

### Others

Microsoft Entra ID Overview: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/fundamentals/active-directory-whatis](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/fundamentals/active-directory-whatis)

Azure Security Documentation: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/)

Azure AD Service Limits: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/enterprise-users/directory-service-limits-restrictions](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/enterprise-users/directory-service-limits-restrictions)

## 9.4 Appendix 4 Logging & Monitoring

### Introduction/Prerequisites

Azure provides comprehensive logging and monitoring services through Azure Monitor, Log Analytics, and related tools. UMS must configure diagnostic settings, log retention policies, and alerting mechanisms to ensure effective operational monitoring.

### Limits and Boundaries

Logging and monitoring services have limits regarding data ingestion rates, retention periods, and query performance. UMS should design monitoring solutions within these constraints to maintain system performance and cost-effectiveness.

### Others

Azure Monitor Documentation: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/overview)

Log Analytics Limits: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/logs/log-analytics-workspace-limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/logs/log-analytics-workspace-limits)

Azure Monitoring Best Practices: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/best-practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/best-practices)

## 9.5 Appendix 5 Cloud Governance

### Introduction/Prerequisites

Cloud governance in Azure encompasses policy management, resource organization, and cost control. UMS must establish governance frameworks, including management groups, policies, and tagging standards, to ensure compliance and operational efficiency.

### Limits and Boundaries

Azure governance features are subject to limits on the number of management groups, policy assignments, and resource locks. UMS should regularly review these limits to avoid governance bottlenecks.

### Others

Azure Governance Overview: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/)

Azure Policy Documentation: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/policy/overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/policy/overview)

Management Groups Limits: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/management-groups/overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/management-groups/overview)

## 9.6 Appendix 6 Microsoft Fabric-specific

### Introduction/Prerequisites

Microsoft Fabric is an end-to-end analytics platform that integrates data engineering, data warehousing, data science, and business intelligence. UMS must provision appropriate Fabric capacities, configure workspaces, and assign roles before utilizing Fabric services.

### Limits and Boundaries

Microsoft Fabric imposes limits on workspace capacities, dataflows, dataset sizes, and concurrent operations. UMS should consult the latest Microsoft Fabric documentation to ensure solution scalability and compliance.

### Others

Microsoft Fabric Documentation: [https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/)

The diagram illustrates a technical architecture involving Azure components and user access management. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This represents a Resource Group in Azure, which is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It is depicted with an icon suggesting a fabric or mesh, indicating a structured environment for resource management.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely refers to a User Management System within the Azure environment, possibly utilizing Azure Fabric for scalable and reliable service deployment. It is part of the Fabric-RG, suggesting it is a resource managed within this group.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature in Azure Active Directory that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It is represented by a shield with a lock, indicating security measures to protect resources.

4. **External User**: This component represents users outside the organization who need access to resources within the Azure environment. The diagram shows a connection from the UMS Fabric to the External User, indicating that user access is managed and controlled through the UMS Fabric and subject to Conditional Access policies.

The data flow suggests that external users interact with the UMS Fabric, which is governed by Conditional Access policies to ensure secure and compliant access to resources within the Fabric-RG.

The diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated against Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   - **Signal**: Various signals are gathered, such as user identity and network location. Examples include users and groups, and network location.
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access permissions.
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, determining whether access is granted or denied.

3. **Inbound Access to Microsoft Fabric**: If access is granted, the user is allowed inbound access to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: The diagram shows integration with various Microsoft Fabric components, including OneLake, which is likely a data storage or management service within the ecosystem.

This architecture ensures secure and conditional access to Microsoft Fabric resources, leveraging Entra's capabilities to evaluate and enforce access policies based on real-time signals and decisions.

The diagram represents a data processing and analytics architecture within the UMS Fabric environment. It begins with data ingestion from a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, labeled as "UMS – Staging Area." The data is ingested using Azure Data Factory, which orchestrates the movement into the XX_Bronze_LK Tables, serving as the initial landing zone for raw data.

The data then progresses to the XX_Silver_LK Tables, indicating a transformation stage where data is cleaned and enriched. This stage is also managed by Data Factory, ensuring seamless data flow and transformation.

Subsequently, the data is processed through a Data Science component, which likely involves advanced analytics, machine learning, or statistical operations to derive insights. This component is integrated with Data Factory to facilitate automated workflows.

The processed data is stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables, representing the final, refined dataset ready for consumption. An Identity_SM component is involved, possibly for security, identity management, or metadata handling.

Finally, the data is connected via OneLake to Power BI, enabling visualization and business intelligence reporting. This connection allows stakeholders to interact with and analyze the data through Power BI dashboards and reports.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components:

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse**: This component represents the on-premises data storage system where data is initially staged. It is likely used for storing and processing large volumes of data before it is transferred or utilized by other systems.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**: This gateway serves as a bridge between the on-premises data warehouse and external systems. It facilitates secure data transfer without requiring inbound ports, ensuring that data can be sent out securely while minimizing security risks associated with open ports.

3. **UMS Fabric**: This component is likely a cloud-based service or platform that receives data from the on-premises data gateway. The connection is described as an outbound secure connection, indicating that data is pushed from the on-premises environment to the UMS Fabric, which could be used for further processing, analytics, or integration with other cloud services.

The data flow begins at the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, moves through the On-Premise Data Gateway, and is securely transmitted to the UMS Fabric for further use or analysis.

The diagram represents a data processing and machine learning workflow within an Azure environment, utilizing UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and integration.

1. **Data Source**: 
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, containing pre-processed data ready for machine learning experiments.

2. **Machine Learning Processes**:
   - **AutoML**: 
     - **AutoML Experiment**: Data from XX_Silver_LK Tables is fed into an automated machine learning experiment. This process involves using Azure's AutoML capabilities to automatically select the best model and hyperparameters.
     - **Result Scoring (Using Notebook)**: The results from the AutoML experiment are scored using a notebook, which likely involves evaluating model performance metrics.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**:
     - **Modelling (Using Notebook)**: Alternatively, data from XX_Silver_LK Tables can be used for manual machine learning modeling. This involves custom model development and training using notebooks.

3. **Data Storage and Integration**:
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The output from both AutoML and manual machine learning processes is stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables, which represent refined and processed data ready for further analysis or reporting.
   - **Identity_SM**: This component is likely involved in managing identities or metadata associated with the data in XX_Gold_LK Tables.

4. **Data Visualization and Reporting**:
   - **Power BI**: The data stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables is connected to Power BI via OneLake, enabling visualization and reporting. This connection facilitates the creation of dashboards and reports for business intelligence purposes.

5. **Integration Framework**:
   - **UMS Fabric**: This framework encompasses the entire workflow, providing integration and management capabilities across the different components and processes.
   - **OneLake**: Acts as a data lake solution, facilitating seamless data storage and access across the workflow, ensuring efficient data management and integration with Power BI.

The diagram illustrates a comprehensive data processing and machine learning architecture, leveraging Azure's capabilities for automated and manual model development, data storage, and business intelligence reporting.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.
