# UMS Power BI & Fabric Solution Design

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a concise overview of the project objectives, the purpose of this Solution Design Document, and the intended audience for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Power BI and Microsoft Fabric implementation.

## 2.1 Project Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) is embarking on a strategic initiative to modernize its data analytics capabilities by leveraging Microsoft Power BI and Microsoft Fabric. The primary business objective is to establish a robust, scalable, and secure data platform that enables end-to-end data workflows—from ingestion through transformation to advanced analytics and visualization. This project is scoped to design and implement a comprehensive data workflow within Microsoft Fabric, tailored to UMS’s unique business requirements and academic use cases.

Key components of the project include the establishment of a dedicated Fabric workspace, the development of an end-to-end ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipeline, the integration of machine learning processes, and the seamless incorporation of Power BI for actionable analytics. The solution leverages Microsoft Fabric services such as Fabric Data Factory for data integration and orchestration, Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, and Data Science capabilities for machine learning applications. By aligning UMS’s cloud adoption strategy with Microsoft Fabric’s latest capabilities, the project aims to empower data-driven decision-making, improve operational efficiency, and support innovative research and academic outcomes.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

The purpose of this Solution Design Document is to provide a comprehensive, authoritative blueprint for the design, implementation, and governance of the Power BI and Microsoft Fabric solution at UMS. This document details the architectural principles, design decisions, technical specifications, security considerations, and operational workflows that will guide the project from inception through deployment and ongoing management. It serves as the primary reference for all stakeholders, ensuring alignment on objectives, scope, and responsibilities throughout the project lifecycle. The document is intended to facilitate informed decision-making, support risk mitigation, and provide a clear roadmap for successful solution delivery.

## 2.3 Document Audience

This Solution Design Document is intended for a broad audience of stakeholders involved in the planning, implementation, and ongoing management of UMS’s Power BI and Microsoft Fabric environment. The primary readers include:

- UMS IT Department (infrastructure, security, and operations teams)
- UMS Data Analytics and Business Intelligence Teams
- UMS Academic and Research Stakeholders
- Enfrasys Solutions Project Team (consultants, architects, and technical specialists)

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the drafting and technical accuracy of this document. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing the content, providing feedback, and formally approving the final design. This collaborative approach ensures that the solution is tailored to UMS’s requirements and is positioned for long-term success.

# 3.0 Microsoft Power BI & Fabric Overview

This section provides a comprehensive overview of Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Microsoft Entra ID, establishing the foundational technologies that underpin the analytics platform designed for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS).

## 3.1 Microsoft Fabric

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated analytics platform that unifies data movement, data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence within a single SaaS environment. Fabric is designed to simplify and accelerate the data lifecycle, from ingestion to transformation, analysis, and visualization, leveraging a set of core services that operate cohesively to deliver enterprise-grade analytics.

The core services of Microsoft Fabric include:

- **Data Factory**: This service provides robust data integration and orchestration capabilities, enabling organizations to ingest data from diverse sources, transform it through ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes, and orchestrate complex data workflows. Data Factory ensures that data is reliably and securely moved across systems, supporting both batch and real-time ingestion scenarios.

- **Synapse**: Fabric Synapse delivers advanced data analytics and big data processing capabilities. It combines data warehousing, big data analytics, and data integration into a unified experience, allowing users to query and analyze data at scale using familiar SQL, Spark, and Data Explorer engines. This enables UMS to derive actionable insights from large and complex datasets efficiently.

- **Data Science**: The Data Science service in Fabric empowers organizations to build, train, and deploy machine learning models directly within the platform. It supports AutoML workflows, custom model development, and integration with operational data, facilitating predictive analytics and intelligent automation for business scenarios.

- **OneLake**: OneLake serves as the unified data lake for Microsoft Fabric, providing a single, secure, and scalable storage layer for all organizational data. It enables seamless data sharing, governance, and collaboration across analytics workloads, ensuring data consistency and accessibility.

By leveraging Microsoft Fabric, Universiti Malaysia Sabah can streamline its data workflow—from ingestion to transformation and advanced analytics—within a secure, governed, and highly integrated environment. This approach accelerates time-to-insight, enhances data-driven decision-making, and aligns with modern cloud adoption strategies.

## 3.2 Power BI

Power BI is Microsoft’s enterprise-grade business intelligence (BI) platform that enables organizations to visualize, analyze, and share data insights across the enterprise. Power BI provides interactive dashboards, rich data visualizations, and self-service analytics capabilities, empowering users at all levels to make informed decisions based on real-time data.

Power BI’s integration with Microsoft Fabric is seamless, enabling data prepared and transformed within Fabric’s Data Factory, Synapse, and Data Science services to be directly consumed and visualized in Power BI. This tight integration ensures that data lineage, security, and governance policies established in Fabric are consistently enforced within Power BI reports and dashboards.

For Universiti Malaysia Sabah, Power BI serves as the primary interface for business-aligned analytics, enabling stakeholders to explore data, monitor key metrics, and collaborate on insights. The platform supports a wide array of data sources and offers robust sharing and collaboration features, ensuring that analytics are accessible, actionable, and secure.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for providing detailed technology explanations and ensuring that Power BI is configured to maximize its integration with Fabric. UMS is responsible for reviewing the contextual fit of Power BI capabilities with its internal analytics and reporting requirements.

## 3.3 Microsoft Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID, formerly known as Azure Active Directory (Azure AD), is Microsoft’s cloud-based identity and access management (IAM) service. Entra ID provides secure authentication, authorization, and identity governance for users, devices, and applications across cloud and on-premises environments.

Entra ID plays a critical role in the Microsoft Fabric and Power BI ecosystem by enabling single sign-on (SSO), multi-factor authentication (MFA), and conditional access policies. It ensures that only authorized users can access sensitive data and analytics resources, supporting compliance with organizational and regulatory requirements.

Within the context of Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s analytics platform, Entra ID will be used to manage user identities, assign access roles, and enforce security policies across Fabric workspaces and Power BI assets. This centralized approach to identity management enhances security, simplifies user onboarding and offboarding, and provides comprehensive audit trails for access activities.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for articulating the technical foundations and benefits of Microsoft Entra ID, while Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing and aligning identity management practices with institutional policies and compliance needs.

# 4.0 Network Design and Decision

This section outlines the network architecture and design decisions for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) in support of Microsoft Fabric deployment. The design ensures secure, reliable connectivity between on-premises resources and the Microsoft cloud, with a focus on secure data flows, robust gateway deployment, and compliance with industry best practices.

## 4.1 Network Connectivity Overview

A secure and resilient network architecture is paramount for integrating on-premises data sources with Microsoft Fabric in the cloud. The overall topology for UMS leverages a hybrid connectivity model, where on-premises resources, such as the UMS Staging Area and SQL Server Data Warehouse, securely connect to Microsoft Fabric services through a dedicated On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). This design eliminates the need for inbound firewall rules, significantly reducing the attack surface and adhering to zero-trust networking principles.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends that all data flows from the UMS on-premises environment to Microsoft Fabric be routed exclusively via outbound connections. This approach ensures that sensitive data remains protected during transit and that only authorized communication is permitted. Outbound-only connectivity leverages SSL/TLS encryption, ensuring confidentiality and integrity for all data exchanges between UMS and Microsoft cloud services.

The network design also incorporates Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) to enforce granular outbound rules. These rules are crafted to allow only the required protocols and destinations, such as Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints, while blocking all unnecessary traffic. This layered security model not only protects UMS assets but also simplifies compliance with institutional and regulatory requirements.

The following diagram illustrates the high-level data flow and network connectivity between UMS’s on-premises data warehouse, the On-Premises Data Gateway, and Microsoft Fabric:

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 1: Secure outbound data flow from UMS on-premises SQL Server to Microsoft Fabric via the On-Premises Data Gateway.

## 4.2 Microsoft Fabric Data Gateway

Microsoft Fabric On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a critical component that enables secure, seamless data transfer from UMS’s on-premises infrastructure to Microsoft Fabric cloud services. The gateway acts as a bridge, facilitating encrypted outbound communication without exposing internal resources to inbound internet traffic. This architecture is essential for maintaining security and operational continuity.

**Microsoft Fabric On-Premises Data Gateway** is a Microsoft-managed service that allows organizations to securely connect on-premises data sources to cloud-based services such as Power BI, Microsoft Fabric, and Azure Logic Apps. The gateway operates by initiating outbound connections to Microsoft cloud endpoints, ensuring that no inbound ports need to be opened on the organization’s firewall. This design significantly reduces security risks and simplifies network management.

For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions will architect and design the network configuration for the OPDG deployment, ensuring compliance with Microsoft and industry best practices. UMS is responsible for provisioning the on-premises virtual machine (VM) that will host the OPDG agent, as well as ensuring that the VM meets all required specifications for performance and reliability.

The OPDG VM will be configured to allow only the necessary outbound traffic to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints. All communications will utilize HTTPS (TCP port 443) for secure data transfer and authentication, with additional outbound rules to support connectivity verification. This ensures that UMS data remains protected throughout its lifecycle and that the gateway operates reliably under varying network conditions.

The following table details the recommended specifications for the OPDG VM to ensure optimal performance and compatibility with Microsoft Fabric workloads:

| Component        | Specification                                 |
|------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework   | Version 4.8                                   |
| vCPU             | 8 cores                                       |
| RAM              | Minimum 8 GB                                  |

Table 1: On-Premises Data Gateway VM Specifications

### Outbound-Only Secure Connectivity

The OPDG VM is configured with a strict set of outbound rules to ensure secure connectivity to Microsoft Fabric and related services. No inbound ports are required, which significantly enhances security by preventing unsolicited external access to the gateway or internal network. This outbound-only model leverages SSL/TLS encryption for all communications, ensuring data confidentiality and integrity.

The following table summarizes the Azure Network Security Group (NSG) outbound rules that must be implemented for the OPDG VM:

| Name                        | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                      | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                   | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

Table 2: Outbound NSG Rules for OPDG VM

Enfrasys Solutions will be responsible for designing and documenting these outbound security rules. UMS shall implement these rules on the on-premises network firewall or virtual network appliances as required, ensuring that only authorized outbound traffic is permitted.

This network design provides UMS with a secure, scalable, and manageable foundation for integrating on-premises data sources with Microsoft Fabric, supporting current analytics needs and future growth.

# 5.0 Roles in Microsoft Power BI & Fabric

This section details the roles, responsibilities, and access assignments for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) within the Microsoft Power BI and Fabric environment. The following subsections define the administrator role, describe native workspace roles, and map UMS personnel to their designated responsibilities to ensure secure and effective platform governance.

## 5.1 Power BI & Fabric Administrator Role

Microsoft Power BI and Microsoft Fabric are integrated analytics platforms designed to enable organizations to visualize data, build robust business intelligence solutions, and manage data workflows securely. The administrator role within these platforms is critical for ensuring operational integrity, compliance, and effective user management.

The Power BI & Fabric Administrator is entrusted with comprehensive privileges, including tenant-level configuration, workspace management, user provisioning, and security policy enforcement. Administrators are responsible for setting up and maintaining workspace structures, controlling access permissions, monitoring usage and auditing activities, and ensuring that data governance standards are upheld. They are also tasked with integrating Power BI and Fabric with other Microsoft services, managing capacity, and troubleshooting platform issues.

By centralizing these responsibilities, UMS ensures that platform operations remain consistent, secure, and aligned with institutional policies. Enfrasys Solutions recommends that only trusted personnel with deep knowledge of data governance and analytics be assigned administrator privileges, as this role has the authority to make changes that affect all users and data assets within the environment.

### Administrator Capabilities

The table below outlines the key capabilities associated with the Power BI & Fabric Administrator role:

| Capability                   | Description                                                                                 |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Tenant Configuration         | Manage organization-wide settings, including security, compliance, and integration options. |
| Workspace Management         | Create, delete, and configure workspaces; assign roles to users.                            |
| User Provisioning            | Add or remove users, assign roles, and manage group memberships.                            |
| Security Policy Enforcement  | Define and enforce data access, sharing, and retention policies.                            |
| Monitoring & Auditing        | Access audit logs, usage metrics, and monitor platform health.                              |
| Integration Management       | Configure connections to data sources and other Microsoft services.                         |
| Capacity & Resource Control  | Allocate and monitor resource usage and workspace capacities.                               |
| Troubleshooting & Support    | Resolve platform issues and provide user support.                                           |

## 5.2 Power BI & Fabric-native Roles

Microsoft Power BI and Fabric provide a set of native roles that govern user access and permissions within workspaces. These roles are designed to enforce the principle of least privilege, ensuring that users have only the access necessary to perform their designated tasks. The primary native roles are Workspace Admin, Contributor, and Viewer.

- **Workspace Admin**: Holds full control over the workspace, including managing datasets, reports, permissions, and workspace settings. This role is typically assigned to data engineers, platform administrators, or those responsible for overall workspace governance.
- **Workspace Contributor**: Can create, edit, and delete content within the workspace but cannot manage workspace settings or assign roles. This role suits data engineers and business users who develop and maintain analytics solutions.
- **Workspace Viewer**: Has read-only access to reports, dashboards, and datasets. This role is ideal for stakeholders, analysts, and project directors who consume analytics content but do not require editing permissions.

Assigning these roles appropriately ensures that UMS personnel can collaborate effectively while maintaining data security and operational control.

### Workspace-level Role Capabilities

The following table summarizes the permissions associated with each workspace-level role in Power BI and Fabric:

| Permission                | Admin | Contributor | Member | Viewer |
|---------------------------|:-----:|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|
| Manage workspace settings |   ✔   |             |        |        |
| Add/remove users          |   ✔   |             |        |        |
| Publish content           |   ✔   |     ✔       |   ✔    |        |
| Edit content              |   ✔   |     ✔       |   ✔    |        |
| View content              |   ✔   |     ✔       |   ✔    |   ✔    |
| Delete content            |   ✔   |     ✔       |   ✔    |        |

## 5.3 UMS Workspace Role and Access

Effective role mapping is essential to ensure that Universiti Malaysia Sabah's personnel have the appropriate access required for their responsibilities within the Power BI and Fabric environment. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for establishing and mapping these roles based on UMS's operational requirements, while UMS is accountable for assigning specific personnel to each role and validating these assignments.

This collaborative approach ensures that access rights are both technically sound and aligned with UMS's internal governance policies. The mapping process involves reviewing job functions, data sensitivity, and operational needs to minimize risk and maximize productivity.

### Client Workspace Role Mapping

The following table presents the mapping of UMS personnel to their respective workspace roles and suggested responsibilities. Enfrasys Solutions will manage the initial mapping and configuration, while UMS will assign and validate personnel as listed.

| No. | Workspace Role      | Suggested Role                  | UMS PIC                                                                                          |

| 3   | Workspace Viewer   | Data Analyst / Stakeholder / Project Director | Director<br>Dr Farashazillah Binti Yahya<br>Stakeholders<br>Roslee Bin Talip<br>Dayangku Rozlina BTE PG. Aliuddin<br>Norhanieyati Binti Norhaizat<br>Zaidie @ Mohd Zaidie Bin Adilai<br>Professor Madya TS Dr Mohd Hanafi Bin Ahmad Hijazi |

This structured allocation of roles and responsibilities ensures that UMS maintains strong governance, minimizes the risk of unauthorized access, and supports efficient collaboration across analytics initiatives. Enfrasys Solutions will continue to support UMS in reviewing and updating these assignments as organizational needs evolve.

# 6.0 Microsoft Power BI & Fabric Design and Decision

This section details the end-to-end architecture, design considerations, and governance for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) adoption of Microsoft Power BI and Microsoft Fabric. The design leverages best practices in data engineering, security, and operational governance to ensure robust, scalable, and secure analytics capabilities.

## 6.1 Data Workflow Design Considerations

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated analytics platform that combines data engineering, data science, data warehousing, real-time analytics, and business intelligence into a unified SaaS experience. Power BI is Microsoft’s enterprise-grade business analytics service, enabling organizations to visualize data, share insights, and drive informed decision-making.

For UMS, the data workflow is architected to support a seamless flow from data ingestion to advanced analytics and visualization. Data is ingested from on-premises sources (such as Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse) using Azure Data Factory pipelines. The workflow is structured using the Lakehouse paradigm, with distinct Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers to manage raw, cleansed, and transformed data respectively. This layered approach ensures data quality, traceability, and efficient processing for both analytics and machine learning workloads.

The integration of machine learning is achieved via Fabric Notebooks and AutoML, enabling both automated and manual model development. The final data products are exposed through Power BI, leveraging OneLake for high-performance, direct data access. This architecture provides UMS with a future-proof, scalable analytics foundation that supports both operational reporting and advanced data science use cases.

### Data Workflow Considerations Table

| ID   | Description                                                                                                         | Workload Type    |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| DC01 | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                                | Data Factory     |
| DC02 | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                               | Data Engineering |
| DC03 | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse.                                    | Data Engineering |
| DC04 | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse.                                   | Data Engineering |
| DC05 | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering |
| DC06 | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold).         | Data Factory     |
| DC07 | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse | Data Science     |
| DC08 | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.                        | Power BI         |
| DC09 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                             | Power BI         |

The workflow ensures data lineage, quality, and optimized performance for both reporting and AI-driven insights.

The following diagram illustrates the data flow architecture from ingestion to analytics and visualization within the UMS Fabric environment. Data is ingested from the on-premises staging area, processed through the Lakehouse layers, and made available for both machine learning and Power BI reporting.

![](images\slide_009_img_003.png)

Figure 1: UMS Fabric Data Workflow from Ingestion to Analytics.

## 6.2 Data Workflow Design Decisions

A robust data workflow is critical for ensuring data integrity, auditability, and scalability. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a multi-layered Lakehouse approach, leveraging Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers to progressively refine data for downstream analytics and machine learning. This design supports modularity, simplifies troubleshooting, and enables UMS to adopt advanced analytics with minimal friction.

ETL processes are orchestrated using Azure Data Factory and Dataflow Gen2, ensuring automated and reliable data movement. Machine learning workloads are integrated via both AutoML and manual notebook-driven processes, with model outputs stored in the Gold Lakehouse for consistency and reuse. This approach not only accelerates time-to-value for analytics but also supports governance and future extensibility.

### Data Workflow Design Decisions Table

| No.  | Design Decision                                                                                                    | Decision                                                                                      |
|------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Adopt a multi-layer Lakehouse architecture (Bronze/Silver/Gold)                                                    | Implemented to ensure data quality, traceability, and modularity                              |
| DD02 | Use Azure Data Factory and Dataflow Gen2 for ETL and data movement                                                 | Selected for orchestration, automation, and integration with Microsoft Fabric                  |
| DD03 | Integrate both AutoML and manual ML workflows using Fabric Notebooks                                               | Enables flexibility in model development and supports both automated and custom ML processes   |
| DD04 | Store all cleansed and modeled data in the Gold Lakehouse                                                          | Ensures a single source of truth for analytics and reporting                                  |
| DD05 | Expose data to Power BI via OneLake and Semantic Models                                                            | Provides high-performance, real-time analytics and visualization capabilities                  |
| DD06 | Register trained models in the Fabric Model Registry                                                               | Supports model lifecycle management, versioning, and reuse                                    |

The following diagram details the AutoML and manual machine learning workflow integration within the UMS Fabric ecosystem, highlighting the flow from Silver Lakehouse to model scoring and storage in the Gold Lakehouse, and subsequent exposure to Power BI.

![](images\slide_021_img_005.png)

Figure 2: Machine Learning Workflow Integration in UMS Fabric.

## 6.3 Access Design Considerations and Decisions

Securing access to analytics resources is paramount in any enterprise deployment. Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) provides identity and access management, while Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) add critical layers of security.

For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions has assessed the eligibility for Conditional Access policies and determined that, due to current licensing constraints, full Conditional Access cannot be enforced. Instead, MFA is implemented at the tenant level to ensure strong authentication for all users accessing Power BI and Fabric resources. This approach balances security with operational feasibility, and can be revisited as UMS’s licensing posture evolves.

The following diagrams illustrate the technical architecture for access management and the authentication flow using Entra ID and Conditional Access policies.

The first diagram depicts the resource group structure, user access management, and the application of Conditional Access policies for external users.

![](images\slide_006_img_001.png)

Figure 3: Access Control Architecture with Conditional Access.

The second diagram details the authentication flow, from user sign-in through Entra Conditional Access evaluation to access enforcement within Microsoft Fabric.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 4: Authentication and Conditional Access Flow for Microsoft Fabric.

### 6.3.1 Conditional Access

Conditional Access is a policy-based approach to securing access to cloud resources, evaluating user, device, and location signals to enforce access controls. While UMS’s current licensing does not permit full Conditional Access, Enfrasys Solutions recommends revisiting this capability as the institution’s security requirements and licensing evolve.

| No.  | Conditional Access                                                                                                 |
|------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                  |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts                              |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                |

### 6.3.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

MFA is enforced at the tenant level to provide an additional layer of security. All users must complete a secondary verification step, significantly reducing the risk of unauthorized access due to credential compromise.

### 6.3.3 Entra ID (Azure Active Directory)

Microsoft Entra ID is the backbone of identity management for UMS’s analytics environment. It centralizes user authentication, supports role-based access control, and integrates with both Conditional Access and MFA to deliver a secure, compliant authentication experience.

## 6.4 Virtual Machine (VM) Size for Data Gateway

The On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a critical component that bridges on-premises data sources with cloud analytics services. Enfrasys Solutions recommends deploying the gateway on a dedicated virtual machine with specifications that ensure reliability and performance for data transfer operations.

The following table summarizes the recommended VM specifications for the Data Gateway:

| Component        | Specification                                   |
|------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework   | Version 4.8                                     |
| vCPU             | 8 cores                                         |
| RAM              | Minimum 8 GB                                    |

This configuration ensures sufficient compute and memory resources for concurrent data refreshes and high-throughput scenarios.

The diagram below illustrates the secure data flow from the UMS on-premises SQL Server Data Warehouse through the On-Premises Data Gateway to the UMS Fabric environment, emphasizing outbound-only secure connections for minimized attack surface.

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 5: Secure Data Gateway Architecture for UMS Fabric Integration.

## 6.5 Resource Organization Design

Effective resource organization is foundational for governance, cost management, and operational efficiency in cloud analytics deployments. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a hierarchical management group structure, clear naming conventions, and standardized tagging to facilitate resource discovery, automation, and chargeback.

### 6.5.1 Management Groups

Management groups in Azure provide a logical hierarchy for organizing subscriptions, resource groups, and resources. For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions proposes a structure that aligns with organizational units (e.g., faculties, departments) and project environments (e.g., development, production). This hierarchy enables policy inheritance, centralized governance, and streamlined access management.

### 6.5.2 Naming and Tagging Conventions

Consistent naming and tagging are critical for managing large-scale analytics environments. The following tables define the recommended conventions for Azure and Microsoft Fabric resources, ensuring clarity, uniqueness, and traceability across all components.

#### Azure Naming Convention Table

| No. | Item           | Naming Convention                   |
|-----|----------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1   | Resource Group | <Project>-<Env>-RG                  |
| 2   | VM             | <Project>-<Env>-VM                  |
| 3   | Data Gateway   | <Project>-<Env>-DG                  |

#### Fabric Naming Convention Table

| No. | Item in Fabric   | Naming                                      |
|-----|------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Fabric Capacity  | stagingareadevcapacity01                     |
| 2   | Workspace        | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                        |
| 3   | Lakehouse        | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_LK<br>Etc. |
| 4   | Dataflow         | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_to_Gold_DF<br>Etc. |
| 5   | Semantic Model   | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM         |
| 6   | Pipeline         | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL<br>Etc.        |
| 7   | Report           | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report     |

#### Acronyms Table

| Acronym | Definition                  |
|---------|-----------------------------|
| RG      | Resource Group              |
| VM      | Virtual Machine             |
| DG      | Data Gateway                |
| LK      | Lakehouse                   |
| DF      | Dataflow                    |
| SM      | Semantic Model              |
| PL      | Pipeline                    |
| WS      | Workspace                   |

## 6.6 Governance Consideration

Strong governance ensures that analytics resources are used efficiently, securely, and in alignment with UMS’s strategic objectives. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the technical design and implementation of governance controls, while UMS provides operational input and ongoing oversight.

### 6.6.1 Governance Disciplines

Cost management, resource ownership, and policy enforcement are key governance pillars. The following table outlines the assignment of resources, cost centers, and owners to ensure accountability and transparency.

| Resource           | Cost Center     | Owner                    |
|--------------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Fabric Capacity    | Analytics      | UMS IT                   |
| Workspace          | Departmental   | UMS Department Leads     |
| Data Gateway       | Infrastructure | UMS IT                   |
| Lakehouse          | Analytics      | UMS Data Engineering     |
| Power BI Reports   | Departmental   | UMS Department Leads     |

### 6.6.2 Violation Triggers and Actions

To maintain compliance and operational integrity, specific triggers and actions are defined for policy violations. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for technical remediation, while UMS ensures operational adherence.

| Trigger                                   | Action                                  | Owner            |
|--------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------|
| Unauthorized access attempt                | Block access, alert security team       | Enfrasys, UMS IT |
| Resource naming convention violation       | Notify resource owner, enforce correction | Enfrasys         |
| Exceeding allocated resource quotas        | Alert cost center owner, review usage   | UMS IT           |
| Data refresh failures                      | Log incident, initiate troubleshooting  | Enfrasys         |
| Non-compliant data sharing                 | Revoke sharing, report to governance    | UMS IT           |

This governance framework ensures that UMS’s analytics environment remains secure, cost-effective, and aligned with institutional policies. Enfrasys Solutions will continue to provide guidance and support for ongoing governance enhancements as UMS’s analytics maturity evolves.

# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section outlines the security architecture, network segmentation, and encryption strategies recommended for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) in the deployment of Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and supporting Azure services. The design ensures robust protection of sensitive data, compliance with industry standards, and adherence to Microsoft and Azure security best practices.

## 7.1 Azure NSG Overview

Network Security Groups (NSGs) in Azure provide a critical layer of defense by controlling inbound and outbound traffic to Azure resources at the subnet and network interface level. For the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine, NSGs are configured to allow only the minimum required connectivity, thus reducing the attack surface and ensuring that only legitimate traffic is permitted.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends a least-privilege approach for NSG rules, permitting only essential services and endpoints. This configuration specifically enables secure communication between the OPDG VM, Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, Azure core services, and Microsoft Entra ID for authentication, while blocking all other unsolicited traffic. Such a strategy is fundamental to protecting the UMS environment from external threats and unauthorized access.

The following tables detail the inbound and outbound NSG rules for the Data Gateway VM. The outbound rules are designed to facilitate secure data transfer, authentication, and connectivity verification, while the inbound rules restrict access to only those sources and ports necessary for operational integrity.

### Outbound NSG Rules for Data Gateway VM

| Name                        | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net                | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com                                                      | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                                                                                                                              | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

### Inbound NSG Rules for Data Gateway VM

For enhanced security, inbound access to the Data Gateway VM should be tightly controlled. Enfrasys Solutions recommends permitting only trusted administrative sources (such as specific UMS jump hosts or management subnets) and essential service ports. All other inbound traffic should be denied by default.

| Name                       | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination         | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|----------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|---------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_Admin_Management     | 100      | UMS Admin Subnet      | *            | Data Gateway VM     | 3389              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Deny_All_Other_Inbound     | 4096     | Any                   | *            | Any                 | *                 | Any      | Deny   |

The above configuration ensures that only authorized administrators from UMS can access the VM for management purposes (e.g., via RDP on port 3389), while all other inbound attempts are blocked.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Data protection is paramount in the UMS environment, especially given the sensitivity of institutional and research data handled by Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. Enfrasys Solutions prescribes a comprehensive encryption strategy that encompasses both data at rest and in transit, leveraging Azure-native capabilities and Microsoft security standards.

### Encryption at Rest

All data stored within Microsoft Fabric, OneLake, and Power BI is encrypted at rest using strong encryption algorithms, such as AES-256, managed by Microsoft’s Azure Storage Service Encryption (SSE). This ensures that data residing in storage accounts, data warehouses, and analytics workspaces is protected from unauthorized access, even if physical storage media were compromised.

For the Data Gateway VM, Azure Disk Encryption is enabled, utilizing Azure Key Vault-managed keys to further safeguard virtual hard disks. This approach aligns with compliance requirements and institutional data governance policies.

### Encryption in Transit

Data in transit between the Data Gateway VM, Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and other Azure services is secured using industry-standard protocols such as TLS 1.2 or higher. All communication over public and private networks—including API calls, data synchronization, and user access—is conducted via HTTPS, ensuring confidentiality and integrity against eavesdropping or man-in-the-middle attacks.

Specifically, the outbound NSG rules permit only HTTPS (TCP port 443) traffic to Microsoft cloud endpoints, enforcing encrypted channels for all operational data flows. Additionally, communication between the Data Gateway and on-premises data sources can be configured to use secure protocols such as IPsec or SSL/TLS, depending on the source system’s capabilities.

### Roles and Responsibilities

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the implementation and validation of the security baseline, including NSG configuration, encryption enablement, and periodic security reviews. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for ongoing compliance validation, user access reviews, and ensuring that operational practices adhere to institutional and regulatory requirements.

This clear delineation of responsibilities ensures that security controls are both robustly implemented and continuously maintained, providing UMS with a secure, compliant, and resilient analytics environment.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration approach for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The strategy is structured to ensure a seamless transition from the existing environment to the new Microsoft Fabric-based architecture, minimizing operational disruption and ensuring data integrity throughout the process.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration/Setup

A successful migration is predicated on thorough preparation and coordination between Enfrasys Solutions and UMS. Pre-migration activities ensure that all foundational elements—such as environment readiness, documentation, and stakeholder alignment—are in place before any production data or workloads are moved. This phase includes the review and sign-off of the solution design, confirmation of the staging environment, initial setup of the Fabric environment, and installation of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG).

Enfrasys Solutions recommends a collaborative approach during this stage, with clear task ownership and defined timelines. This approach mitigates risks and ensures that both technical and business prerequisites are addressed before proceeding to pilot and production phases.

The table below details the preparatory tasks, responsible parties, status, and remarks:

| No | Activities                                                            | Planned Start   | Action By         | Status       | Remarks |
|----|----------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1  | Deliver Design Document to be reviewed by UMS & Sign Off             | Fri, 7-Nov-25   | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |
| 2  | Confirmation on the new server (data warehouse copy – Staging Area DB) to be ingested | Mon, 10-Nov-25  | UMS               | Not-Started  |         |
| 3  | Fabric Environment Setup                                              | Wed, 12-Nov-25  | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |         |
| 4  | Install On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in VM to establish Data Gateway | Mon, 17-Nov-25  | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |

## 8.2 Pilot

The pilot phase is critical in validating the migration approach, technical configurations, and operational processes before full-scale production deployment. Enfrasys Solutions will lead the pilot deployment, working closely with UMS IT personnel to ensure that all components function as intended in a controlled environment.

During the pilot, a subset of representative data and workloads will be migrated to the new Fabric environment. This allows both technical and business stakeholders to assess system performance, data integrity, and user experience. The pilot also provides an opportunity to refine migration scripts, test the On-Premises Data Gateway, and validate security and access controls.

Key steps in the pilot phase include:

- Selection of pilot data sets and user groups by UMS.
- Configuration and validation of the Fabric environment by Enfrasys Solutions.
- Execution of initial data ingestion and transformation processes.
- End-to-end testing of data flows, reporting, and user access.
- Collection of feedback and identification of any issues or gaps.

Enfrasys Solutions will document all findings and lessons learned during the pilot, which will inform adjustments to the production migration plan. UMS is responsible for providing access to pilot data and relevant business users for acceptance testing.

## 8.3 Production

Transitioning to production requires meticulous planning and coordination to minimize downtime and ensure business continuity. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a phased approach, structured around a D-7 (seven days before cutover), D0 (cutover day), and D+1 (post-cutover) timeline. Each phase includes specific activities and clear assignment of responsibilities.

### D-7: Pre-Cutover

Seven days prior to the production cutover, all preparatory activities must be finalized. This includes a final review of the environment, confirmation of data sets, user communication, and scheduling.

| Item | Activities                                                                 | Action By         | Status       |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Finalize and freeze migration scripts and runbooks                         | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Confirm production data sets and user mapping                              | UMS               | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Communicate migration schedule and expected downtime to stakeholders       | UMS               | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Complete backup of all critical data                                       | UMS               | Not-Started  |
| 5    | Validate Fabric environment readiness                                      | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |

### D0: Cutover

The cutover day marks the actual migration of production data and switch-over to the new environment. Enfrasys Solutions will execute the migration in close coordination with UMS to ensure all systems are transitioned smoothly.

| Item | Activities                                                                 | Action By         | Status       |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Initiate migration of production data                                      | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Monitor data transfer and validate integrity                               | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Switch user access to the new Fabric environment                           | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Conduct smoke testing and validate business-critical reports               | UMS               | Not-Started  |

### D+1: Post-Cutover

The first day after cutover is dedicated to post-migration validation, issue resolution, and user support. Enfrasys Solutions will provide hypercare support, while UMS will monitor business operations and escalate any issues.

| Item | Activities                                                                 | Action By         | Status       |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Conduct post-migration validation and reconciliation                       | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Provide hypercare support for users                                        | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Document and resolve post-migration issues                                 | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Finalize handover and sign-off on successful migration                     | UMS               | Not-Started  |

## 8.4 Post-Migration/Support

Following successful migration, a structured post-migration support and handover phase ensures that UMS is fully equipped to operate and maintain the new environment. Enfrasys Solutions will provide ongoing support during an agreed transition period, addressing any residual issues and ensuring knowledge transfer to UMS personnel.

Key post-migration activities include:

- Monitoring system performance and user experience.
- Resolving any outstanding technical issues.
- Conducting knowledge transfer sessions and documentation handover.
- Formal transition of operational ownership to UMS IT teams.

The following table outlines the support and handover activities, with clear assignment of responsibilities:

| Item | Activities                                                        | Action By         | Status       |
|------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Monitor system health and performance                             | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Address and resolve post-migration support tickets                | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Conduct knowledge transfer and training for UMS IT staff          | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Handover operational documentation and runbooks                   | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 5    | Formal sign-off of migration and support completion               | UMS               | Not-Started  |

Through this structured deployment and migration approach, Enfrasys Solutions ensures that Universiti Malaysia Sabah achieves a smooth, secure, and effective transition to the new Microsoft Fabric platform, with minimized risk and maximized operational readiness.

The diagram illustrates a technical architecture involving Azure components and user access management. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This represents a Resource Group in Azure, which is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It is depicted with an icon suggesting a fabric or mesh network, indicating a structured environment for managing resources.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely refers to a User Management System within the fabric, responsible for handling user identities, roles, and permissions. It is part of the Fabric-RG, suggesting it operates within the defined resource group.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature in Azure Active Directory that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It is represented by a shield with a lock, indicating its role in securing access.

4. **External User**: This component represents users outside the organization who need access to resources within the Azure environment. The diagram shows a connection from the UMS Fabric to the External User, indicating that user access is managed and controlled through the UMS Fabric and Conditional Access policies.

The data flow begins with the UMS Fabric managing user identities and permissions within the Fabric-RG. Conditional Access policies are applied to ensure secure access, and external users are granted access based on these policies. The connection between UMS Fabric and External User signifies the pathway through which access is facilitated, ensuring security and compliance.

The diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated against Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   - **Signal**: Various signals are gathered, such as user identity and network location. Examples include users and groups, and network location.
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access. This could involve evaluating risk levels or compliance requirements.
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, which could result in granting or denying access, or requiring additional authentication steps.

3. **Inbound to Microsoft Fabric**: Once the conditional access policies are applied, the request is processed inbound to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: The diagram shows integration with various Microsoft Fabric components, including OneLake, which is likely a data storage or management service within the ecosystem.

This architecture ensures secure and conditional access to Microsoft Fabric resources, leveraging Entra's capabilities to assess and enforce access policies dynamically.

The diagram represents a data processing and analytics workflow within the UMS Fabric environment. It begins with data ingestion from a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, labeled as "UMS – Staging Area." The data is ingested using Azure Data Factory, which orchestrates the movement into the XX_Bronze_LK Tables.

The workflow progresses through a series of transformation stages:

1. **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: Initial landing zone for raw data.
2. **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Intermediate processing stage where data is refined and cleansed.
3. **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: Final stage where data is fully processed and ready for analysis.

Azure Data Factory is used again to facilitate the transition between these stages, ensuring data is moved and transformed efficiently.

The diagram also includes a Data Science component, indicating advanced analytics or machine learning processes applied to the data, possibly using Azure Machine Learning or similar services.

Identity_SM is a component involved in managing identities or security aspects, ensuring data integrity and access control.

The processed data in XX_Gold_LK Tables is connected via OneLake to Power BI, enabling visualization and business intelligence reporting.

Overall, the architecture illustrates a comprehensive data pipeline from ingestion to analytics, leveraging Azure services for data transformation, storage, and visualization.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components:

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse**: This component represents the on-premises data storage system where data is initially staged. It is likely used for storing and processing large volumes of data before it is transferred or utilized by other systems.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**: This gateway serves as a bridge between the on-premises data warehouse and external systems. It facilitates secure data transfer without requiring inbound ports, ensuring that data can be sent out securely while minimizing security risks associated with open ports.

3. **UMS Fabric**: This component appears to be a cloud-based service or platform that receives data from the on-premises data gateway. The connection is described as an outbound secure connection, indicating that data is pushed from the on-premises environment to the UMS Fabric, which could be used for further processing, analytics, or integration with other cloud services.

The data flow begins at the UMS Staging Area, where data is stored and possibly processed. It is then securely transmitted via the On-Premise Data Gateway to the UMS Fabric, leveraging an outbound connection that does not require inbound ports, enhancing security and simplifying network configuration.

The diagram represents a data workflow within an Azure environment, focusing on machine learning processes and data integration.

1. **UMS Fabric**: This is the overarching framework within which the components operate, providing the infrastructure for data processing and machine learning.

2. **Data Sources**:
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, containing pre-processed data ready for machine learning experiments.
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: These tables store the refined and processed data, which is the output of machine learning models.

3. **Machine Learning Processes**:
   - **AutoML**: This component automates the machine learning process. It takes data from the XX_Silver_LK Tables and performs experiments to generate models. The results are scored using notebooks, and the output is stored in the XX_Gold_LK Tables.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**: This involves custom modeling using notebooks. Data from the XX_Silver_LK Tables is manually processed and modeled, with results also stored in the XX_Gold_LK Tables.

4. **Identity_SM**: This component likely handles identity management or security, ensuring that data access and processing are secure and compliant.

5. **OneLake**: This is the data lake service that facilitates data storage and management, connecting the various components and enabling seamless data flow.

6. **Power BI**: The XX_Gold_LK Tables are connected to Power BI via OneLake, allowing for data visualization and business intelligence reporting.

The diagram illustrates a comprehensive data processing and machine learning workflow, integrating automated and manual processes, with secure data management and visualization capabilities.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

# UMS Power BI & Microsoft Fabric Solution Design

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a concise overview of the project’s business context, the purpose of this Solution Design Document, and identifies the key stakeholders involved in Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) adoption of Microsoft Fabric and Power BI.

## 2.1 Project Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) is embarking on a transformative initiative to modernize its data analytics and business intelligence capabilities. The primary business drivers for this project are to enhance data-driven decision-making, streamline data integration processes, and leverage advanced analytics to support the university’s strategic objectives. UMS seeks to establish a robust data workflow that encompasses data ingestion, transformation, and visualization, thereby enabling timely and actionable insights for stakeholders across academic and administrative domains.

The project scope covers the design and implementation of an end-to-end data workflow within Microsoft Fabric. This includes the establishment of a dedicated Fabric workspace, development of comprehensive ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines, execution of machine learning processes, and seamless integration with Power BI for business-aligned analytics. The adoption of Microsoft Fabric’s suite of services—such as Fabric Data Factory for data integration and orchestration, Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, and Data Science for machine learning applications—will ensure that UMS can efficiently manage and analyze large volumes of diverse data sources.

The high-level objectives of this initiative are to:
- Align UMS’s cloud adoption strategy with Microsoft Fabric’s capabilities.
- Enable secure, scalable, and automated data workflows from ingestion to visualization.
- Empower UMS’s stakeholders with self-service analytics and advanced reporting through Power BI.
- Foster a data-driven culture that supports academic excellence and operational efficiency.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

The intent of this Solution Design Document is to provide a comprehensive and authoritative blueprint for the design, implementation, and governance of UMS’s Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environment. This document articulates the architectural decisions, technical specifications, and operational workflows that will guide the successful realization of the project objectives.

Serving as a critical reference throughout the project lifecycle, this document ensures alignment between UMS’s business requirements and the technical solution. It provides clarity on roles and responsibilities, facilitates informed decision-making, and forms the basis for subsequent phases such as implementation, testing, and operational handover. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for drafting and maintaining this document, while UMS is accountable for its review, validation, and formal sign-off.

## 2.3 Document Audience

This Solution Design Document is intended for a diverse audience, each with a distinct role in the project’s success. The primary readers include:

- UMS IT Department: Responsible for technical review, implementation coordination, and ongoing operational support.
