# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section details the security architecture and controls for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Data Gateway deployment. It covers network security group (NSG) rule design, encryption strategies, and conditional access enforcement, ensuring robust protection of UMS’s data assets and compliance with Microsoft best practices.

## 7.1 Azure NSG Overview

A Network Security Group (NSG) in Azure is a critical component that acts as a virtual firewall, controlling inbound and outbound traffic to Azure resources. NSGs enable granular network segmentation and restrict access based on specified rules, thus reducing the attack surface and ensuring only authorized communication occurs between UMS’s on-premises and cloud environments.

For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions has designed NSG rules to specifically support secure operations of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine. These rules are tailored to permit essential outbound connectivity to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and required authentication endpoints, while minimizing unnecessary exposure. The rules enforce secure communication over HTTPS (port 443) for all sensitive data transfers and authentication, and allow limited HTTP (port 80) traffic solely for connectivity verification purposes. By explicitly defining destination domains and protocols, this NSG configuration mitigates risks associated with open or overly permissive network policies.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the design and documentation of these NSG rules. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing and implementing the rules within their Azure environment, ensuring that the deployment aligns with organizational security standards.

The following table summarizes the NSG rules recommended for the OPDG virtual machine:

| Name                        | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net                              | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com                                                                     | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                                                                                                                                             | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules ensure that the OPDG VM can securely communicate with Microsoft cloud services, authenticate users, and verify network connectivity, while blocking all other unnecessary outbound traffic.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Encryption is fundamental to safeguarding data confidentiality and integrity across all stages of the data lifecycle. Enfrasys Solutions mandates a comprehensive encryption strategy for UMS’s Fabric, Power BI, and Data Gateway deployment, addressing both data at rest and data in transit.

### Encryption at Rest

Microsoft Fabric and Power BI employ Azure Storage Service Encryption (SSE) by default, ensuring that all stored data—including datasets, reports, and logs—is encrypted using strong AES-256 algorithms. This encryption is transparent and managed by Microsoft, eliminating the risk of human error in key management. For the On-Premises Data Gateway, any cached credentials or configuration files stored on the VM’s disk are protected using Windows Data Protection API (DPAPI) or, where available, BitLocker encryption. These mechanisms ensure that, even in the event of unauthorized physical or logical access to storage media, sensitive data remains protected.

### Encryption in Transit

All data transmitted between UMS’s users, the On-Premises Data Gateway, and Microsoft cloud services is secured using industry-standard Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocols. Power BI and Fabric enforce TLS 1.2 or higher for all client-server and service-to-service communications, protecting data from interception or tampering during transit. The OPDG VM is configured to only allow outbound HTTPS traffic for service communication and authentication, as enforced by the NSG rules, further ensuring that all data exchanges occur over encrypted channels.

### Operational Responsibilities

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for defining and documenting the encryption requirements and ensuring that all configurations adhere to Microsoft security best practices. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing these recommendations and implementing encryption controls within their operational environment, including the activation of BitLocker on gateway VMs and verification of TLS enforcement for all endpoints.

This layered encryption approach ensures that UMS’s sensitive research, administrative, and operational data is protected against both external threats and insider risks, supporting regulatory compliance and institutional trust.

## 7.3 Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication

Conditional Access is a powerful security feature within Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) that enables organizations to enforce access controls based on user identity, device state, location, and risk signals. While Conditional Access policies provide granular control, it is noted that UMS’s current account tier does not support advanced Conditional Access features. As such, Enfrasys Solutions recommends enforcing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level to strengthen user authentication and mitigate unauthorized access.

MFA ensures that all users accessing Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related services must provide an additional verification factor beyond their password. This significantly reduces the risk of account compromise due to phishing or credential theft. Enfrasys Solutions has designed the access policy to include all UMS users in the MFA enforcement scope, ensuring consistent protection across the environment.

The following diagram illustrates the access flow, highlighting the role of Entra Conditional Access and MFA in securing user authentication to Microsoft Fabric:

The diagram below depicts the authentication and access control workflow for Microsoft Fabric. Users initiate authentication, which is evaluated by Entra Conditional Access policies (where available) or enforced MFA. Signals such as user identity and network location are assessed, a decision is made, and enforcement is applied before granting access to Microsoft Fabric and its components, including OneLake.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 7.1: Authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra ID and MFA.

The following table summarizes the key access control design decisions:

| No.  | Conditional Access                                                                                                      |
|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                       |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts                                   |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                     |

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the design and documentation of access policies. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for reviewing the recommendations and ensuring that MFA is enforced for all users in the tenant.

## 7.4 Security Design Summary

The security controls outlined in this section provide a defense-in-depth approach for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s Microsoft Fabric and Power BI deployment. By implementing tightly scoped NSG rules, enforcing encryption at rest and in transit, and mandating MFA for all users, UMS will achieve a robust security posture that aligns with Microsoft best practices and institutional requirements. Enfrasys Solutions will continue to support UMS in reviewing and optimizing these controls as the environment evolves.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration approach for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), ensuring a structured, risk-mitigated transition to the new Microsoft Fabric environment. The process is divided into four distinct phases: Pre-Migration/Setup, Pilot, Production, and Post-Migration/Support. Each phase includes detailed activities, assigned responsibilities, and defined timelines to ensure accountability and operational clarity.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration/Setup

The Pre-Migration/Setup phase establishes the foundational elements required for a successful migration. This phase is critical to minimize risks, ensure readiness, and align all stakeholders on objectives and deliverables. Enfrasys Solutions will lead the technical preparations, while UMS will provide necessary data, environment confirmations, and formal sign-offs.

Key activities in this phase include delivering the finalized Solution Design Document for review and approval, confirming the availability and readiness of the new data warehouse server (staging area database), setting up the Microsoft Fabric environment, and installing the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) to facilitate secure data ingestion from UMS’s on-premises systems to the cloud.

These preparatory steps ensure that all technical prerequisites are met and that UMS’s data landscape is fully mapped and validated before any migration activities commence. Early engagement and sign-off from UMS reduce the risk of misalignment and ensure that the migration proceeds with full organizational support.

### Pre-Migration/Setup Activities Table

| No | Activities                                                          | Planned Start    | Action By         | Status       | Remarks |
|----|---------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|-------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1  | Deliver Design Document to be reviewed by UMS & Sign Off            | Fri, 7-Nov-25    | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |
| 2  | Confirmation on the new server (data warehouse copy – Staging Area DB) to be ingested | Mon, 10-Nov-25   | UMS               | Not-Started  |         |
| 3  | Fabric Environment Setup                                            | Wed, 12-Nov-25   | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |         |
| 4  | Install On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in VM to establish Data Gateway | Mon, 17-Nov-25   | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |

### Pre-Migration/Setup Timeline

- **D-14 to D-7:** Review and sign-off of design documentation.
- **D-7 to D-5:** Confirmation and provisioning of the staging area database.
- **D-5 to D-3:** Microsoft Fabric environment setup.
- **D-3 to D-1:** Installation and configuration of the On-Premises Data Gateway.

## 8.2 Pilot

The Pilot phase is designed to validate the migration approach, processes, and technology stack in a controlled environment. This phase involves migrating a subset of representative data and workloads from UMS to the Microsoft Fabric environment. The pilot allows both Enfrasys Solutions and UMS to identify potential issues, optimize configurations, and refine migration runbooks before full-scale production migration.

During the pilot, Enfrasys Solutions will execute the technical migration steps, monitor system performance, and document any deviations from the expected outcomes. UMS will provide test data, participate in user acceptance testing, and formally approve the pilot results. This collaborative approach ensures that both technical and business requirements are met and that the migration process is robust and repeatable.

A successful pilot mitigates risks, builds stakeholder confidence, and provides a blueprint for the production migration phase.

### Pilot Activities Table

| Item | Activities                                                    | Action By         | Status       |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Select pilot data sets and workloads                          | UMS               | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Migrate pilot data to Fabric environment                      | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Validate data integrity and system functionality              | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Conduct user acceptance testing (UAT)                         | UMS               | Not-Started  |
| 5    | Document lessons learned and update migration runbook         | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |

### Pilot Phase Timeline

- **D-7:** Selection of pilot data sets and workloads.
- **D-6:** Migration of pilot data to Microsoft Fabric.
- **D-5:** Validation of data and system functionality.
- **D-4:** User acceptance testing by UMS.
- **D-3:** Documentation of lessons learned and migration runbook updates.

## 8.3 Production

The Production phase encompasses the full-scale migration of all targeted data and workloads from UMS’s on-premises environment to Microsoft Fabric. This phase is executed according to a rigorously defined timeline to minimize business disruption and ensure data consistency.

The production migration is structured into three core stages: Pre-Migration (D-7), Cutover (D0), and Post-Migration Validation (D+1). During Pre-Migration, final checks, user communications, and backup procedures are completed. The Cutover stage (D0) involves the actual migration of data and switchover of production workloads. Post-Migration (D+1) focuses on system validation, user verification, and resolving any issues that arise during the transition.

Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the technical execution, while UMS provides data, monitors business continuity, and grants formal acceptance at each stage.

### Production Activities Table

| Item | Activities                                                    | Action By         | Status       |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Final pre-migration checks and user communication             | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Backup of critical data and systems                           | UMS               | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Execute production data migration and cutover                 | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Monitor migration progress and system performance             | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 5    | Validate system functionality and data integrity post-migration | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 6    | Obtain final user acceptance and sign-off                     | UMS               | Not-Started  |

### Production Phase Timeline

- **D-7:** Final checks, communication, and backups.
- **D0:** Production migration and cutover.
- **D+1:** Post-migration validation and user acceptance.

## 8.4 Post-Migration/Support

The Post-Migration/Support phase ensures a smooth transition to steady-state operations and provides UMS with the necessary support and knowledge transfer to maintain the new environment. This phase includes monitoring system stability, addressing post-migration issues, conducting knowledge transfer sessions, and formally handing over operational responsibilities to UMS.

Enfrasys Solutions will provide post-migration monitoring, incident response, and documentation, while UMS will participate in knowledge transfer sessions and acknowledge the formal handover. This collaborative approach ensures that UMS’s IT team is fully equipped to operate and support the Microsoft Fabric environment independently.

### Post-Migration/Support Activities Table

| Item | Activities                                                    | Action By         | Status       |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1    | Monitor system stability and performance                      | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 2    | Address and resolve post-migration issues                     | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |
| 3    | Conduct knowledge transfer and training sessions              | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 4    | Formal handover of documentation and operational procedures   | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |
| 5    | Acknowledge handover and commence BAU operations              | UMS               | Not-Started  |

### Post-Migration/Support Timeline

- **D+1 to D+7:** System monitoring and issue resolution.
- **D+2 to D+5:** Knowledge transfer and training sessions.
- **D+7:** Formal handover and commencement of business-as-usual (BAU) operations.

The diagram illustrates a technical architecture involving Azure components and user access management. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This represents a Resource Group in Azure, which is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It is depicted with an icon suggesting a fabric or mesh, indicating a structured environment for resource management.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely refers to a User Management System within the Azure environment, possibly utilizing Azure Fabric for scalable and reliable service deployment. It is part of the Fabric-RG, suggesting it is a resource managed within this group.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature in Azure Active Directory that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It is represented by a shield with a lock, indicating security measures to protect resources.

4. **External User**: This component represents users outside the organization who need access to resources within the Azure environment. The diagram shows a connection from the UMS Fabric to the External User, indicating that user access is managed through the UMS Fabric and governed by Conditional Access policies.

The data flow suggests that external users interact with the UMS Fabric, which is secured by Conditional Access policies to ensure that only authorized users can access the resources within the Fabric-RG.

The diagram illustrates the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra conditional access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated against Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   - **Signal**: Various signals are gathered, such as user identity and network location. Examples include users and groups, and network location.
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access. This could involve evaluating risk levels or compliance requirements.
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, which could result in granting or denying access, or requiring additional authentication steps.

3. **Inbound to Microsoft Fabric**: Once the conditional access policies are applied, the request is processed inbound to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: The diagram shows integration with Microsoft Fabric, which includes various components such as data analytics, storage, and processing tools. OneLake is highlighted as a part of the ecosystem, indicating its role in data storage or management within Microsoft Fabric.

This architecture ensures secure and conditional access to Microsoft Fabric resources, leveraging Entra's capabilities to evaluate and enforce access policies based on real-time signals and decisions.

The diagram represents a data processing and analytics architecture within the UMS Fabric environment. It illustrates the flow of data from ingestion to visualization using various Azure components.

1. **Data Ingestion**: 
   - Data originates from the UMS Staging Area, specifically a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse.
   - Data Factory is used to ingest data into the system.

2. **Data Processing**:
   - **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: Initial landing zone for ingested data, likely used for raw data storage.
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Intermediate processing stage where data is cleaned and transformed.
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: Final processing stage where data is refined and ready for analysis.

3. **Data Science and Transformation**:
   - Data Factory is used again for further data transformation and orchestration.
   - Data Science tools are employed for advanced analytics and machine learning processes.
   - Identity_SM component is involved, possibly for security or identity management.

4. **Data Storage and Access**:
   - OneLake is used for unified data storage, providing seamless access and integration across the architecture.

5. **Data Visualization**:
   - Processed data in the XX_Gold_LK Tables is connected via OneLake to Power BI for visualization and reporting.

This architecture supports efficient data processing, transformation, and visualization, leveraging Azure services for scalability and integration.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components:

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 - Data Warehouse**: This component represents the on-premises data warehouse where data is stored and managed using Microsoft SQL Server 2017. It serves as the staging area for data processing and storage.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**: This gateway facilitates secure communication between the on-premises data warehouse and external services. It enables data to be securely transferred without requiring inbound ports, ensuring that the connection is outbound and secure.

3. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely represents a cloud-based service or platform that interacts with the on-premises data warehouse through the OPDG. The UMS Fabric receives data from the warehouse via the secure outbound connection established by the gateway.

The data flow begins at the UMS Staging Area, where data is stored in the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse. The On-Premise Data Gateway acts as a bridge, securely transmitting data to the UMS Fabric without the need for inbound ports, thus maintaining security and integrity.

The diagram represents a data workflow within an Azure environment, focusing on machine learning processes and data integration.

1. **UMS Fabric**: This is the overarching framework within which the components operate, providing the infrastructure for data processing and machine learning.

2. **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, containing pre-processed data ready for machine learning experiments.

3. **AutoML**: This component automates the machine learning process. It takes data from the XX_Silver_LK Tables and runs an AutoML Experiment. The results are scored using a notebook, which likely involves evaluating model performance and metrics.

4. **Manual Machine Learning**: An alternative to AutoML, this component involves manual modeling using notebooks. It allows for custom model development and experimentation, providing flexibility for data scientists to tailor models to specific needs.

5. **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: These tables store the refined and processed data post-experimentation, whether from AutoML or Manual Machine Learning. They represent a higher quality dataset, ready for further analysis or reporting.

6. **Identity_SM**: This component likely handles identity management or security, ensuring that data access and manipulation are secure and compliant with organizational policies.

7. **OneLake**: This is the data lake service facilitating storage and integration, connecting the XX_Gold_LK Tables to Power BI for visualization and reporting.

8. **Power BI**: The final component in the workflow, Power BI connects via OneLake to the XX_Gold_LK Tables, enabling data visualization and business intelligence reporting.

The diagram illustrates a seamless flow from data ingestion and processing in XX_Silver_LK Tables, through machine learning experimentation (either automated or manual), to refined data storage in XX_Gold_LK Tables, and finally to data visualization in Power BI.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

# UMS Power BI & Microsoft Fabric Solution Design

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a concise overview of the project’s objectives, the purpose of this Solution Design Document, and the intended audience and responsibilities for review and approval.

## 2.1 Project Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) has embarked on a strategic initiative to modernize its data analytics capabilities by leveraging Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. The primary business objective is to design and implement a robust data workflow that supports the university’s evolving analytics and reporting needs. The project scope encompasses the end-to-end data lifecycle, beginning with data ingestion, followed by transformation and advanced analytics, and culminating in business-aligned visualization.

The high-level goals of this project include the establishment of a dedicated Microsoft Fabric workspace, the development of comprehensive ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines, and the integration of machine learning processes to support predictive analytics. Furthermore, the project aims to seamlessly integrate Power BI to provide actionable insights and facilitate data-driven decision-making across the university. The solution will align with UMS’s broader cloud adoption strategy by utilizing key Microsoft Fabric services, namely Fabric Data Factory for data integration and orchestration, Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, and Data Science capabilities for machine learning applications. By adopting this architecture, UMS will benefit from improved scalability, enhanced data governance, and accelerated time-to-insight.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

The intent of this Solution Design Document is to serve as the authoritative reference for the architecture, design decisions, and operational workflows that will underpin the Power BI and Microsoft Fabric implementation at UMS. This document details the technical blueprint, including the rationale behind each design choice, to ensure alignment with UMS’s business requirements and Microsoft best practices. It provides clear guidance on the deployment, configuration, and integration of the proposed solution components, and establishes a baseline for future enhancements and operational management.

Within the project lifecycle, this document functions as a critical milestone, ensuring all stakeholders have a shared understanding of the solution approach prior to implementation. It will be used to validate requirements, facilitate informed decision-making, and serve as a benchmark for post-deployment review and continuous improvement.

## 2.3 Document Audience

The Solution Design Document is intended for a diverse audience comprising both technical and business stakeholders. The primary readers include:

- UMS IT Department: Responsible for operational ownership, ongoing support, and integration with existing university systems.
- UMS Business Stakeholders: Senior leadership and departmental representatives who will leverage analytics for strategic and operational decision-making.
- Enfrasys Solutions Implementation Team: Accountable for the design, deployment, and configuration of all solution components.
- Project Sponsors and Governance Committees: Oversight and alignment with university-wide digital transformation initiatives.

Responsibility for the review and formal sign-off of this document is jointly assigned to Universiti Malaysia Sabah and Enfrasys Solutions. Both parties must ensure that the documented solution meets the university’s business objectives, adheres to compliance requirements, and is technically sound prior to commencement of implementation activities.

# 3.0 Microsoft Fabric and Power BI Overview

This section provides an authoritative overview of Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Microsoft Entra ID, with a focus on their relevance and integration within the Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) data analytics solution.

## 3.1 Microsoft Fabric

Microsoft Fabric is an integrated, end-to-end analytics platform designed to unify data movement, data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence. Fabric consolidates a suite of core services, including Data Factory, Synapse, Data Science, and OneLake, into a single SaaS environment. This unified approach streamlines the development and management of complex data workflows, enabling organizations to derive actionable insights efficiently and securely.

For Universiti Malaysia Sabah, Microsoft Fabric serves as the foundational platform for orchestrating the entire data lifecycle—from ingestion to transformation, advanced analytics, and visualization. The platform’s modular services enable UMS to address diverse analytical requirements, ensuring scalability, security, and operational agility. The following core services are central to the UMS solution design:

- **Data Factory**: Provides robust data integration and orchestration capabilities, allowing UMS to ingest, transform, and move data across various sources and destinations. This service ensures that data is reliably prepared for downstream analytics and reporting.
- **Synapse**: Delivers advanced analytics and big data processing, enabling UMS to perform large-scale data exploration, transformation, and analysis with high performance. Synapse empowers data engineers and analysts to build complex analytical models and pipelines.
- **Data Science**: Facilitates the development and operationalization of machine learning models. UMS can leverage this service to conduct predictive analytics and automate decision-making processes using advanced algorithms.
- **OneLake**: Acts as a unified data lake, centralizing storage for structured and unstructured data. This ensures data consistency, accessibility, and governance across the UMS analytics ecosystem.

By adopting Microsoft Fabric, UMS benefits from a cohesive platform that reduces integration overhead, enhances data governance, and accelerates time-to-insight for business-aligned analytics initiatives.

## 3.2 Power BI

Power BI is Microsoft’s enterprise-grade business intelligence and data visualization platform, enabling organizations to create interactive reports and dashboards from diverse data sources. Power BI seamlessly integrates with Microsoft Fabric, allowing users to visualize data processed and curated within Fabric’s unified environment.

For Universiti Malaysia Sabah, Power BI is a critical component for delivering actionable insights to stakeholders. The integration with Fabric ensures that data visualizations are always based on the most current, governed, and secured datasets. Power BI’s self-service capabilities empower UMS business users to explore data, identify trends, and make informed decisions without dependency on IT or data engineering teams.

The synergy between Power BI and Fabric ensures that UMS can implement end-to-end analytics workflows—from data ingestion and transformation in Fabric to rich, interactive visualizations in Power BI. This integration streamlines the delivery of business intelligence, supports data-driven culture, and aligns with UMS’s strategic objectives for advanced analytics.

## 3.3 Microsoft Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is Microsoft’s cloud-based identity and access management service. It provides secure authentication, single sign-on, and role-based access control for users and applications across the Microsoft ecosystem, including Fabric and Power BI.

Within the Universiti Malaysia Sabah solution, Entra ID plays a pivotal role in safeguarding data assets and managing user access. By leveraging Entra ID, UMS ensures that only authorized personnel can access sensitive data and analytics resources. Entra ID supports multi-factor authentication, conditional access policies, and comprehensive audit logging, thereby strengthening the security posture of the analytics environment.

The integration of Entra ID with Microsoft Fabric and Power BI enables centralized identity management, simplifies user provisioning, and enforces consistent security policies across all analytics workloads. This approach aligns with best practices for data governance and regulatory compliance, providing UMS with the assurance that its analytics platform is both secure and scalable.

# 4.0 Network Design and Decision

This section outlines the network architecture and connectivity requirements for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric deployment. The network design prioritizes secure, reliable outbound data flows from on-premises systems to Microsoft Fabric, leveraging the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) as the critical bridge. All connectivity, security controls, and platform components are detailed to ensure both compliance with Microsoft best practices and operational efficiency for UMS.

## 4.1 Network Connectivity Overview

The overall network topology for UMS’s Microsoft Fabric solution is designed to provide secure, managed, and auditable data flows between on-premises data sources and the Microsoft Fabric environment. The architecture leverages the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG), which is deployed within the UMS on-premises network, to facilitate outbound connectivity to Microsoft Fabric and related Microsoft cloud services. This design ensures that sensitive data remains protected within UMS’s network perimeter while enabling seamless integration with cloud analytics and reporting capabilities.

A key principle of this architecture is the use of outbound-only connectivity from the OPDG virtual machine. No inbound ports are required or permitted, significantly reducing the attack surface and simplifying firewall management. All communications from the OPDG to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication services are encrypted using HTTPS (TCP port 443), adhering to industry-standard security protocols. This approach ensures that UMS’s data flows are both secure and compliant with institutional and regulatory requirements.

To support this connectivity, Enfrasys Solutions recommends the implementation of explicit outbound network security group (NSG) rules. These rules allow only the necessary traffic to Microsoft endpoints for data transfer, authentication, and connectivity verification. UMS is responsible for ensuring that the on-premises network is prepared to support these outbound connections, including any required firewall or proxy adjustments. Enfrasys Solutions will provide design guidance and perform the initial configuration of the OPDG and its network settings.

The following diagram illustrates the secure data flow from the UMS on-premises data warehouse, through the OPDG, to the Microsoft Fabric environment. Data is staged and processed within UMS’s local infrastructure before being securely transmitted to the cloud for further analytics and reporting.

![](images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Figure 1: Secure outbound data flow from UMS on-premises staging area to Microsoft Fabric via the On-Premises Data Gateway.

## 4.2 Microsoft Fabric Data Gateway

The Microsoft On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a specialized component that enables secure data transfer between UMS’s on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric services such as Power BI, Dataflows, and Data Warehousing. The OPDG acts as a bridge, facilitating encrypted outbound communication to the cloud without exposing internal resources to inbound internet traffic. This architectural decision is critical for maintaining the security and integrity of UMS’s data assets.

Enfrasys Solutions recommends deploying the OPDG on a dedicated virtual machine within the UMS on-premises environment. The gateway agent will be installed and configured to connect securely to Microsoft Fabric endpoints. The VM hosting the OPDG must meet specific hardware and software requirements to ensure reliability and performance. The following table details the recommended specifications for the OPDG VM:

| Component        | Specification                                     |
|------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework   | Version 4.8                                      |
| vCPU             | 8 cores                                          |
| RAM              | Minimum 8 GB                                     |

This configuration ensures that the OPDG can efficiently handle concurrent data transfers, authentication requests, and service health monitoring. The use of a modern, supported Windows operating system and the latest .NET Framework version is essential for compatibility and security.

A key security advantage of the OPDG architecture is that no inbound ports are required on the UMS firewall. All communications are initiated outbound from the gateway VM, using HTTPS for data transfer and authentication, and HTTP for connectivity testing. This minimizes the risk of unauthorized access and aligns with best practices for hybrid cloud integration.

Enfrasys Solutions will be responsible for the design, sizing, and initial deployment of the OPDG virtual machine and gateway agent. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for ensuring that the on-premises network environment is ready for the deployment, including provisioning the required VM resources and configuring any necessary outbound firewall or proxy rules.

## 4.3 Outbound Network Security Group (NSG) Rules

To ensure secure and reliable connectivity between the OPDG and Microsoft cloud services, specific outbound NSG rules must be implemented. These rules restrict traffic to only the necessary Microsoft endpoints for data transfer, authentication, and connectivity validation. All traffic is encrypted, and no inbound access is permitted.

The following table outlines the recommended outbound NSG rules for the OPDG VM:

| Name                       | Priority | Source                 | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                                                                   | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|----------------------------|----------|------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services  | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication  | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com                               | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160     | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                                                                | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules ensure that the OPDG can authenticate with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), transfer data securely to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, and verify network connectivity. All other outbound traffic should be restricted to minimize exposure.

Enfrasys Solutions will document and validate these NSG rules as part of the OPDG deployment. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for implementing these rules within the on-premises firewall or equivalent network security solution, ensuring compliance with institutional security policies.

# 6.0 Microsoft Fabric Solution Design and Decision

This section details the Microsoft Fabric solution architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), encompassing data workflow, access, resource organization, and governance. The design leverages best practices for data engineering, machine learning, and analytics, ensuring secure, scalable, and operationally efficient outcomes for UMS.

## 6.1 Data Workflow Design Considerations

A robust data workflow is essential for UMS to ensure reliable ingestion, transformation, and analytics across its data estate. The solution is designed to accommodate both traditional ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and advanced machine learning (ML) workloads, providing a seamless integration between raw data sources, processing layers, and reporting endpoints.

The design incorporates a multi-layered lakehouse architecture—Bronze, Silver, and Gold—each serving a distinct purpose in the data lifecycle. The Bronze layer acts as the raw data landing zone, the Silver layer is dedicated to data cleansing and enrichment, and the Gold layer provides curated, analytics-ready datasets. This structured approach enables UMS to maintain data lineage, quality, and governance across all stages. Integration with Power BI and the adoption of Dataflow Gen2 and Notebooks ensures flexibility for both data engineers and data scientists, supporting operational analytics and advanced ML scenarios.

The following table summarizes the key workflow considerations for both ETL and ML workloads:

| ID    | Description                                                                                                                   | Workload Type    |
|-------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| DC01  | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                                          | Data Factory     |
| DC02  | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                                         | Data Engineering |
| DC03  | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse.                                              | Data Engineering |
| DC04  | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse.                                             | Data Engineering |
| DC05  | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook, and Visual Query. | Data Engineering |
| DC06  | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold).                   | Data Factory     |
| DC07  | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse | Data Science |
| DC08  | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.                                  | Power BI         |
| DC09  | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                                       | Power BI         |
| DE01  | The cleaned data stored in Silver Lakehouse will serve as the input for all machine learning experimentation.                 | Data Engineering |
| DE02  | The project will primarily adopt the Manual Machine Learning approach using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python).             | Data Science     |
| DE03  | Users will perform model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning directly in the Notebook environment.                          | Data Science     |
| DE04  | Trained models will be registered in the Fabric Model Registry for lifecycle management and reuse.                            | Data Science     |
| DE05  | Scoring Notebooks will be used to apply trained models to new data, with outputs stored in the Gold Lakehouse.                | Data Engineering |
| DE06  | The Gold Lakehouse will serve as the standardized repository for all modeled or scored results.                               | Data Engineering |
| DE07  | The Semantic Model will connect to the Gold Lakehouse via Direct Lake (OneLake).                                              | Power BI         |
| DE08  | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                                       | Power BI         |

## 6.2 Data Workflow Design Decisions

The Microsoft Fabric solution for UMS is architected to deliver a modern, scalable, and secure data platform. The design leverages Azure Data Factory for orchestrated data ingestion from the Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse into the Bronze Lakehouse. Data is then systematically refined through the Silver and Gold Lakehouses, with each stage managed by Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query tools. This ensures that data quality and transformation logic are consistently applied, supporting both operational reporting and advanced analytics.

Machine learning workflows are integrated directly into the Fabric environment. Cleaned data from the Silver Lakehouse serves as the input for both AutoML and manual ML processes. Data scientists at UMS can leverage Fabric Notebooks (using PySpark or Python) for custom feature engineering, model training, and evaluation. Trained models are registered in the Fabric Model Registry, supporting lifecycle management and reuse. Scoring notebooks apply these models to new data, with results stored in the Gold Lakehouse. The Gold Lakehouse acts as the single source of truth for analytics, with Power BI connecting via OneLake for seamless visualization and reporting.

This architecture provides UMS with a flexible, end-to-end platform for data-driven decision-making, supporting both batch and real-time analytics, and enabling advanced ML scenarios while maintaining strict data governance.

For the machine learning workflow, the integration of AutoML and manual ML processes ensures that UMS can address both automated and custom modeling requirements. ![](images\slide_021_img_005.png)

Figure 2: Machine learning workflow leveraging AutoML, Notebooks, and Fabric Model Registry in UMS Fabric.

The table below summarizes the key design decisions for the workflow:

| No. | Design Decision                                                                                         | Decision                                                                                       |
|-----|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Data ingestion from SQL Server via Data Factory                                                        | Adopted for orchestrated, secure, and scalable data movement                                   |
| 2   | Multi-layered Lakehouse architecture (Bronze, Silver, Gold)                                            | Ensures data quality, lineage, and separation of concerns                                      |
| 3   | Data transformation using Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query                                   | Allows for flexible, code-based, and visual data engineering                                   |
| 4   | Machine learning integration using both AutoML and manual ML in Notebooks                              | Supports both automated and custom modeling needs                                              |
| 5   | Model registration in Fabric Model Registry                                                            | Enables lifecycle management and model reuse                                                   |
| 6   | Storage of scored results and curated data in Gold Lakehouse                                           | Centralizes analytics-ready data and ML outputs                                                |
| 7   | Power BI connection via OneLake to Semantic Model                                                      | Provides seamless, real-time analytics and visualization                                       |
| 8   | Identity management and access control using Entra ID and Conditional Access policies                  | Ensures secure, compliant access to data and analytics                                         |

## 6.3 Access Design Considerations and Decisions

Ensuring secure and compliant access to data and analytics resources is paramount for UMS. The access design leverages Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) for identity and access management, Conditional Access policies for risk-based controls, and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for enhanced security.

### 6.3.1 Conditional Access

Conditional Access policies provide dynamic, risk-based access controls, enabling UMS to enforce security requirements based on user context, device compliance, and location. Enfrasys Solutions recommends defining granular policies to restrict access to sensitive data and administrative functions within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environments.

| No. | Policy                                                                                 | Decision                                                                                         |
|-----|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Require compliant or hybrid Azure AD joined device for admin access                     | Enforced for all administrative roles                                                            |
| 2   | Block access from non-trusted locations                                                 | Enforced for sensitive datasets and administrative endpoints                                     |
| 3   | Require MFA for all privileged operations                                               | Enforced for all users with access to Gold Lakehouse or Model Registry                           |

### 6.3.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

MFA is a critical component in mitigating credential-based attacks. UMS shall implement MFA for all users accessing Microsoft Fabric, especially those with administrative or data engineering privileges. This ensures that even if credentials are compromised, unauthorized access is prevented.

| No. | Policy                                                      | Decision                                              |
|-----|-------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Enforce MFA for all users accessing Fabric workspaces        | Mandatory                                             |
| 2   | Enforce MFA for Power BI service access                      | Mandatory                                             |
| 3   | Exempt service principals with managed identities            | Allowed, with monitoring and review                   |

### 6.3.3 Entra ID (Azure Active Directory)

Microsoft Entra ID provides a unified platform for identity management, supporting single sign-on, role-based access control, and integration with Conditional Access and MFA. Enfrasys Solutions recommends leveraging Entra ID groups to assign workspace roles and manage access at scale.

| No. | Policy                                                   | Decision                                              |
|-----|----------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Assign workspace roles via Entra ID groups               | Recommended                                           |
| 2   | Use role-based access control for all Fabric resources   | Mandatory                                             |
| 3   | Enable audit logging for all access and role changes     | Mandatory                                             |

# 7.0 Security Design and Decision

This section details the comprehensive security architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environment. The design encompasses network security controls, encryption standards, and identity protection measures, ensuring robust protection of sensitive data and compliance with industry best practices.

## 7.1 Azure Network Security Group (NSG) Overview

Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) are a fundamental security feature that enables granular control over network traffic to and from Azure resources. An NSG contains a list of security rules that allow or deny inbound and outbound network traffic, based on source and destination IP address, port, and protocol. By implementing NSGs, UMS can restrict access to critical resources, minimize the attack surface, and ensure that only authorized communications are permitted between the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine, Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and supporting Azure services.

For UMS, Enfrasys Solutions has designed NSG rules that specifically address the secure operation of the OPDG VM. These rules ensure that only essential outbound traffic is permitted, such as secure HTTPS connections to Microsoft cloud endpoints for data transfer, authentication, and connectivity verification. No inbound rules are required for the OPDG VM, as all interactions are initiated outbound from the VM to Microsoft services, further reducing exposure to external threats.

The following table presents the outbound NSG rules recommended for the OPDG VM. Each rule is carefully prioritized and scoped to only the necessary endpoints and ports, adhering to the principle of least privilege.

| Name                        | Priority | Source                 | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                                              | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|-----------------------------|----------|------------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services   | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication   | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com           | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                                           | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules are designed to ensure secure, uninterrupted operation of the data gateway while maintaining strict control over network traffic. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the initial configuration and validation of these NSG rules during deployment. UMS shall assume responsibility for ongoing monitoring and periodic review of NSG configurations to maintain compliance and adapt to any evolving security requirements.

## 7.2 Encryption Design

Encryption is a critical component of the security posture for UMS’s data analytics and reporting infrastructure. Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and the On-Premises Data Gateway all support industry-standard encryption mechanisms to safeguard data both at rest and in transit. This dual-layered encryption approach ensures that sensitive information is protected from unauthorized access, interception, or tampering throughout its lifecycle.

### Encryption at Rest

Data at rest within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI is protected using Azure Storage Service Encryption (SSE) with Microsoft-managed keys by default. This includes all datasets, reports, and artifacts stored in OneLake, Fabric Data Warehouses, and Power BI workspaces. SSE leverages 256-bit AES encryption, which is recognized as a robust and compliant standard for protecting stored data. For additional control, UMS may consider implementing customer-managed keys (CMK) in the future, subject to licensing and operational requirements.

The On-Premises Data Gateway VM, if configured with persistent storage or caching, should utilize Azure Disk Encryption (ADE) to ensure all local data remains encrypted. ADE integrates with Azure Key Vault for secure key management, further enhancing data security.

### Encryption in Transit

All data transmissions between UMS users, the On-Premises Data Gateway, Microsoft Fabric, and Power BI services are encrypted using Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.2 or higher. This includes:

- User connections to Power BI and Fabric via web browsers or client applications, protected by HTTPS.
- Communication between the OPDG VM and Microsoft cloud endpoints, enforced by the NSG rules outlined above to permit only secure protocols.
- Data movement within Azure services, leveraging Azure’s internal encrypted channels.

By mandating TLS for all data flows, UMS ensures that sensitive information is shielded from interception and man-in-the-middle attacks during transit.

### Operational Responsibilities

Enfrasys Solutions is accountable for the initial security configuration, including the setup of encryption settings, validation of secure communication channels, and documentation of key management practices. Universiti Malaysia Sabah is responsible for ongoing monitoring, compliance checks, and responding to any security incidents or audit findings related to encryption and data protection.

The following table summarizes the encryption design decisions:

| No. | Design Decision                                                                                     | Decision                |
|-----|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------|
| ED01| All data at rest in Fabric and Power BI is encrypted using Azure Storage Service Encryption (SSE).  | Enforced                |
| ED02| On-Premises Data Gateway VM disks are encrypted using Azure Disk Encryption (ADE).                  | Enforced                |
| ED03| All data in transit is protected by TLS 1.2 or higher.                                              | Enforced                |
| ED04| Enfrasys Solutions configures encryption settings; UMS monitors and maintains ongoing compliance.   | Assigned                |

## 7.3 Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication

Conditional Access is a Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) feature that provides adaptive access control based on user, device, location, and risk signals. However, based on the current assessment, UMS’s tenant is not eligible for advanced Conditional Access policies due to licensing constraints. As a compensating control, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) has been enforced at the tenant level for all UMS users accessing Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related services.

This approach ensures that, even in the absence of Conditional Access, user identities are strongly protected against unauthorized access and credential compromise. MFA requires users to provide an additional verification factor beyond their password, significantly reducing the risk posed by phishing and brute-force attacks.

The following diagram illustrates the secure access workflow for Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, highlighting the role of MFA and conditional access policies:

The first diagram below shows the technical architecture for user access management, where external users are subjected to security controls before accessing resources within the Azure environment.

![](images\slide_006_img_001.png)

Figure 7.1: High-level access architecture with Conditional Access and resource group boundaries.

The second diagram details the authentication process, including the evaluation of Entra conditional access policies and enforcement of MFA, ensuring that only compliant users are granted access to Microsoft Fabric resources.

![](images\slide_006_img_002.png)

Figure 7.2: Authentication and access control flow with Entra conditional access and MFA enforcement.

The following table summarizes the key access-related design decisions for UMS:

| No.  | Conditional Access                                                                                                 |
|------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                  |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts                              |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                |

Enfrasys Solutions will revisit Conditional Access policy implementation if UMS upgrades to the required Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses in the future. For now, MFA enforcement provides a strong baseline for identity security.

---

This security design ensures that Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s analytics environment is protected by industry-leading controls at the network, data, and identity layers, with clear responsibility delineation between Enfrasys Solutions and UMS for deployment and ongoing operations.

# 8.0 Deployment & Migration Approach

This section outlines the comprehensive deployment and migration approach for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), detailing the preparatory activities, pilot execution, production cutover, and post-migration support. The methodology ensures a structured, low-risk transition to the new solution, with clear task ownership and acceptance checkpoints.

## 8.1 Pre-Migration/Setup

A successful migration begins with meticulous planning and preparation. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a phased approach, starting with the delivery and review of the Solution Design Document, followed by confirmation of the new server environment for data ingestion, setup of the Microsoft Fabric environment, and installation of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). These preparatory steps are critical to ensuring that all technical prerequisites are met and that both Enfrasys Solutions and UMS are aligned on the migration objectives and infrastructure readiness.

Enfrasys Solutions will lead the delivery of the design documentation and the technical setup of the Fabric environment and OPDG. UMS is responsible for reviewing and signing off on key deliverables, as well as confirming the availability of the required server resources. This collaborative approach ensures that all stakeholders are engaged and that the migration proceeds on a solid foundation.

The following table details the preparatory activities, associated timelines, responsible parties, and current status:

| No | Activities                                                         | Planned Start   | Action By         | Status       | Remarks |
|----|---------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1  | Deliver Design Document to be reviewed by UMS & Sign Off           | Fri, 7-Nov-25   | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |
| 2  | Confirmation on the new server (data warehouse copy – Staging Area DB) to be ingested | Mon, 10-Nov-25  | UMS               | Not-Started  |         |
| 3  | Fabric Environment Setup                                            | Wed, 12-Nov-25  | Enfrasys          | Not-Started  |         |
| 4  | Install On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in VM to establish Data Gateway | Mon, 17-Nov-25 | Enfrasys, UMS     | Not-Started  |         |

## 8.2 Pilot

The pilot phase is designed to validate the migration process, test the end-to-end data flow, and ensure that all components function as intended in a controlled environment. Enfrasys Solutions will coordinate the pilot deployment, leveraging a representative subset of UMS data and user scenarios to simulate production conditions. The pilot will include the configuration of data ingestion pipelines, validation of data transformation logic, and verification of reporting outputs.

Acceptance criteria for the pilot phase will be jointly defined by Enfrasys Solutions and UMS, focusing on data integrity, system performance, and user accessibility. UMS will be responsible for validating the migrated data and confirming that the solution meets functional requirements. Successful completion of the pilot, as evidenced by documented acceptance from UMS, is a prerequisite for advancing to full production deployment.

Key steps in the pilot phase include:

- Configuration of Fabric workspaces and dataflows for pilot datasets.
- Installation and testing of the On-Premises Data Gateway to ensure secure, reliable connectivity.
- Execution of test data migrations and validation of data accuracy.
- User acceptance testing (UAT) by UMS stakeholders.
- Documentation of issues and remediation actions.

Acceptance Criteria:

- All pilot data is migrated accurately and completely.
- Reports and dashboards reflect correct, up-to-date information.
- Data refresh and gateway connectivity are stable and performant.
- UMS stakeholders formally accept pilot results.

## 8.3 Production

The production cutover is structured to minimize risk and disruption to UMS operations. Enfrasys Solutions recommends a phased timeline, with defined milestones and responsibilities for each stage. The process includes a pre-migration review, the main cutover event, and immediate post-migration validation and support.

The following timeline outlines the key activities for the production migration:

### D-7: Pre-Migration

In the week leading up to the migration, Enfrasys Solutions will finalize all technical preparations, including final environment checks, backup verification, and communication of migration schedules to all stakeholders. UMS will ensure that all necessary data and systems are ready for migration.

| Item                    | Activities                                                                 | Action By         | Status       |
|-------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Final Environment Check | Validate Fabric environment, OPDG connectivity, and data source readiness  | Enfrasys          | Pending      |
| Backup Verification     | Confirm backup of legacy data warehouse and staging area                    | UMS               | Pending      |
| Stakeholder Notification| Communicate migration window and support contacts                          | Enfrasys, UMS     | Pending      |

### D0: Cutover

On the day of migration, Enfrasys Solutions will execute the data migration processes, monitor system performance, and coordinate with UMS for real-time validation. UMS will perform data validation checks and provide immediate feedback on any anomalies.

| Item                | Activities                                               | Action By         | Status       |
|---------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Data Migration      | Execute migration scripts and monitor data flows         | Enfrasys          | Pending      |
| Data Validation     | Review migrated data for completeness and accuracy       | UMS               | Pending      |
| Issue Resolution    | Address any migration-related issues in real time        | Enfrasys, UMS     | Pending      |

### D+1: Post-Migration

Following the migration, Enfrasys Solutions will provide intensified support to address any post-migration issues. UMS will continue to validate system functionality and report any discrepancies.

| Item                 | Activities                                              | Action By         | Status       |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Post-Migration Support | Provide hypercare support and monitor system health   | Enfrasys          | Pending      |
| Ongoing Validation     | Continue data and report validation                   | UMS               | Pending      |
| Final Acceptance      | Formal sign-off on migration completion                | UMS               | Pending      |

## 8.4 Post-Migration/Support

A robust post-migration support model is essential to ensure operational continuity and user confidence. Enfrasys Solutions will provide a structured support framework, including hypercare in the immediate aftermath of migration and ongoing support as UMS transitions to steady-state operations. The escalation path will be clearly defined, ensuring that any issues are promptly addressed and resolved.

During the hypercare period, Enfrasys Solutions will monitor system performance, respond to incidents, and assist UMS with user queries and troubleshooting. UMS will be responsible for ongoing validation of data and reporting any issues encountered. Escalation procedures will be documented, with clear points of contact for both technical and functional support.

Support Model Overview:

- Hypercare support by Enfrasys Solutions for a defined period post-migration.
- Incident logging and tracking via agreed support channels.
- Defined escalation tiers: Level 1 (UMS IT), Level 2 (Enfrasys Solutions), Level 3 (Microsoft Premier Support, if required).
- Knowledge transfer sessions to UMS IT staff for ongoing management.

This approach ensures that Universiti Malaysia Sabah receives comprehensive support throughout the migration lifecycle, minimizing risk and maximizing the value of the new solution.
